question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any councils that are taking steps to make swift bricks mandatory on new buildings.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether he would consider introducing a policy to ensure that swift bricks are included in all new build social housing.
To ask the Assembly Commission whether it will consider (i) the use of swift bricks; and (ii) the installation of a swift tower, at Parliament Buildings.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on their Department's work with regard to relations with the European Union.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the installation of a cycle lane on the Templepatrick Road, Ballyclare, between the junctions of Jubilee Road and Mill Road.
answer_text: The proposed active travel improvement on the Templepatrick Road, Ballyclare, between the junctions of Jubilee Road and Mill Road aims to enhance safety and connectivity for pedestrians and cyclists along this busy corridor.Design of the scheme is well advanced and discussions with affected landowners are ongoing There are also considerations relating to nearby residential developments that must be fully addressed before the scheme can progress. It is hoped that construction could commence in the 2026/27 financial year, subject to the successful resolution of the above issues and budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on a new People and Place Strategic Framework.
answer_text: I continue to witness first-hand the excellent work delivered by our community sector partners through the Neighbourhood Renewal Programme. In the last financial year, I invested more than £19.5 million in this programme, which is making a tangible difference to people living in our most deprived communities. Through my continued investment, during 2024-25 more than 5,500 adults volunteered over 314,000 hours in their communities valued at £3.8 million, enabled support for over 2,500 local community organisations, over 6,000 employees and volunteers trained in community development, and assisted community groups in securing over £40 million in additional external funding. While the achievements of the Neighbourhood Renewal Programme are significant, I am keen to ensure it continues to deliver even greater benefits for disadvantaged communities in the future. The review of the Neighbourhood Renewal People and Place Strategy has now been completed, and I am considering how to take forward any new Frameworks. Subject to the agreement of my Executive colleagues, a public consultation on the Strategic Framework will be launched in due course. It is not anticipated that any changes to the existing Neighbourhood Renewal Programme will take place during the 2026/27 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the development of an aviation strategy.
answer_text: Our geographical position as an island on the edge of Europe means we rely heavily on air connectivity to support trade, tourism and foreign direct investment.  It is my ambition to strengthen our domestic and international connectivity. For this reason, my predecessor and I have recognised the importance of developing a regional aviation policy that is tailored to local priorities and reflects the specific needs of our communities and economy. Over the past year, my department has been working closely with key aviation stakeholders – including our three airports, the Tourism Partnership Board, Invest NI, and other Departments – to define the scope and objectives of the proposed policy. The public consultation on the aviation policy was launched on 27 March and closes on 22 May.  It seeks views on a set of policy positions aimed at unlocking opportunities to enhance connectivity.  Responses will be analysed following consultation closure to provide a robust evidence base to inform future policy interventions aligned with my economic priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Arc21 incinerator is intended to receive waste from all local councils.
answer_text: My Department is not party to the contractual arrangements put in place by arc21 or individual local councils in respect of these matters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans his Department has for meeting the recycling targets in his Rethinking Our Resources: Northern Ireland Resources and Waste Management Strategy.
answer_text: Our recycling targets in the Waste Management Strategy are binding legislative targets set by the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 and the Waste and Contaminated Land Order 1997 (as amended).  They require 70% of waste to be recycled by 2030 and 65% of municipal waste to be recycled by 2035.			We need to take action if we are to meet these targets. The most recent published statistics on local authority collected municipal waste, show that our recycling rates have stagnated at around 50% in recent years.		While the Waste Management Strategy aims to set the strategic direction for waste management in Northern Ireland for the next 6 years, my department consulted on proposals in 2024, specifically designed to improve recycling rates for householders and businesses. The responses helped inform our draft Departmental Response which contains plans to drive down the volume of residual waste and improve both the quality and quantity of material being collected for recycling, to support local processing. 		I am seeking Executive approval to publish the Departmental Response, thereby providing councils and businesses with the direction and clarity I know they want in order to improve recycling rates and meet our targets.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has carried out on road safety on rural roads.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and with approximately 90% of our road network being classed as “B” or “C” roads, rural roads are a keen focus for analysis and improvement initiatives.My Department commissioned analysis of Killed and Serious Injury (KSI) collisions in the North between 2019 and 2023. This research showed that 70% of speeding KSI collisions occurred on rural roads.New, mainly young, drivers are over-represented in KSI collisions in NI. This is particularly evident on rural roads. Between 2020-2024, a car driver aged 17-23 was involved in 30% of rural road KSIs. The most frequently reported principal causation for KSI collisions caused by drivers aged 17-23 is excessive speed. The Graduated Driver Licensing scheme I am introducing in October this year is designed to improve road safety for new drivers, particularly young drivers aged 17-23, and reduce KSI collisions.  It is likely that any KSI reductions would be proportionally greater in rural areas where speed limits tend to be higher than in urban areas and the consequences of collisions at speed can be more severe.I am also taking forward a review on a number of speed related matters, including consideration of the lowering of speed limits on rural roads, which is now open for consultation. I would encourage you to contribute to this significant and important piece of work. Details can be found at Speed limit review launched by DfI | Department for Infrastructure.As reflected in my Department’s Road Safety Strategy 2030, we will continue to work with colleagues in the south/Irish Government to tackle the death toll across the island and to address any cross-border road engineering and speed concerns. Road safety is regularly discussed at the North South Ministerial Council - Transport Meetings, where opportunities for effective collaboration on proposed cross-border initiatives are considered. I am also aware that our road network, the majority of which is considered to be rural, is in need of investment. A public consultation has now concluded on my draft Road Maintenance Strategy, which seeks to improve the overall condition of the road network and enhance safety and quality, as a means of improving resilience. The Strategy proposes a targeted programme of intelligent maintenance investment, supported by a more sustainable maintenance regime.In addition, I have invested heavily through the Roads Recovery Fund, a significant proportion of which is focused on addressing the condition of rural roads and supporting local connectivity.However, I have been clear that these initiatives alone cannot address the consequences of many years of underinvestment. I will therefore continue to work with Executive colleagues to secure appropriate levels of funding to improve the quality of our roads and to support delivery of the priorities set out in the Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any assessment that has been made to ensure his Department’s current data definitions provide an accurate understanding of pressures across health and social care.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 41062/22-27, to detail any plans she has to reduce the cost of driving licence renewals to the same levels applied in Great Britain.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to reduce the fee for renewing a driving licence to the level applied in Britain.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on how the Reserve Claim repayments will affect the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the reason why the trees on the M2 central reservation are being cut down, between the Rathbeg and Dunsilly Roundabouts, Antrim; and (ii) whether her Department has any plans to replace these trees.
answer_text: The tree plantation between the M2 carriageways from Dunsilly Roundabout to the junction at Antrim Area Hospital suffered significant storm damage, most notably during Storm Eowyn in early 2025. Subsequent surveys found that almost 30% of the 1970s conifer planting had been blown down and was unsuitable for the site, with further damage caused by later storms. Clearance works were therefore carried out outside the bird nesting season, with replanting and natural regeneration using native species planned for mid-2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has (i) undertaken an assessment of her Department's current vesting scheme; and (ii) any plans to review the scheme.
answer_text: The Local Government Act (NI) 1972 outlines the procedure for the compulsory acquisition of land by all departments and district councils via a vesting order. It establishes the legal framework for serving notices, handling objections and compensation. Functions under the Local Government Act (NI) 1972 were transferred to the Department for Communities by the Departments (Transfer of Functions) Order (NI) 2016. As the 1972 Act forms the basis of compulsory acquisition by all departments and public bodies, the Department for Communities would have primary responsibility for any review of the procedures under that Act. Regarding any review of how compulsory purchase compensation is calculated, the Department of Finance’s Land & Property Services (LPS) provides independent advice to all government departments and other public bodies on the assessment of compensation following the exercise of compulsory purchase powers. Any review of how compulsory purchase compensation is calculated would therefore be a matter for the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any monitoring will take place of the number of schools taking part in activities linked to World Wildlife Day, including how this information will be used.
answer_text: World Wildlife Day offers an opportunity for schools to further explore themes in the Northern Ireland Curriculum, including topics such as biodiversity, ecosystems, climate change and the management of human impact on the natural world. It is a matter for individual schools to determine the programmes, activities and resources they use to support their delivery of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.Accordingly, decisions regarding the marking of days such as World Wildlife Day rest with individual schools.  In keeping with this approach, my Department does not promote specific activities, events, competitions, or learning materials for this purpose,  nor has it issued any associated guidance.My Department has not undertaken specific work with external organisations, including environmental non-governmental organisations, to develop World Wildlife Day resources. Schools may choose to draw on a wide range of materials from recognised local, national, or international sources should they wish to incorporate World Wildlife Day into their teaching and learning.As participation in World Wildlife Day is entirely at the discretion of individual schools, my Department does not collect information on the number of schools that choose to engage and has no plans to introduce any such monitoring arrangements in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department is working with (i) councils; (ii) housing providers; and (iii) the voluntary sector, to encourage community participation in World Wildlife Day events, or related environmental activities.
answer_text: My Department has no remit in relation to encouraging community participation in World Wildlife Day or related environmental activities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider using World Wildlife Day to promote driver awareness of wildlife on the roads.
answer_text: Although World Wildlife Day, which takes place annually on 3 March, has now passed, my Department continually keeps under review appropriate opportunities to reinforce road-safety messaging throughout the year, including encouraging drivers to be alert to all potential hazards, particularly in rural areas. My Department’s road safety campaigns are designed to improve road user behaviour and reduce the number of people killed or seriously injured on roads here.  Improvement in road user behaviour benefits all road users, which may also help protect wildlife too.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration that has been given to marking World Wildlife Day in schools, including any suggested activities, competitions, or learning resources issued by his Department.
answer_text: World Wildlife Day offers an opportunity for schools to further explore themes in the Northern Ireland Curriculum, including topics such as biodiversity, ecosystems, climate change and the management of human impact on the natural world. It is a matter for individual schools to determine the programmes, activities and resources they use to support their delivery of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.Accordingly, decisions regarding the marking of days such as World Wildlife Day rest with individual schools.  In keeping with this approach, my Department does not promote specific activities, events, competitions, or learning materials for this purpose,  nor has it issued any associated guidance.My Department has not undertaken specific work with external organisations, including environmental non-governmental organisations, to develop World Wildlife Day resources. Schools may choose to draw on a wide range of materials from recognised local, national, or international sources should they wish to incorporate World Wildlife Day into their teaching and learning.As participation in World Wildlife Day is entirely at the discretion of individual schools, my Department does not collect information on the number of schools that choose to engage and has no plans to introduce any such monitoring arrangements in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department is working with external partners, such as environmental non-governmental organisations, or education bodies, to develop World Wildlife Day materials for teachers.
answer_text: World Wildlife Day offers an opportunity for schools to further explore themes in the Northern Ireland Curriculum, including topics such as biodiversity, ecosystems, climate change and the management of human impact on the natural world. It is a matter for individual schools to determine the programmes, activities and resources they use to support their delivery of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.Accordingly, decisions regarding the marking of days such as World Wildlife Day rest with individual schools. Schools may choose to draw on a wide range of materials from recognised local, national, or international sources should they wish to incorporate World Wildlife Day into their teaching and learning.As participation in World Wildlife Day is entirely at the discretion of individual schools, my Department does not collect information on the number of schools that choose to engage and has no plans to introduce any such monitoring arrangements in this area.

Lowland is outside Areas of Natural Constraint.The table below shows the land types claimed by farm businesses on their 2025 Single Applications. department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the percentage of designated sites within Areas of Natural Constraint that are in favourable condition.
answer_text: Overall condition status is not assigned to a designated site, and it is not possible to determine the percentage of designated sites within Areas of Natural Constraint that are in favourable condition. However, the percentage of designated features in favourable condition within designated sites located in Areas of Natural Constraint is approximately 50%.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the percentage of land within designated Areas of Natural Constraint that is Northern Ireland Priority Habitat.
answer_text: Priority habitats in Northern Ireland are those that are considered to be at risk and require conservation efforts to protect and restore them.  The percentage of land within designated Areas of Natural Constraint that is Northern Ireland Priority Habitat is estimated at 44%.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the reason why there is a difference in cost between driving licence renewals in Northern Ireland and Great Britain.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) is responsible for the administration of driver licensing for those drivers who are resident in the north and the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) in Britain is responsible for the administration of driver licensing for drivers who are resident in England, Scotland and Wales. The fee for an ordinary driving licence renewal in the north is higher than in Britain. This difference in fees is largely attributable to the economies of scale associated with the volumes of licences issued.  Medical renewals and renewals for those aged 70 and over remain free of charge in both jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any assessment his Department has made of health inequalities specific to rural populations.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any engagement she has undertaken with local councils to encourage progress around outstanding Local Development Plans.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of the Local Development Plan programme in setting the long-term plan led framework for decision making.Throughout the plan making process, my officials have met regularly with all councils regarding their Local Development Plans, and they continue to encourage regular engagement, both collectively and individually.My officials have published guidance designed to aid councils and as part of the Planning Improvement Programme, they are in the process of updating this suite of Development Plan Practice Notes.Good progress has been made. To date, six councils have adopted their Plan Strategies (PS), and all six are now advancing with Local Policies Plans (LPPs).  Only two remaining councils have to publish their draft PS, and their timetables have indicated this will be within this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 30409/22-27.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any steps the Executive Office is taking to ensure cross-departmental collaboration, to prevent and end homelessness.
answer_text: We recognise the often complex and difficult circumstances that lead to people finding themselves homeless. Tackling the issue requires a cross-departmental, multi-agency approach. It is the responsibility of the Department for Communities to lead on this issue within the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of recent figures from the Simon Community showing that there are over 2,600 people homeless in South Antrim, to detail any actions his Department is taking to address this issue.
answer_text: Prevention of homelessness and the prioritisation of housing solutions for those most in need is a key objective within the Housing Supply Strategy. In addition to the £4.3m allocated to the Housing Executive for community prevention activities, this year I allocated an additional ringfenced £2.5 million for Strategic Homelessness Prevention which is currently supporting 35 organisations to deliver 42 projects in local areas, including South Antrim. I also allocated £3 million for the New Foundations Programme to support young people leaving care and prevent them entering homelessness. My Department will continue to work closely with partners across the Executive to explore opportunities for meaningful joint working and to ensure greater collaboration and engagement at a strategic level to prevent homelessness and improve outcomes for people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking, in collaboration with the Department for Communities, to address the health and mental health impacts of homelessness in South Antrim.
answer_text: My Department works in collaboration with the Department for Communities to meet the health needs of the homeless population across Northern Ireland, primarily through the implementation of the Inter Departmental Homeless Action Plan.  The Public Health Agency commission a specialist homeless nurse in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust to support people experiencing homelessness; this includes South Antrim.  Individuals requiring further assessment or treatment, including mental health support, are referred to their GP or directed into established Trust mental health services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any projected homelessness trends for the South Antrim constituency, over the next five years, including any internal modelling or risk assessments undertaken by his Department, and the Housing Executive.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“While the Housing Executive does not predict the level of homelessness, our focus, as per the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27, is to prioritise the prevention of homelessness by working with a range of statutory and voluntary partners to minimise the number of households who present as homeless in areas such as South Antrim.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department is working on a cross-departmental basis with other Ministers, to prevent and end homelessness.
answer_text: I regularly engage with Executive colleagues to stress the need for greater collaboration to prevent homelessness. Prevention and intervention to reduce housing stress and prioritise housing solutions for those most in need is a key objective within the Housing Supply Strategy I brought to the Executive. Along with the Minister for Health I have also launched the New Foundations project to help prevent young people leaving care entering homelessness.My Department also leads on the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan and will continue to work closely with partners across the Executive to explore opportunities for meaningful joint working and to ensure greater collaboration and engagement at a strategic level to prevent homelessness and improve outcomes for people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of new social homes commenced and completed in the South Antrim constituency, in each of the last three years; and (ii) whether he intends to increase targets, in light of the current homelessness figures from the Simon Community.
answer_text: The number of new social homes commenced and completed in the South Antrim constituency in the last three years are as follows:YearSocial Homes CommencedSocial Homes Completed2022/23108932023/244302024/253370The Housing Executive is committed to ensuring that housing need is targeted equitably throughout Northern Ireland and has developed Strategic Guidelines to distribute the new build social housing requirement against the available annual budget at a Council level over the three years of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP). I am doing everything I can to stretch my budget to deliver as many new social homes as possible. It is imperative that the Executive also commits to doing everything it can to ensure that sufficient budget is allocated to the SHDP as early as possible, to give us the best chance of achieving our Programme for Government target.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when the departmental response to the consultation on Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy will be published.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the recent flooding in Antrim town, whether she has considered bringing forward flood alleviation plans for the area.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline any specific health economic modelling his Department is using to assess whether financial decisions are delivering long-term value and better outcomes in the health system, as opposed to balancing budgets and deferring costs into future years.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will make swift bricks mandatory, in new houses.
answer_text: Biodiversity measures such as this are normally addressed as a planning matter and expanding the scope of Building Regulations to include measures aimed at conserving species would represent a significant shift in regulatory intent.I nonetheless appreciate the importance of maintaining and encouraging biodiversity and I can assure you that my Department will continue to consider implications for biodiversity as part of its current and future work. Our Building Regulations are typically reviewed in light of developments across the local administrations, which allows us to benefit from the research and lessons learned elsewhere, prior to any introduction here. Should appropriate nature friendly measures come forward elsewhere, these may also be considered in our future work plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures his Department will take to help arrest the population decline in house martins and swifts.
answer_text: The Common swift is listed by the Department as a Northern Ireland Priority Species, meaning that the species is a species of principal importance for conserving biodiversity in Northern Ireland. Conservation action for swifts by the Department has focused on protecting existing nest sites through advice to planning authorities on consultations related to development proposals. House martins also prefer to use existing nests remaining on buildings from the previous year, and the Department would encourage house owners to leave these nests in situ. The Department is a partner in the Northern Ireland Species Recovery Partnership, which is currently developing a concise Species Action Plan towards the recovery of swifts in Northern Ireland, due for publication in 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps he will take to ensure building regulations help promote biodiversity.
answer_text: My Department’s current work to improve Building Regulations is focused on crucial fire safety and energy related matters, while reform of our wider building safety regime is also being progressed by the Department for Communities.  Biodiversity measures are normally addressed as a planning matter and expanding the scope of Building Regulations would represent a significant shift in regulatory intent.I nonetheless appreciate the importance of maintaining and encouraging biodiversity and I can assure you that my Department will continue to consider implications for biodiversity as part of its current and future work. Our Building Regulations are typically reviewed in light of developments across the local administrations, which allows us to benefit from the research and lessons learned elsewhere, prior to any introduction here. Should appropriate nature friendly measures come forward elsewhere, these will also be considered in our future work plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any actions his Department is taking to increase recycling in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: It is clear from annual figures that the recycling rate has stagnated, with minimal change in recent years, indicating that further action is needed if we are to meet our legislative targets.  While our local councils and the general public are to be commended for their efforts in driving up recycling rates from a low base at the start of this century, demonstrating the effectiveness of previous interventions, more needs to be done.However, my ambition is not just to increase the rate of recycling.“Rethinking Our Resources” is a collection of workstreams developed by my department that will set out the necessary reforms needed to drive better resource and waste management practices that aim to contribute to Northern Ireland’s Net Zero ambition, improve the environment and build a better, more circular economy.At the centre of this work is The Resources and Waste Management Strategy, which I launched for consultation on 15th January. I would encourage everyone to respond to ensure their views are capturedThe new waste management strategy will set the policy direction at a strategic level for the future management of resources and waste, through a range of actions and targets over the next 6 years.The goal of the strategy is to futureproof resources and waste management in NI whilst helping to embed the Circular Economy and achieve our climate goals.  The other strand of this work (“Rethinking Our Resources”) is Waste and Recycling Reforms in Northern Ireland, which my department consulted on in 2024.  That work focused on measures to improve recycling and waste collections across both households and businesses. Responses helped inform our draft Departmental Response, which contains recommendations aimed at driving down the volume of residual waste and improving both the quality and quantity of material being collected for recycling, to support local reprocessing.  I have also heard from councils, first hand, that there is a clear desire for the Departmental Response to be published to provide clarity on future direction and help inform investment decisions.I am currently seeking approval from my Executive colleagues for publication of the Departmental Response.My department also continues to provide funding opportunities through the Household Waste Recycling Collaborative Change Programme (HWRCCP), with over £8.2m already provided to Councils in NI to improve recycling infrastructure and services.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has discussed the introduction of an infrastructure levy with the Minister of Finance.

To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the Supreme Court judgment in the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant), to outline any plans for a review of the Religious Education syllabus.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on how their Department is taking forward the findings of the UK Covid-19 Inquiry, including the recommendations set out in the Module 2C report on core decision-making and political governance.
answer_text: Publication of the Covid-19 Inquiry’s reports and recommendations represents an important milestone in learning lessons from the pandemic. The recommendations aim to strengthen governance, transparency, inclusivity, and emergency response structures.We acknowledge there are lessons to be learned. The Cross-Departmental Steering Group established to take forward recommendations arising from the Inquiry reports continues to meet and is considering the Module 2 and 2C findings, particularly those directly relevant to here.  We are already making changes including the appointment of a Chief Scientific and Technical Adviser in June 2024 and improvements to emergency preparedness.Our approach will include:Careful consideration of all recommendations addressed to Northern Ireland and those with UK-wide implications.Continued engagement with counterparts across the four nations to ensure a coordinated response and avoid unnecessary constitutional or operational issues.Continued engagement with the NI Covid Bereaved Families for Justice and other stakeholders to ensure their experiences inform this work. Reporting progress to the Executive and relevant Assembly Committees in due course.We remain committed to implementing lessons learned to improve decision-making and governance for future emergencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any payment schemes available for farm owners whose farms are under three hectares.
answer_text: Subject to meeting scheme eligibility requirements, farm businesses under three hectares, may be able to avail of support through a number of current and future Schemes within the Sustainable Agriculture Programme.  These include the Farming with Nature Package, the pilot Protein Crop Scheme, the planned Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme, the Farming for the Generations Scheme, and the Horticulture Pilot Scheme. . These schemes are being introduced, subject to legislative and business case approval, in accordance with the published SAP timeline that can be viewed on the Department’s website: www.daera-ni.gov.uk/topics/sustainable-agriculture-programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment that has been made of the contribution of Christmas lighting schemes to town centre footfall, in the context of sustainable development.
answer_text: No formal assessments have been made of the specific impact of the contribution of Christmas lighting schemes to town centre footfall, in the context of sustainable development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures in place to ensure responsible disposal or recycling of Christmas packaging, decorations, and trees.
answer_text: Seasonal variations relating to both household and Non-Household Municipal (NHM) waste during the Christmas period present a challenge, due to the volume of waste collected at kerbside and through Household Recycling Centres which are operated and managed by local councils. My officials engage with local councils regularly to provide support and guidance on matters relating to resources and waste collection. My department also provides funding to the Waste & Resources Action Programme (WRAP) to provide essential local authority support, including specialist technical guidance. The programme includes collaborative efforts with local councils on key national and regional behaviour change campaigns through the National Communications Advisory Panel (NCAP), such as Recycle Week and Food Waste Action Week, designed to enhance the quality and quantity of recycling.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department is facilitating the use of renewable energy sources for town centre Christmas lighting.
answer_text: I have not facilitated the use of renewable energy sources for town centre Christmas lighting as this is not a matter which falls within my Department’s remit.I would advise the Member to contact each local authority to establish if they are facilitating the use of renewable energy sources within their area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has issued guidance to councils on reducing the environmental impact of Christmas (i) decorations; and (ii) lighting schemes.
answer_text: The Department for Communities will not be issuing guidance to councils on reducing the environmental impact of Christmas decorations or lighting schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures in place to monitor and prevent illegal dumping or fly-tipping following the festive period.
answer_text: I recognise the significant negative impact that illegal dumping and fly-tipping can have on the natural environment and the problems it can cause. My Department works closely with local councils to support effective waste management across Northern Ireland. Responsibilities for illegal waste dumping and fly-tipping are split between my Department and Councils. NIEA’s Environmental Crime Unit (ECU) focuses its resources on those cases which present the most significant risk to the environment and public health and utilises powers contained within the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 to pursue prosecutions. Such prosecutions are often coupled with confiscation proceedings under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 aimed at depriving those convicted of the proceeds generated from their crimes whilst asserting the ‘Polluter Pays Principle’. On 2 January 2023 my Department commenced provisions of the Waste and Contaminated Land (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 affording Northern Ireland District Councils similar enforcement powers to those given to NIEA under the 1997 Act. ECU continues to work in close partnership with local councils to address fly tipping of waste across NI through a working protocol which has been agreed with the majority of councils.	In response to your concerns regarding illegal dumping and fly-tipping after the festive period, my Department will continue to process reports and investigate allegations of waste-related offences throughout the entire year, including during and following the festive season. The procedures available for reporting waste crime remain unchanged for members of the public and other relevant organisations or agencies, both before and after the festive period.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to adopt roadway sections at Riverside, Antrim, which have not already been formally adopted.
answer_text: My Department has no plans to adopt further sections of road at Riverside, Antrim, beyond the stretch which is already part of the publicly adopted network.There are processes whereby my Department can adopt a private road into the public road network; however, the road should be brought to an adoptable standard prior to the adoption, with costs borne by those wishing to have the road adopted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will consider incorporating repair and reuse education, including engagement with local repair cafés, into the curriculum.
answer_text: There are already many opportunities within the current Northern Ireland Curriculum to explore topics related to sustainability and reuse education through science and technology, engineering and mathematics subjects. It is a matter for individual schools to determine the resources or programmes they use to deliver this aspect of the curriculum. The Eco-Schools programme also provides an ideal way for fostering environmental awareness in the entire school through links to many curriculum subjects. The primary aim of the Eco-Schools programme is to educate and empower young people to make positive decisions and become change makers for an environmentally sustainable world.The Council for Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment’s Sustainable Futures resources also provides structured guidance and progression frameworks to support teaching sustainability from Foundation Stage to Key Stage 3.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether (i) deprived; or (ii) rural, communities have access to funding to establish repair cafés.
answer_text: My Department has supported the Northern Ireland Resources Network (NIRN) a representative body, working in partnership with DAERA, for reuse and repair in Northern Ireland.  Carrier Bag Levy (CBL) funding has enabled NIRN to support a growing network of community-led organisations promoting inclusive and sustainable change. NIRN have promoted the establishment of repair cafes through a range of communities within deprived, urban and rural communities which has included the first repair café to be held on Rathlin Island.   NIRN provide support and guidance to all communities on steps to access funding to establish repair cafes and encourage collaborative support across their network.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the opportunities that exist for pupils to engage in hands-on repair or upcycling projects, as part of science, technology, engineering and mathematics subjects.
answer_text: There are already many opportunities within the current Northern Ireland Curriculum to explore topics related to sustainability and reuse education through science and technology, engineering and mathematics subjects. It is a matter for individual schools to determine the resources or programmes they use to deliver this aspect of the curriculum. The Eco-Schools programme also provides an ideal way for fostering environmental awareness in the entire school through links to many curriculum subjects. The primary aim of the Eco-Schools programme is to educate and empower young people to make positive decisions and become change makers for an environmentally sustainable world.The Council for Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment’s Sustainable Futures resources also provides structured guidance and progression frameworks to support teaching sustainability from Foundation Stage to Key Stage 3.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has considered repair cafés as a tool for community regeneration and skills development.
answer_text: My Department supports the Northern Ireland Resources Network (NIRN) in promoting skills development and community-led repair and reuse initiatives. NIRN helps individuals and communities develop practical skills, such as repairing household items, using tools, and upcycling.  These skills can also open pathways to employment or volunteering which help to stimulate local economies and contribute to community regeneration.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps his Department is taking to support local community organisations in developing repair cafés.
answer_text: In accordance with the waste hierarchy and in promotion of a circular economy, my Department aims to prevent waste generation and promote reuse and repair to keep products in circulation for as long as possible. To support this goal, I have provided funding to Northern Ireland Resources Network (NIRN) through the Carrier Bag Levy fund.  This has enabled NIRN to actively support and promote repair cafés as part of its broader mission to encourage reuse, repair, and waste prevention across community associations, social enterprises, charities, local authorities and the public.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s implementation of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence’s guidance on hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in type 1 diabetes.
answer_text: Following the endorsement of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications.   Work has been completed in terms of overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support. Funding is required for devices in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  I have recently supported the establishment of a clinical working group to work with relevant stakeholders across Health and Social Care to develop an implementation plan which outlines an appropriate timescale for rollout. The implementation plan is currently being drafted and will support equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology, which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support available for teachers who wish to become Forest School leaders.
answer_text: Unfortunately, due to the significant financial constraints facing my Department in this financial year, we do not currently provide funding in respect of Forest Schools. However, I can confirm that Outdoor play is part of the Northern Ireland Curriculum at both the Foundation Stage and Key Stage 1, specifically within the Areas of Learning "The World Around Us" and "Physical Development and Movement". My Department is committed to helping children in Northern Ireland fully realise the benefits that Forest Schools can bring. My officials and I engage with Forest Schools and outdoor learning initiatives at every opportunity to celebrate, showcase and support the work of Forest Schools in Northern Ireland. In June this year I attended the opening ceremony for Kilmaine Primary School and Nursery Unit’s newly redeveloped Forest School site and my officials have met with representatives of the Northern Ireland Forest School Association (NIFSA) to discuss the benefits of their programmes.As the Department of Education does not currently provide funding in respect of Forest Schools, we do not monitor the number of educational settings engaging with such programmes. However, NIFSA reports having engaged with over 500 educational settings across Northern Ireland.Teachers and schools interested in Forest School programmes can access support through various channels. The Education Authority (EA) has produced professional learning videos on outdoor learning, available via its Supporting Learning website. Additionally, schools may use their own resources to implement a Forest School programme.Although my Department has not published any assessments of the positive outcomes often associated with Forest Schools, the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment has published guidance for working with children in pre-school and at Foundation Stage which recognises that being physically active, spending time outdoors and connecting with nature can affect emotions and allow for relaxation, calmness and a heightened sense of wellbeing. In addition to this, educational settings with Forest School provisions have reported increases in pupil confidence, language development, improved pupil behaviour, increased attention and listening, opportunities to develop problem-solving and decision-making skills and many other improved aspects of child development and learning. I was pleased to announce my Curriculum-led Capital Investment Programme in December 2024, with a specific focus on Physical Education. Following this, the Outdoor Learning Project launched in early 2025, which provided funding for outdoor play equipment to all nursery, primary and special schools. A further call was issued to all primary schools, inviting applications for the provision of outdoor covered sports space.  Currently there are no plans to include Forest Schools as part of the overall Programme.Through these programmes, I am striving to provide enhanced outdoor facilities for schools

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Rivers Agency has undertaken an assessment of flood risk to residential properties at Chestnut Grange, Glenavy.
answer_text: My Department’s partner consultant, Amey, were commissioned in September 2021 to undertake a feasibility study into flooding within the Glenavy catchment. The feasibility study which was completed in October 2023 assessed the flood risk within the entire Glenavy catchment, identifying areas at risk and potential remedial options to alleviate the flooding. The study recommended installing a new roughing screen in the Glenavy River, providing enhanced protection to four properties within Chestnut Glen. These flood alleviation works are now complete and there are no other outstanding actions from the report. The feasibility study also identified that all the properties in Chestnut Grange had a high level of existing flood protection, in excess of the 100 year target flood event (plus climate change allowance) standard. Therefore, there were no remedial measures recommended for Chestnut Grange.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the village of Glenavy will be included in areas being considered for flood alleviation plans.
answer_text: My Department’s partner consultant, Amey, were commissioned in September 2021 to undertake a feasibility study into flooding within the Glenavy catchment. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any educational value green roofs could bring to schools, in terms of outdoor learning.
answer_text: Green roofs, generally, have not been designed as accessible spaces for students, however there is scope for the design of green roofs to offer the following benefits:Teaching students about sustainable infrastructure, biodiversity and environmental stewardship.Understanding of the Science, Technology, Engineering and Maths (STEM) of living roofs.Opportunity to learn about ecology, biology and environmental science in a live environment.It is a matter for schools to decide how they incorporate outdoor learning into their teaching.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has undertaken a cost-benefit analysis of installing green roofs on school estates.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken a cost-benefit analysis of installing green roofs on school estates.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how green roof projects could be linked to the curriculum, in science and geography.
answer_text: There are already many opportunities within the curriculum to explore topics related to green growth and sustainability through science and geography. These subjects cover topics such as ecosystems, climate change, and sustainable development. The Council for Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment’s Sustainable Futures resources also provide structured guidance and progression frameworks to support teaching sustainability from Foundation Stage to Key Stage 3.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will pilot green roof projects in schools, to improve biodiversity and reduce energy use.
answer_text: My Department has no specific plans to pilot green roof projects in schools. It is committed to ensuring all new build schools improve biodiversity and are as energy efficient as possible. During the design process various options are considered on a case by case basis and the most suitable solutions are identified, balancing the schools day to day running and maintenance requirements with energy and biodiverse targets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether green roof projects are being considered for (i) hospitals; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trust estates.
answer_text: Green roof installations are considered for all new construction projects within the health estate as part of the design process for the development of sustainable healthcare buildings. Several healthcare buildings currently have green roofs, and a green roof is forming a portion of the roof element at the new Lisnaskea Health Centre currently under construction.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her Department’s plans for coronial investigations into deaths abroad.
answer_text: AQO 2518 22-27 - ANSWER Last month I published proposals for legislation which would allow coroners to investigate deaths of Northern Ireland residents which have occurred abroad. This would bring Northern Ireland into line with our neighbouring jurisdictions.  I have proposed legislation that would be family-led. Families would be able, but not required, to report a death abroad to the Coroners Service, where it is unexpected, unexplained, or in suspicious circumstances, and where they are concerned that overseas authorities have not established the cause and circumstance of the death or have doubt about the accuracy of any findings.   To ensure clarity and a consistent approach, I have also proposed that it would be for the Attorney General for Northern Ireland to decide when an inquest should be held, based on clear statutory criteria.   Amendment of primary legislation is required to implement these changes. Owing to other legislative priorities and a shortened mandate, this would have to be progressed in the next Assembly mandate, subject to the agreement of an incoming Minister and Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department’s plans to provide active travel routes in the South Antrim constituency.
answer_text: Future active travel routes within our towns and cities will be prioritised primarily in line with the emerging Active Travel Delivery Plan. Public consultation on the draft Plan closed on 28 February 2025 and my officials are now considering the responses received with a view to presenting a final plan to me for consideration shortly.  I hope to be in a position to publish the final plan by the end of the year.The plan will include network maps for our larger towns and cities, including Antrim, Ballyclare, Crumlin, Newtownabbey and Randalstown in the South Antrim constituency.When complete, the Active Travel Delivery Plan will complement the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan and the Strategic Plan for Greenways to provide a firm basis for prioritising delivery of improved active travel infrastructure for the next ten years and beyond.In addition to this, schemes to provide shared footway and cycleways between Crumlin and Glenavy, along Stiles Way from Avondale to Junction One, and Steeple Road from the train station to Steeple Park, Antrim are currently in design.  A scheme to improve pedestrian and cycling facilities along a narrow section of Hydepark Road, Mallusk is also being progressed. Footways at Staffordstown Road, Creggan and Templepatrick Road, Ballyclare are also at design stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework aligns with his Department's initiatives on improving public health and nutrition.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have worked with DAERA colleagues on the Food Strategy Framework from the outset and have been members of the Food Programme Board since July 2021. We and other Board members have worked closely to align outcomes, themes, and actions across Departments to link with other strategies and to build synergies while avoiding duplication. We welcomed the publication of Food Strategy Framework Action Plan 2025-2027 in May 2025, having worked in collaboration across Departments to include actions and outcomes which will deliver real change and improvements to public health and nutrition, while tackling overweight and obesity across the life course. These actions directly align with the overarching public health priorities in my Department’s forthcoming ‘Healthy Futures’ obesity strategic framework.We are committed to delivering outcomes within the Food Strategy Framework which contribute to the Framework’s ‘Mission One: To use food to improve lifelong health and wellbeing’. Actions included in the Food Strategy Framework Action Plan 2025-2027 under this mission which link to My Department’s public health and nutrition goals include:Addressing food inequalities, redistributing surplus food, and working across Departments to develop the ‘Right to Food’ guiding principle, as part of a suite of actions to tackle and alleviate food poverty.Promoting healthy, nutritious eating in early years, childcare and nursery settings, implementing all aspects of the existing Food in Schools Programme, as well as taking forward actions to increase the uptake of free school meals, while also increasing the numbers of pupils taking a school meal, either free or paid.Working with partners to implement nutritional standards in Health and Social Care, public sector and local council settings while evaluating implementation of nutritional standards for vending in public sector and local council settings. The Food Standards Agency (FSA) is also a named delivery partner in the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework. The FSA’s work under the Framework is focused on helping to support food businesses to make the food environment healthier through reducing calories, saturated fat, sugar, and salt in the food they produce, sell, or serve, reducing portion size and providing nutritional information. These actions are closely aligned to the work that the FSA undertakes with my Department under the extant and forthcoming obesity strategic frameworks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on discussions with the UK Treasury on the Local Growth Fund.
To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has collaborated with local universities in promoting cancer prevention and early detection amongst students.
To ask the Minister for Communities how the Food Action Plan aims to address (i) social inclusion; and (ii) access to affordable, nutritious food for all communities.
answer_text: The Department for Agriculture Environment and Rural Affairs are the lead Department for the Executive’s Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework Action Plan.My Department is lead for one action within it under the Addressing Food Inequalities theme which commits to utilising the community-based interventions we support to promote healthy eating messaging, and awareness of how to store, prepare, cook food and minimise waste.  To this end, my officials will be working with colleagues across Departments to identify opportunities to utilise our networks to promote and support this agenda.  My Department will also collaborate with other action leads, where possible, to address social inclusion and access to food – particularly where there are opportunities to reduce poverty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework Action Plan 2025-27 aims to stimulate economic growth within the food and drink sector in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My department is working with DAERA to deliver several actions under the Food Strategy Framework Action Plan 2025–27.   This work includes the areas of leadership skills and collaboration within the sector.  In addition, through the Agri-Food Manufacturing Sectoral Partnership, apprenticeship programmes are being shaped around employer needs, ensuring relevant skills and knowledge are embedded in the curriculum. Open to individuals aged 16 and over who meet eligibility criteria, current apprenticeship provision spans Levels 2 to 6, supporting career progression and enhancing productivity across the sector.My department, through Invest NI, also supports food and drink start-up, early stage, locally focused, and businesses seeking to export through a range of supports, in addition to North/South trade assistance from InterTrade Ireland.  This activity is underpinned by my department’s £46m Agri-Food Investment Initiative, which is supporting transformative capital investments across the processing sector – in close alignment with the aims of the Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there will be specific funding opportunities available for enterprises that align their practices with the objectives of the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework Action Plan 2025-27.
answer_text: My department, through Invest NI, offers a range of financial and non-financial support to Food and Drink businesses, not least through the Agri Food Investment Initiative – a £46m initiative to improve the economic performance, productivity and competitiveness of the sector through transformative capital investments. Alignment with the Food Strategy Framework Action Plan is not a requirement for assistance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any initiatives that will be introduced in schools to promote the objectives of the Food Action Plan, particularly in relation to (i) healthy eating; and (ii) environmental sustainability.
answer_text: As an action within the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework Action Plan and under the joint Department of Education and Department of Health Food in Schools Policy, a food theme week for primary schools has been developed in partnership by the Education Authority (EA) and the Regional Food in Schools Coordinator. ‘Eat Smart with the Lunch Bunch’ will run from 29 September 2025 to 3 October 2025 and aims to promote key, evidence-based healthy eating messages, inspire children to explore new foods and make positive choices at lunchtime. Each weekday has been structured around a principal food group from the Eatwell Guide, culminating in a food sustainability focus. EA Catering teams will serve a themed menu and daily taster samples in dining spaces vibrantly decorated to encourage pupil engagement and participation. To foster a whole-school approach, a suite of daily activity worksheets has been developed for classroom integration to help reinforce and amplify key messages and learning. Additionally, schools will receive digital recipe books for sharing with parents to inspire continuation of healthy choices in the home environment. Food sustainability will be a key aspect of this initiative with support materials exploring “Where does my food come from?” and one of the days within the theme week will focus on food wastage and how leftover food can used in different ways.Over 500 primary schools are due to participate in this initiative. A subsequent  evaluation with key stakeholders will inform an assessment of the impact and shape future planning.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to ensure that information about transport options for medical care is readily accessible in rural communities.
answer_text: My Department has no remit to provide transport specifically for the purpose of medical care. My Department provides funding to Translink to provide public transport services and facilities.  This means creating a consistent customer experience for everyone, ensuring accessible public transport for people of all ages and abilities.Both the Translink website and App can provide transport options from any location, address or stop, urban and rural, where transport links exist.  The website and app allow for address-to-address planning, park & ride planner and a bike it option.  All major hospitals have public transport stop listings.My Department also provides grant funding to community transport organisations to support the delivery of the rural Dial-a-Lift (DAL) Scheme across the North for those rural residents who find it difficult or impossible to access mainstream transport services.  DAL provides access to local services for a wide range of transport purposes which may include transport to local health facilities, e.g. doctor’s surgery, dentist, pharmacy etc.  DAL cannot be used for transport for hospital inpatient appointments.  DAL services are membership criteria based and are therefore not open to all members of the public.  Information on the DAL Scheme is available on the Departmental website and on individual rural community transport websites.  The respective community transport provider will also advise their members directly on availability of services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to provide vegan meals to users of (i) adult day centres; (ii) learning disability centres; and (iii) children’s day centres, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts are responsible for the provision of Health and Social Care Services. Health and Social Care Trusts provided the following response as noted below:South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT):Adult Day Centres and Learning Disability Day Centres:The vast majority of adults attending Adult and Learning Disability Day Centres are served cook freeze meals. These meals are procured from a contracted catering supplier with an extensive range of vegan options available on request.  The Trust will review our method of promoting vegan options in these centres to promote availability.  Children’s ServicesChildren’s Services do not operate Day Centres for Children, therefore the AQW does not apply to this Directorate.Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT):Adult Day Centres and Learning Disability Day Centres:As part of the pre-admission assessment to a Day Care placement, a Day Care representative will meet with the Service User and their Carer and one of the areas discussed is dietary needs and requirements. Food preferences including dietary and specialist diet requirements will be discussed and recorded in the Service Users Care Plan and staff in the Day Centre will be informed.  The centre will then order and serve meals in keeping with the Service User’s preferences, dietary or specialist requirements.Children’s ServicesChildren’s Services do not operate Day Centres for Children, therefore the AQW does not apply to this Directorate.Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT):Every effort is made to provide Service Users with specific meal choices.  The Western Trust has had no requests for vegan meals. However, in respect of any new Service User with any special dietary requirements, commencing attendance at a Day Centre (either adult, learning disability or children), the Centre Manager would contact support services and they would provide a number of options to meet their assessed needs.Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT):The Northern Trust caters for those who require special dietary requirements, including individuals who request vegan meals. Trust Catering Services provide vegan meal options that are prepared in accordance with dietary guidelines to ensure patient safety and nutritional adequacy. All special diets are coordinated in collaboration with Trust dietetic and catering teams to meet needs and preferences wherever possible.In the NHSCT we currently have 13 Adult Centres, 10 Day Centres, 8 Children facilities including residential homes and supported living units.Catering departments in these facilities offer a range of vegetarian options for the Services Users and will provide a variety of vegan options as requested. Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT)The Belfast Trust currently provide vegan meals to users of (i) adult day centres; (ii) learning disability centres; and (iii) children’s day centres. A 3 weekly Vegan menu shows options available each day.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered providing cancer awareness training to community groups and agri-businesses in rural areas.
answer_text: It is particularly important that we reach out to communities and those living and working in rural areas, to ensure that they have access to health awareness, prevention and early intervention initiatives.The Farming Community Network & Rural Community Cancer Project (‘Nip in the Bud’) project brings together The Farming Community Network (FCN) and Macmillan Cancer Support to raise awareness of the importance of early detection and prevention of cancer among the rural and agriculture community.The Public Health Agency (PHA) are also working in partnership with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) to develop and co-fund the Farm Families Health Checks Programme (FFHC). The Programme has been successfully delivered by the Northern Trust since 2012, as a means of improving the health and social wellbeing of rural and farming families within Northern Ireland.In addition, the PHA works closely with the Department of Health, local Health and Social Care Trusts, and other partners to support a range of programmes and strategies that incorporate cancer awareness training to community groups and agri-businesses in rural areas, including:The Skin Cancer Prevention Strategy and Action Plan 2011-2021. Digital and targeted media campaigns and resources for the wider population and high-risk groups e.g. ‘Care in the Sun’ and ‘Be UV Aware’ campaign resources for outdoor workers. Dissemination of sun safety resources at rural events, sports organisations and summer schemes e.g. Royal Ulster Agricultural Society Shows and The Open.Provision of training and e-learning modules for health professionals to recognise early signs of cancer, available to GP’s, HSENI, pharmacists, dermatologists, environmental health officers, teachers, HSC Trusts, clinical students and beauty therapists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered providing mobile cancer screening units for hard-to-reach rural areas.
answer_text: There are currently three commissioned population cancer screening programmes in Northern Ireland: the breast, bowel and cervical screening programmes.For breast screening, the Breast Screening Unit in Linenhall Street, in Belfast, provides mammography screening for women in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust area. In the other HSC Trust areas, most screening mammograms are already carried out on mobile breast screening trailers which rotate between a variety of locations across Northern Ireland. As the Bowel cancer screening programmes involves the use of a home test kit which is posted directly to participant’s homes, a mobile unit is not appropriate or required.The NI Cervical Screening Programme is provided at GP practices located across Northern Ireland in local settings, which are accessible. Mobile screening units are not currently used or being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any funding or resources his Department is providing for rural community groups to host cancer awareness events.
answer_text: The Department provides infrastructure support funding to several rural community groups.  We do not provide funding for cancer awareness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail what steps are being taken to ensure public procurement supports environmentally sustainable outcomes.
answer_text: ANSWERA number of actions and policies ensure public procurement supports environmentally sustainable outcomes.See links below:Public Procurement Policy Statement,Procurement Policy Note (PPN) 01/21 - Social Value in Procurement Social Value Strategy Construction Procurement Toolkit In addition, the Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD), Construction Division has been advising clients to consider requiring buildings to achieve certification / standards such as:Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Method (BREEAM) (New Buildings: BREEAM Excellent rating, but with an Outstanding rating on energy conservation credits)Passivhaus certificationNet Zero Carbon Building StandardsMy department will continue to support sustainable strategies and targets as they develop and include these in procurement strategies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the supply of oxygen in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: A procured Oxygen Service is currently provided by BOC Homecare Service for patients who require home treatment with oxygen in Northern Ireland. HSC Trusts and GPs access this same service. 				To ensure there are adequate supplies and to ensure the correct patients get oxygen therapy in line with their needs there is an agreed prioritisation process with Trust respiratory leads.				SPPG, together with colleagues from each of the 5 Trust areas, meet with BOC on a bi-monthly basis to discuss the contract performance in relation to the KPIs specified within the procured oxygen contract.  				SPPG have contacted BOC in response to your query and I can confirm there are no current shortages in oxygen or delays in delivery of home oxygen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department has a small fund available for the regeneration of scheduled monuments, if the owner wishes to apply directly.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the incentives available to encourage private developers to install green roofs on commercial and residential buildings.
answer_text: My department does not have an incentive scheme to encourage private developers to install green roofs on commercial and residential buildings. However, green roofs are a form of Sustainable Drainage Systems (SuDS), and the planning system and regional strategic planning policy has at its heart the objective of furthering sustainable development. It encourages and supports efforts to mitigate and adapt to climate change. While green roofs are not specifically referenced in the Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS), it does promote the use of SuDS to reduce flood risk and improve water quality. In addition, the new Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill, which is currently going through the Assembly, will allow my department to make regulations in relation to the design, approval, operation, and maintenance of SuDS. In tandem with this, my department is carrying out a public consultation to seek views on the development and implementation of new policies and regulatory arrangements to ensure nature-based SuDS, such as grassed swales, raingardens and detention ponds are provided in new housing developments in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what steps her Department is taking to incorporate green roofs in new public infrastructure projects, as part of its sustainability agenda.
answer_text: Green roofs are a form of Sustainable Drainage Systems (SuDS), and the planning system and regional strategic planning policy has at its heart the objective of furthering sustainable development. It encourages and supports efforts to mitigate and adapt to climate change. While green roofs are not specifically referenced in the Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS), it does promote the use of SuDS to reduce flood risk and improve water quality’		As part of a programme of phased improvements, Translink is planning upgrades along a number of Metro routes including the installation of Living Roofs on some bus shelters to promote biodiversity and create a more sustainable environment. Work on this project commenced in July 2024 with the installation of the first Living Roof bus shelter on Belfast’s Shankill Road. 		My officials are currently considering a business case from Translink seeking to replace and refurbish existing bus shelters over a ten-year period. This will also include installing several new bus shelters across the public transport network, some of which may be adapted to include green roofs. This work will be progressed subject to the availability of funding.The Driver & Vehicle Agency, as part of its sustainability agenda, will continue to consider a range of sustainability measures including green roofs in the design and development of its new test centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the implementation of the recommendations in the Gillen Review Report into the law and procedures in serious sexual offences in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 13 - AQO 2368/22-27		  ANSWER I am pleased to report that 174, or 69%, of the original 253 Gillen Review recommendations are now complete with a further 25 (10%) partially complete. However, I believe that focus on these numbers alone, does not convey the true scale of change in terms of outcomes for victims.I have prioritised recommendations that deliver meaningful improvements for victims of sexual crime; including:access to free legal advice for all victims of serious sexual offences, through the SOLA and Children’s SOLA schemes;access to Remote Evidence Centres, where victims can give their best evidence from a secure and comfortable facility away from the court building – I am encouraged that the conviction rate for REC cases currently stands at over 90%;measures to reduce delay, including committal reform, which means that victims are no longer required to give oral evidence at the committal hearing;delivery of the Under 13 protocol which is significantly reducing the time taken to deal with cases involving young victims;launch of PSNI led Power to Change campaign in January, which tackles the behaviours and attitudes that fuel sexual offending and gender-based violence, including violence against women and girls.There have been significant improvements to Disclosure and ABE procedures;
