better training to enable front-line staff to adopt a trauma-informed approach with victims and new legislation to exclude the public from court in serious sexual offence trials.My department is also progressing work on new legislation to clarify the right of complainants in serious sexual offence cases to  third party representation during pre-trial applications and to enhance the protection of complainants’ personal information in the third-party material disclosure process.Wider work is also well advanced and in coming months I plan to:Publish research findings in relation to the extent and reasons for attrition in serious sexual offences and the use of previous sexual history in trials;launch a social media campaign to challenge rape myths;publish guidance for the media in relation to the reporting of sexual crime; and,consider options for a Barnahus-informed facility in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any action her Department is taking to stop raw sewage being pumped into Lough Neagh.
answer_text: I am fully committed to continuing the work being taken forward through the DAERA Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan published last year and endorsed by the Executive.The member will be aware that the Action Plan contains 37 recommendations, one of which falls under my Department’s responsibility. This is in respect of the delivery on the upgrade of Lough Neagh wastewater treatment schemes.The investment need for the delivery on NI Water wastewater assets around Lough Neagh has already been identified, as part of the price control process, and is subject to budget availability.NI Water has already invested £60m in Lough Neagh since 2021, and is committed to investing £36m in the remaining 3 years of the current Price Control, PC21, subject to budget availability.I remain committed to working with Executive colleagues to secure funding for NI Water.The crisis at Lough Neagh is something we all must work together to tackle and my Department and NI Water remain committed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has considered signalling or dualling upgrades to the Bleach Green line, to manage extra demand in light of the Knockmore line feasibility study.
answer_text: The feasibility studies arising from the All -Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR) have taken cognisance of the wider rail network, including infrastructure constraints and opportunities for lines to be reopened in conjunction with enhancements elsewhere on the network. It is anticipated that re-opening the Antrim to Lisburn line will require installation of passing loops on this single line section. Further dualling or an additional passing loop may also be required on the Antrim to Bleach Green line, dependent on the future operating model.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps her Department has taken to assess the feasibility of a rail halt at the Ballymartin park and ride facility at Templepatrick.
answer_text: The All-Island Strategic Rail Review considered the potential to improve the inter-urban and regional/rural rail system for passengers and communities in Ireland, North and South. The Rail Review recommended exploring the case for new stations in the Belfast and Derry city regions under the Sustainable Cities goal, with specific reference to Templepatrick / Ballymartin. This is a medium-term intervention in the Rail Review for completion between 2030 and 2040, subject to securing the necessary funding and approvals.Translink has identified Ballymartin Railway halt within its overall capital plan for network development. The proposed station would be integrated with the existing Bus Park & Ride. The project will be considered as part of the overall feasibility study on the potential for enhancing capacity and journey times on the existing Belfast – Derry rail corridor as part of the Derry Enhancement programme. While I am keen to deliver what I can within the remainder of my mandate, I will need to consider which projects to prioritise as funding opportunities become available. This is likely to require some difficult decisions and bearing down on costs will be essential to protect essential public service delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has been made aware of any rail traffic issues on the Bleach Green line, between Antrim and Belfast.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:There was some rail disruption during the month of June on the section of railway line between Antrim and Belfast.  This was mainly due to factors outside of Translink’s control, specifically linked to civil disturbances in Coleraine on 11th June and two occasions on 17th and 23rd June, when 3rd party road vehicles came in contact with railway bridges in the Antrim area causing temporary closures of the line whilst the bridges were subject to safety assessment.. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what strategies and plans relating to the environment are currently awaiting Executive approval.
answer_text: As set out in the ‘Conduct of Executive Business Protocol’, all aspects of Executive business are regarded as confidential.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact that the proposed Nutrient Action Programme will have on water quality at Lough Neagh.
answer_text: The proposals within the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) have been based on evidence provided through water quality monitoring data, scientific analysis and academic research.The evidence clearly demonstrates that there is an overall surplus of phosphorus in the Northern Ireland (NI) agricultural system and that excess phosphorus is a main cause of poor water quality in Lough Neagh and across NI.  Source apportionment analysis identifies that agriculture and wastewater treatment are the major sources of excess phosphorus.  Therefore, a range of measures, aimed at reducing phosphorus from agricultural sources, is proposed in the NAP consultation.Surplus phosphorus accumulates in the soils, and from there it is at risk of loss to water.  The NAP includes proposed measures to improve nutrient management and reduce the accumulation of phosphorus in the soil.  Reducing soil phosphorus levels towards the agronomic optimum (or lower) will deliver reductions in the levels of soluble reactive phosphorus in the associated stream and rivers.  Scientific research demonstrates that a change in the NI Agricultural phosphorus surplus can be seen the following year in our rivers through the measurement of the water quality metric of soluble reactive phosphorus. It is envisaged that if the proposed NAP measures were implemented, over a five year period, we should see a reduction in the total phosphorus levels in the inflowing rivers to Lough Neagh which would have a positive impact on water quality.  This is not the same as the level of the total phosphorus in the Lough water.  For the quality of the Lough itself, the accumulated phosphorus in the lake sediment also needs to be factored in.  Achieving sufficient reduction in the phosphorus coming from the lake sediment will take many years.  However, reducing the phosphorus inputs to the Lough is an essential step in improving water quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has received any submissions from political parties that detail alternative proposals to reduce the amount of nutrients in Lough Neagh.
answer_text: To date I have not received any submissions from political parties that detail alternative proposals to reduce the amount of nutrients in Lough Neagh.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any major environmental decisions he plans to bring to the Executive before the end of the 2022-27 Assembly Mandate.
answer_text: The DAERA Corporate Plan 2025-27 provides a framework for my Department’s work over the remainder of this Executive’s mandate.  It is built around eight key interconnected priorities that guide our work and includes ten key pledges that we will work tirelessly to deliver by the end of this Assembly mandate.To this end, I will bring forward for Executive approval several matters relating to the environment.  These are set out in the table below. A Nature Recovery StrategyThe Northern Ireland Peatland StrategyAn Ammonia StrategyA Clean Air StrategyA revised Nutrients Action Programme 2026 - 2029A Waste Management StrategyMeasures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in Northern IrelandProposals for strengthening environmental governance in Northern IrelandIntroduction of a Fisheries and Water Environment BillA Remediation Strategy for the Mobuoy siteA Wildfires Strategy (Wildfires in Northern Ireland: A Strategic Way Forward)The Third Northern Ireland Climate Change Adaptation Programme (NICCAP3)Regulations to set the fourth carbon budget (CB4) to cover the period 2038-2042Designation of Inner Belfast Lough Shellfish Waters Protected Area as a sensitive areaPotential developments to the UK Emissions Trading Scheme Climate Action Plan 2023-2027Green Growth Strategy
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the review of the Supporting pupils with Medication Needs guidance being undertaken by his Department, with specific reference to the management of food allergies.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has engaged with counterparts in Scotland and Wales to coordinate a joint devolved response to the UK Government’s Pathways to Work Green Paper.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his plans for the future use of the Muckamore Abbey Hospital site.
answer_text: As the owner of the Muckamore Abbey Hospital site, the Belfast Trust is responsible for planning and implementing the closure arrangements, although the hospital will not close until those patients who remain there are successfully resettled to agreed alternative community placements.   The Trust have confirmed to my Department that they do not expect to have any further use for the site when the hospital closes.   I have asked my officials to develop options for the future use of the Muckamore site with a view to maximising its potential value for the HSC system, and once completed this work will help inform my decision on the future use of the site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Education Information Solutions project to move all post-primary schools to a cashless system.
answer_text: A cashless system for the provision of catering services has been installed and are operational in approximately 45% of the post-primary schools where the Education Authority (EA) provides the catering. These schools have implemented a cashless system with the assistance and support of the EA and the external contractor. While no new systems have been installed with EA involvement for some time, a number of post-primary schools have independently introduced these systems. Schools that introduced a cashless system in partnership with the EA have benefited from the EA's contract with external service providers for annual maintenance. The contractor has provided technical support for system implementation and assistance with data protection requirements. Currently, there is no funding available to install new cashless catering systems or renew existing systems. The EA is actively seeking funding to expand access, and this initiative is being considered as part of the Education Information Solutions programme. The EA is able to provide advice and guidance to any school wishing to introduce a cashless catering system. The Schools Management System (SMS) will see a replacement to the current Schools Information Management Solution and is currently in pre-pilot phase. The pilot of the Bromcom School Management System, including online payments, is expected to launch in late summer 2025.Schools have been advised to contact the EA Commercial Procurement Team directly for ongoing support regarding this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the feasibility study to assess the flood risk from the Six Mile Water River.
answer_text: Antrim has been identified as a ‘Transitional Area of Potential Significant Flood Risk’ and as a result is included on the Rivers Directorate Capital Works Programme for further investigation.My Department has commissioned our Partner Consultant to undertake a Feasibility Study to assess the flood risk from the Sixmilewater River to properties in Antrim, Muckamore and Dunadry and to investigate if there are any economically viable schemes to help reduce the impacts of river flooding. It is currently anticipated that this study should be completed by summer 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Sands and Tommy’s Policy Unit May 2025 report entitled Saving Babies’ Lives 2025.
answer_text: The third annual Saving Babies’ Lives’ Progress report which includes National Maternity Safety Ambitions, aims to halve the 2010 rates of stillbirth, neonatal and maternal deaths, and neonatal brain injuries and reduce the rate of preterm birth from 8% to 6%.  It should be noted that this report only covers England.I want to assure you that Saving babies’ lives is a key priority for my Department.  There is a comprehensive programme of work currently underway to support the implementation of the recommendations set out within the Saving Babies lives care bundle. This work is being led collaboratively across the region, with a dedicated group established to oversee progress.The group actively reviews implementation status, identifies any gaps, challenges, or areas requiring additional focus, and works collectively to address them. Informed by this analysis, a regional action plan has been developed, setting out clear priorities to support consistent, high-quality maternity care and drive forward improvements in outcomes.This structured approach ensures that identified issues are addressed in a coordinated way and that best practice is shared across services to support the overall aims of reducing stillbirth and improving neonatal outcomes.This work supports the development of a regional action plan for Maternity and Neonatal Services, which consolidates all the actions / recommendations from the suite of work on maternity and neonatal safety that has been taken forward by the Maternity and Neonatal Services Safety Oversight Group. It is clear that there will be a funding requirement associated with the full implementation of the regional action plan for improving maternity and neonatal services, however while it is not possible to allocate additional funding due to the significant funding deficit my department faces, my officials are working closely with the Public Health Agency and Trusts to improve services within existing resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what support is available to schools that wish to transfer to a cashless system.
answer_text: A cashless system for the provision of catering services has been installed and are operational in approximately 45% of the post-primary schools where the Education Authority (EA) provides the catering. These schools have implemented a cashless system with the assistance and support of the EA and the external contractor. While no new systems have been installed with EA involvement for some time, a number of post-primary schools have independently introduced these systems. Schools that introduced a cashless system in partnership with the EA have benefited from the EA's contract with external service providers for annual maintenance. The contractor has provided technical support for system implementation and assistance with data protection requirements. Currently, there is no funding available to install new cashless catering systems or renew existing systems. The EA is actively seeking funding to expand access, and this initiative is being considered as part of the Education Information Solutions programme. The EA is able to provide advice and guidance to any school wishing to introduce a cashless catering system. The Schools Management System (SMS) will see a replacement to the current Schools Information Management Solution and is currently in pre-pilot phase. The pilot of the Bromcom School Management System, including online payments, is expected to launch in late summer 2025.Schools have been advised to contact the EA Commercial Procurement Team directly for ongoing support regarding this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the impact that late changes in defendant pleas, from not guilty to guilty, are having on all those involved in the justice system.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 17757/22-27, for an update on the replacement of the pedestrian footbridge at Muff Glen, Eglington.
answer_text: Since I corresponded with you in October 2024 via AQW 17757/22-27, I can provide you with an update as follows. The design and location for the footbridge has been finalised and agreed and a planning application is currently being prepared. This requires a flood risk management assessment, which has been commissioned by the Construction & Procurement Delivery of the Department of Finance. Following completion of the assessment the planning application will be submitted for consideration by the planning authorities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what role research and innovation will play in advancing the objectives outlined in the Food Strategy Framework Action Plan 2025-2027.
answer_text: The importance of research and innovation is recognised in the first Food Strategy Framework Action Plan, including the important role of social science and behavioural research to drive individual behaviour change. There is a specific Action to ensure effective signposting of the ranges of support services available to food & drinks processing businesses across areas including research and innovation support.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how the Food Strategy Framework Action Plan 2025-2027 aims to address sustainability in food production and distribution.
answer_text: Sustainability is at the heart of the Food Strategy Framework Action Plan and underpins our social, economic and environmental ambitions. Mission Two of the Action Plan centres on supporting environmentally sustainable and ethical agri-food supply chains. This recognises the important role for farmers/growers, processors, retailers, food service sector, government and consumers in protecting and enhancing natural resources, reducing our environmental footprint and transitioning to a more circular economy. It also recognises the challenges associated with social justice and fairness and transparency in agri-food supply chains. This first two-year Action Plan sets out 39 actions and a commitment to build purposeful partnerships across government and with key stakeholders to deliver real change and promote a positive future for our food system.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of how victims of domestic abuse can be better supported through the social housing system.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to confirm whether they both received a formal invitation to Pope Francis’s funeral.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the cost incurred by her Department to resolve the issue of water affected by nutrient pollution over the last five years.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has advised my department that the cost of treating drinking water, is made up of chemicals, power, staff, facilities and the cost to manage drinking water protected areas across the north. It cannot separate the costs to treat drinking water as a result of individual raw water constituents, risks or drinking water catchment pressures, including differences resulting from nutrient loading in the raw water source.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any discussions they have had with the UK Government in relation to funding for legacy issues, given it is not a factor in assessing need.
answer_text: We are committed to supporting victims and survivors of the past through the Victims’ Payments Scheme, the work of the Victims and Survivors Service, and the Commission for Victims and Survivors, and the commitments that we have made in the new Strategy for Victims and Survivors. As we have noted previously, the Victims’ Payments Scheme was designed and legislated for by the UK Government, so it is important that it makes a financial contribution to the Scheme’s costs. The Finance Minister is continuing to engage with the Treasury on this matter. Whilst legacy issues are a policy area for the NIO, we will continue to act in the best interests of victims and survivors at all times and we remain fully committed to ensuring that their needs are prioritised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on school placements for pupils with special educational needs for September 2025.
answer_text: The EA has advised that their early planning assumptions for September 2025 showed a need for approximately 1300 additional places, with similar increased geographical pressure areas as last year. Prior to Easter, Senior EA officials took forward an intensive round of engagements with those schools in areas with the greatest demand for capacity in September 2025. The EA have worked hard to increase capacity across the school estate, and as of 01 May 2025, they advise that there is an assumed pressure of around 400 places remaining for September 2025: approximately 250 in Special Schools and 160 in Specialist Provisions.  On 10 June last year, I reported that the number of places required for children in either a Special School or Specialist Provision was 470 for September 2024. While the overall demand for placements has decreased, the pressure for all types of placements is increasing. This is as a direct result of the continued lack of available capacity in the system to place children.In order to address these challenges, in April 2024, I announced the establishment of a SEN Capital Programme, which marked a significant step change in the approach to SEN capital investment and ensured that the system would be put on a stronger strategic footing across future years. My recently published SEN Reform Agenda also contains a range of actions which aim to ensure appropriate forward planning for special education provision in line with projected need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether her Department plans to adopt Riverside, Doagh, as an approved road; and (ii) to detail any support in place for residents to help repair road defects on unadopted roads.
answer_text: Riverside, Doagh is a private shared access which was never designed to be a public road. This private lane has never been part of the adopted road network.In accordance with the Private Streets (NI) Order the department may consider a private street for adoption where:- the department is of the opinion that the street should become a public road;a majority of the owners/ occupiers request it: and,the roads/streets are first brought up to an adoptable standard at the expense of the owners/ occupiers. I understand that agreement to fund the works to upgrade this private road to departmental standards has not been confirmed by a majority of the residents and that the ownership of the laneway has not been established. As the consent of the landowner is required for the legislative process required for the adoption of the road and the current layout of the private lane does not meet adoptable standards, the department could not consider the lane for adoption. As with all unadopted private roads, the maintenance of this shared access lane rests with the street owners, usually the frontagers or residents of the street.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of rising temperatures as a public health concern.
answer_text: Rising temperatures caused by climate change has been recognised as a public health concern. The World Health Organization (WHO) states that climate change presents a threat to public health and is impacting health in a myriad of ways, including by leading to death and illness from increasingly frequent extreme weather events, such as heatwaves, storms and floods, the disruption of food systems, increases in zoonoses and food-, water- and vector-borne diseases, and mental health issues.In accordance with the Climate Change Act 2008, the UK Government conducts a risk assessment for the UK every five years and this includes summaries for each UK country. These include assessments in relation to health, such as from high temperatures, and help to inform country specific national adaptation plans. Additionally, the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) established the Centre for Climate and Health Security in October 2022 and led on the fourth ‘Health Effects of Climate Change in the UK’ report published in 2023. The report brings together up to date evidence to inform policies and actions to secure health.The Health Effects of Climate Change report points out that many of the anticipated adverse health impacts of climate change in the UK are still avoidable through mitigation and adaptation and that there are other benefits to health to be gained. The latest Climate Change Committee summary for Northern Ireland points to an opportunity for health from high temperatures that needs further exploration. An example of this is the physical and mental health benefits of increased physical activity and contact with nature. As summer temperatures rise, opportunities for more outdoor activities could increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department is promoting community gardening programmes, including initiatives enabling communities to grow their own fruit and vegetables.
answer_text: My Department is not promoting community gardening programmes or initiatives enabling communities to grow their own fruit and vegetables.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures in place to ensure that young people in rural areas have adequate access to mental health services and support programmes.
To ask the Minister of Education how his Department is promoting school gardening programmes, including initiatives enabling schools to grow their own fruit and vegetables.
answer_text: My Department does not normally endorse or promote any particular programme or initiative to schools.  It is entirely a matter for individual schools to determine which programme or initiative to participate in to enhance curriculum delivery.The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment supports schools by developing curricular resources.  An example of this is the ‘Growing for the Future’ resource produced in partnership with Tourism Northern Ireland.  This resource aims to encourage young people to grow fruit and vegetables and explore where food comes from while promoting healthy eating.Schools here have the autonomy to engage with external providers to assist in the delivery of lessons if they feel it is a priority for their pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline whether a comprehensive review of alternative funding arrangements for Northern Ireland Water will be initiated.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 24243/22-27, whether she can provide a time frame for the erection of signage along the A26 road.
answer_text: The provision of any such signage needs careful consideration, balancing a sufficient evidence of need alongside the potential proliferation of signage on our roadsides.My Department has assessed the A26 and concluded that signs warning drivers of the presence of animals on carriageways are not required at this location.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any support her Department is providing to help rural residents access electric vehicles, particularly electric vehicle charging points.
answer_text: The provision and operation of both Electric Vehicles and publicly accessible Electric Vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure is a commercially led activity in the north.  Responsibility for the maintenance, development and utilisation of charge points rests solely with the Charge Point Operators (CPOs).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department is having with supermarkets to reduce their plastic usage.
answer_text: DAERA is the lead department with policy and legislative responsibility for plastics, specifically in relation to Single Use Plastics and Extended Producer Responsibility Schemes.  My department has worked closely with DAERA, alongside a coalition of stakeholders including Retail NI, FSB, CBI and others in developing a draft Circular Economy vision for the north that will be an important enabler of the Executive’s Green Growth Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department is having with the hospitality sector to switch to more environmentally conscious takeaway containers.
answer_text: I am committed to engaging with the hospitality sector to understanding the issues they are facing. My predecessor, Conor Murphy, met with NI Food to Go Association in October last year and the issue of single use plastics was raised. DAERA is the lead department with policy and legislative responsibility for packaging, specifically in relation to Single Use Plastics, and Extended Producer Responsibility Schemes.DAERA officials are working on transposing Articles 2-7, 14 and 17 of the Single Use Plastic Directive (EU) 2019/904 into domestic legislation. From this will flow several actions including producing a plan to reduce the consumption of single use plastic cups and food containers, increasing public awareness and promoting sustainable alternatives.DAERA officials will continue to collaborate closely with stakeholders to progress these significant measures that will help drive the North’s recycling rates and contribute to our green growth, climate change and environment strategy ambitions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any measures being taken to enhance access to training and apprenticeship opportunities in rural areas; and (ii) how her Department is engaging with local rural businesses to enhance these opportunities.
answer_text: My Department is committed to ensuring that training and apprenticeship opportunities are accessible across all of the North. The ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship programmes, which fund the off-the-job training element of an apprenticeship, are demand-led and provide a wide range of opportunities for people of all ages.  Most recent published statistics indicate that rural areas such as West Tyrone, Fermanagh and South Tyrone and Newry and Mourne have some of the highest apprenticeship participation rates across the North.Apprentices and employers are supported by a strong regional network of training providers that includes the six Further Education (FE) colleges, contracted private training organisations and the three local universities, providing access to the pathways and skills needed in rural areas.  In addition, the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) also plays a significant role in delivering apprenticeship training relevant to rural industries.The FE colleges are also involved in the delivery of the Traineeship programme, with support for work-based learning provided by local employers, and the Skills for Life and Work programme, which also benefits from delivery by some private training organisations across the North. 	A network of Sectoral Partnerships brings employers, qualification experts and policy advisors together with the aim of ensuring that apprenticeship and traineeship qualifications meet the needs of our wide range of industries.		Promotion of training and apprenticeship opportunities is not regional specific.  My Department runs a suite of advertising campaigns which raise awareness of apprenticeships and traineeships.  My Department also facilitates the annual Apprenticeship Week which, in February 2025, consisted of over 60 public events being held across the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the Climate Change Committee’s report entitled Northern Ireland’s Fourth Carbon Budget: Advice for the Northern Ireland Executive.
answer_text: The Climate Change Committee’s advice aligns with the Executive’s Energy Strategy, ‘The Path to Net Zero Energy’, in mapping a pathway to a secure, affordable and sustainable energy system. Decarbonisation is one of my key economic priorities and I support a just transition to net zero that generates prosperity for all.  My officials will continue to support DAERA in all aspects relating to the Climate Change legislation and in bringing forward the proposed Fourth Carbon Budget to the Assembly before the end of 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the Climate Change Committee’s report entitled Northern Ireland’s Fourth Carbon Budget: Advice for the Northern Ireland Executive.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the Climate Change Committee’s (CCC’s) Fourth Carbon Budget report and value the important role that the CCC plays, as an independent body, in advising on emissions targets and reporting to Parliament on the progress made in reducing greenhouse gas emissions. CCC held an online briefing session on their advice, for departmental sectoral leads on 1st April 2025, facilitated by DAERA, and my officials have been working through the report to consider the implications for the Department.Carbon Budgets are an important mechanism for keeping the North on track towards longer term emissions reduction targets. The CCC’s report made clear that the Executive need to broaden and accelerate action from today and through the 2030s, in order to meet the Fourth Carbon Budget and I am committed to ensuring that my Department plays its part in that. As the transport sectoral lead, my Department has a statutory responsibility to reduce emissions from the ‘transport sector’ to net zero by 2050. We have developed a strong evidence base to inform a credible pathway and an associated set of actions to achieve this. Data and expert advice are at the heart of our approach so, as you would imagine, my officials are giving careful consideration to the CCC’s advice and the recommendations included in the report. While this work is ongoing, it is clear that many of the recommendations are aligned to the actions my Department is already progressing such as the implementation of the Zero Emissions Vehicle Mandate; the development of integrated active travel networks; action to encourage greater use of public transport, and work with Translink to increase the provision of Park and Ride facilities.Translink is also contributing to climate change targets by operating and enhancing the public transport network, supported by capital funding from the Department. Recent projects include the opening of Belfast Grand Central Station, which improves infrastructure and encourages the use of public transport. Additionally, Translink is investing in a zero-emissions fleet, including electric and hydrogen-powered buses, to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and promote sustainable transportation. Subject to funding, plans are also being progressed to electrify the rail network, initially between Belfast to the border and following this, across the rest of the network.Collaborative working is key to addressing the shared challenges we face, and aligned to the Committee’s advice, my officials are also engaging with their counterparts in the Irish government, to explore how we can work in partnership on our shared objective of transport decarbonisation. This work will continue in the weeks and months ahead as we consider the full range of the Committee’s advice in more detail.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the Climate Change Committee’s report entitled Northern Ireland’s Fourth Carbon Budget: Advice for the Northern Ireland Executive.
answer_text: We welcome the ongoing advice and support of the Climate Change Committee. We will need to consider the advice report in detail and will discuss this with the Executive in due course.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on whether their Department has an agreed timescale for the appointment of a Northern Ireland Climate Commissioner.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 23277/22-27, whether her Department can erect signs along the A26 to warn drivers of the presence of animals on the road to reduce the risk of roadkill.
answer_text: My Department can erect signs to warn drivers of the presence of animals on the road to reduce the risk of impact, in line with policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration her Department has given, in the interests of active travel and safe travel for children from Ballymacrickett Primary School, to installing footways at Glen Road, Glenavy, between Glenavy village and Ballymacrickett Primary School.
answer_text: My department is currently updating its active travel design guidance in response to the recent public consultation on the Active Travel Delivery Plan. This work includes the development of a route assessment framework for Active Travel schemes which will place greater emphasis on encouraging modal shift, taking into account the population being served, the places it will connect to, and the level of improvement between the existing and proposed provision.  Officials will reassess this route once the framework has been fully developed and implemented.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what steps are being taken to ensure that givinostat, used to treat Duchenne muscular dystrophy, will be available through an Early Access Programme.
To ask the Minister of Education when he will publish the Early Learning and Childcare strategy.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action being taken by her Department to ensure that community input is incorporated into flood resilience planning decisions.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate provides Planning Authorities with advice on planning and flood risk matters in line with Planning Policy. This advice is to ensure that new development, where possible, is kept out of flood risk areas and does not contribute to an increase in flood risk elsewhere.Under the ‘plan-led’ planning system, Council Planning Authorities are responsible for determining the vast majority of planning applications. Applications are determined on a case-by-case basis taking account of community representations as well as the Local Development Plan, regional strategic planning policy, the responses of statutory consultees and all other material considerations. The relevance and weight to be afforded to any material consideration, including from the local community, would be a matter for the relevant planning authority in deciding each application.Through the work of the Regional Community Resilience Group (RCRG), which is co-chaired by my Department and local government, there continues to be engagement with 50 community groups in many areas across the North. These communities are primarily in flood risk areas, and the work of the RCRG helps them prepare for and respond to weather related emergencies. Through this community resilience work my Department seeks to provide an extra layer of support for those who may be impacted by flooding in periods of severe weather.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the conclusions her Department has drawn following COP29, including lessons learnt from the conference.
answer_text: COP29 made progress in securing increased climate finance for developing countries and finalising a carbon market agreement. However, the conference also highlighted the challenges and complexities of global climate action. Urgent steps are still needed to contribute to meeting the Paris Agreement temperature goal and address the growing importance of adaptation. In the face of these global challenges, we continue to lead by example through our net zero commitments within the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022. The transition to a net zero economy offers valuable opportunities for the creation of good jobs while achieving regional balance and increasing productivity. 30% of EU economic growth in 2023 was tied to the net-zero economy and it is important that we make the most of those opportunities while ensuring a just transition for all. My officials will be paying close attention to the outcomes of COP30.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the conclusions his Department has drawn following COP29, including lessons learnt from the conference.
answer_text: My Department did not attend the 29th Conference of the Parties (COP). As such any conclusions and lessons learnt are not from direct attendance. A core aim of COP29 was to build on previous discussions in relation to the ‘loss and damage’ fund which was established at COP27. Whilst there was some progress made at COP29, through the agreement to triple the finance available to developing nations impacted by climate change by 2035, there is still further work required to deliver on this commitment. On 23 January 2025, the UNFCCC reported that the fund had $741 million in pledges made by 27 contributors. Whilst this is a significant achievement, and represents positive progress, it unfortunately still falls below the $1.7tn levels requested by developing nations. At COP29 a key deliverable was the significant progress made in relation to the dialogue on international carbon market standards stemming from Article 6 of the Paris Agreement, on which, after close to 10 years of discussion, a consensus was reached. This is a vital step in helping to ensure that there are standards in place to verify and support investment in emissions reduction projects and to avoid potential double counting. Whilst there was progress made in relation to these two areas, unfortunately global emissions have continued to increase year on year which cannot be overlooked.  It has been widely reported that 2024 was the warmest year on record at about 1.5c above pre-industrial levels. As such a key conclusion must be that we need to accelerate taking meaningful action which delivers emissions reductions and builds resilience to the impacts of climate change. For some Nations this is about their very existence and survival. The decade ahead must be a decade of action.I welcomed the Prime Minister’s announcement at COP29 regarding the UK’s commitment to a Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) emissions reduction target of 81% by 2035. I believe that this commitment is a vital step in the UK Government reestablishing themselves as international leaders on climate change which must be a key take away.The UK Government subsequently published the UK 2035 NDC plan, on 30 January 2025, prior to the UNFCCC deadline of 10 February 2025. I am, however, disappointed that the UNFCCC NDC Registry shows that the UK is one of only twenty COP Signatory Parties to have submitted an NDC by this deadline. These plans are central to the delivery of the Paris Agreement as they set out how each Signatory Party to the conference plans to reduce their national emissions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department is addressing the potential effects of climate change on healthcare services in rural areas.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to implement strategies to mitigate wildlife roadkill incidents, particularly in known high-risk areas.
answer_text: While my Department can erect signs to warn drivers of the presence of animals on the road to reduce the risk of impact, there is no plan to develop specific strategies for high risk areas as suggested.You will be interested to know that my Department is currently working with the lead road authorities in England, Scotland and Wales on the development of standards aimed at protecting animals and wildlife.  This work will update the provisions in the Design Manual for Roads and Bridges (DMRB), specifically LD 121 ‘Design of wildlife connectivity structures’, which is used in the design of wildlife crossing structures where a connectivity need has been identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to ensure that rural areas are adequately equipped with healthcare resources to address future challenges posed by climate change.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when the Race Relations (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 will be amended.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount of money his Department has saved through the use of renewable energy sources in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust in the last five years.
answer_text: The Northern Health and Social Care Trust has reported savings in energy spend totalling £522,928 over the last five years due the use of renewable energy sources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans (i) his Department; and (ii) its arm's-length bodies has to change its vehicles to electric vehicles.
answer_text: My Department owns 1 motor vehicle- the Ministerial car.  This is a hybrid vehicle which is powered by an internal combustion engine and an electric motor. My Department has asked its Arm’s-Length Bodies to develop an approach to enable the phasing out of fossil-fuel vehicles in health and social care fleets. This includes consideration of alternative fuels such as Hydrogenated Vegetable Oil (HVO) as an interim lower carbon fuel and electricity.  Any change to electric vehicles will be part of the organization’s fleet asset management plan taking account of the vehicle type and age, affordability, installed charging infrastructure for fleet and suitable electric vehicle availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following Storm Éowyn, what learning the Northern Health and Social Care Trust has adopted into constituency plans to deal with severe weather.
answer_text: Northern HSC Trust have confirmed they have a formal Trust debrief scheduled for 12 March 2025 where they will aim to identify any learning to be incorporated into future plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department is raising public awareness in relation to the necessity of maintaining high nutritional standards in individuals’ diets.
answer_text: Improving diet and nutrition across our population has been a longstanding element in my Department’s ‘A Fitter Future For All’ obesity prevention strategy, which has a long term objective to increase the percentage of people eating a healthy, nutritionally balanced diet.The Public Health Agency raises public awareness in maintaining high nutritional standards in individuals’ diets through a range of programmes, promotions, and online resources supporting service providers and individuals to increase knowledge and skills relating to food and nutrition. The Food Standards Agency (FSA) raises public awareness of the necessity of maintaining high nutritional standards in individuals’ diets by providing resources to help everyone understand and learn about the Eatwell Guide which is a visual representation of the types of foods and drinks we should consume and in what proportions to have a healthy, balanced diet. Both organisations work with partners to implement nutritional standards in Health and Social Care, public sector and local council settings to ensure healthier options are available for staff and visitors. The FSA also works with industry partners to raise awareness of the nutritional content of foods that contribute significantly to energy, saturated fat, sugar and/or salt intakes of the Northern Ireland population, to make the products they make or serve healthier. Alongside local government, the FSA supports food businesses to display energy information on their menus through the Calorie Wise scheme which gives customers more information about the food they eat.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline how her Department works with women and girls, in contact with the justice system, who have offended or are at risk of offending.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 11 - AQO 1610 22-27ANSWERI recognise that a distinct approach is needed for women and girls who have offended, or are on the cusp of offending. That is why I launched the Supporting Change Strategy in March 2022, which fosters partnership working across and beyond justice to address the specific needs of this group.Under this Strategy, a range of initiatives have progressed. The PSNI have developed a Dignity in Custody gender responsive policy, covering health, hygiene, welfare issues, and the specific needs and vulnerabilities of females. It is also in the process of securing the designation of custody suites as Onus ‘Safe Places’.Looking to girls, the Youth Justice Agency has developed guidance underpinned by its Model of Practice and Child First principles. It has commissioned research to develop a better understanding of young women’s needs, helping staff to effectively support young women and girls and utilising best practice.Moving from juvenile to adult justice can be daunting. To combat this, Youth Justice and Probation are developing a transition protocol to support young women with the move.In respect of women, Hydebank Wood College has a dedicated Women’s Worker to support the unique needs of women in custody. Also, in recognition that many are also victims, the rollout of ‘White Ribbon’ training to both staff and prisoners is helping to raise awareness that violence against women and girls is unacceptable.Through the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime, Probation are funded to deliver the ENGAGE project. ENGAGE works with women in custody and the community, to build resilience and equip them with the skills and learning to withstand paramilitary influence when they exit the justice system.  These are just some examples of my Department’s work to support a reduction in offending and reoffending by women and girls. However, there is a much more happening, ‘on the ground’ across and beyond justice to support women and girls move away from crime.This is not something justice can do alone and I am hopeful that the new crossgovernmental strategy on offending and reoffending will strengthen our collective efforts to create brighter futures and safer communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how the energy network will be better protected from storms such as Storm Éowyn going forward.
answer_text: Question 10 AQO 1594/22-27Storm Éowyn was an unprecedented event. NIE Networks and partner agencies should be recognised for the tireless work that was undertaken during and after the storm.Once NIE Networks has completed its major event review following Storm Éowyn, my Department will engage with them and the Utility Regulator to understand the lessons learned.The Utility Regulator has recently approved an unprecedented £2.23 billion for the development and maintenance of the electricity grid over the period April 2025 to March 2031, this sum includes consideration of plans for risks related to the weather.Climate change was likely a material factor in the severity of Storm Éowyn.  My department has been working with DAERA and all departments to finalise the third Northern Ireland Climate Change Adaptation Programme (NICCAP3) in response to the risks and opportunities set out in the most recent UK Climate Change Risk Assessment (CRA3). The draft NICCAP3 will go to the Executive before a public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the condition of the Glen Road, Glenavy; and (ii) whether her Department has any plans to repair defects on the road.
answer_text: Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections of Glen Road and to repair defects in accordance with my Department’s current maintenance policy. The most recent inspection was carried out on 12 February 2025 and four defects were noted. These have been passed to our external contractor for repair.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the appointment of a Northern Ireland Climate Commissioner, as required in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The draft Northern Ireland Climate Commissioner Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2025 were laid in the Assembly in late January.This Statutory Rule is subject to the draft affirmative resolution procedure before the Assembly. That is, they must be laid in draft and then approved by the Assembly. The Business Committee will now schedule a date for a debate in the Assembly on a motion to approve the draft Statutory Rule.The Statutory Rule is in line with what is allowed for in the Climate Change Act in that it will allow TEO to establish an independent office to be known as the ‘Northern Ireland Climate Commissioner’ who will be empowered to oversee and report on the operations of the Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration his Department gave when planning the development of the new Northern Regional College facilities at (i) Causeway campus, Coleraine; and (ii) Farm Lodge campus, Ballymena, in relation to environmental sustainability.
answer_text: These projects were planned and developed by Northern Regional College and approved by the Department in 2016 under a combined business case. In terms of the environmental sustainability aspects, the approved business case provided that the college would be replacing inefficient buildings with modern, energy efficient facilities, and the design for both was to Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Methodology (BREEAM) “Excellent” standard. BREEAM “Excellent” is awarded to facilities that demonstrate best practice for environmental performance through design, specification, construction and operation and both campuses remain on target to achieve certification.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the 6-Year Rivers Strategy in the context of his Department’s response to climate change.
answer_text: My Departments approach to flood risk climate change adaption is already encapsulated in the 2021-2027 Flood Risk Management Plan (FRMP), which is required by the Water Environment (Floods Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2009. The FRMP is an interdepartmental approach to managing flood risk here. It sets the objectives and measures to manage flood risk at known areas of significant risk and more widely on a regional basis.In terms of our existing infrastructure the Rivers Asset Management Plan (AMP) 2020 – 2030 is another key strategic document to drive our flood risk climate change adaption work. Subsequently, the 6 Year Strategy was developed in order to provide focus and direction, to enable the implementation of the Flood Risk Management Plan (FRMP) and the Asset Management Plan. Work is already complete in 30 of the main activities including work in relation to the review of capital investment requirements and identification of the delivery teams needed to successfully deliver flood risk management activities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how his Department is addressing climate change mitigation requirements in the context of planning advice.
answer_text: I recognise that the planning system can play a positive role in supporting the wider efforts of government in the transition to a net zero carbon economy. Furthering sustainable development (including mitigating and adapting to climate change) is at the heart of the planning system and policy advice contained in my Department’s Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS). The provisions of the SPPS apply to the whole of the North and must be taken into account in the formulation of Local Development Plans (LDPs) and are material to all decisions on individual planning applications and appeals. To ensure that the SPPS remains up-to-date to inform plan-making and decision-taking in supporting climate action, my Department has carried out a review of policy on renewable and low carbon energy to reflect the changed context brought about through the Executive’s Energy Strategy and the Climate Change Act. I hope to publish this update to the SPPS as soon as possible, following Executive Committee agreement. Whilst responsibility for local planning policies and advice, rests with local government, my Department continues to work closely with all Councils as part of Plan Strategy and Local Policies Plan preparation to ensure that climate change is recognised and that local solutions to any challenges are suitably addressed. As regards to decision-making, under the two-tier plan led system all planning applications, including those for determination by my department, will be assessed on a case-by-case basis against the provisions of the SPPS, LDPs, guidance, the views of statutory consultees (including DfI Roads and DfI Rivers), and all other material considerations. The relevance and weight of material considerations is ultimately a matter of planning judgement for the relevant planning authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how Inland Waterways is working with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs in relation to addressing concerns around the ecological condition of Lough Neagh.
answer_text: The DAERA Minister established the Lough Neagh Stakeholder forum in December 2024 to ensure that stakeholder views are understood and properly considered during the implementation of the Lough Neagh Action Plan.  My Department participates in the Lough Neagh Stakeholder forum alongside NI Water and is content to be part of any discussions with DAERA on matters within its remit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how his Department is adapting flood risk management plans, taking consideration of climate change.
answer_text: Our approach to flood risk climate change adaption is already encapsulated in the 2021-2027 Flood Risk Management Plan (FRMP), which is required by the Water Environment (Floods Directive) Regulations (NI) 2009. The FRMP is an interdepartmental approach to managing flood risk here. It sets the objectives and measures to manage flood risk at known areas of significant risk and more widely on a regional basis.My Department undertakes a range of climate change flood risk management measures. This includes publishing climate change flood maps, advising planning authorities in relation to development proposals in climate change flood plains and including climate change considerations in the development of new flood alleviation schemes.In terms of our existing infrastructure the Rivers Asset Management Plan (AMP) 2020 – 2030 is another key strategic document to drive our flood risk climate change adaption work. It has identified the requirement to further invest in existing River’s flood risk management assets and made recommendations including, crucially, a need to assess of the level of flood protection existing assets actually provide when climate change flood flows are considered. This enables us to properly understand not only the investment in new infrastructure needed but how upgrades to infrastructure can also be adapted to be climate resilient.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the decision to remove intimidation points in the Housing Selection Scheme by the end of the financial year has led to an increase in applications.
answer_text: I sought the requested information from the Housing Executive’s Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“An Applicant presenting to the Housing Executive as homeless or threatened with homelessness due to alleged intimidation does not make a specific application for Intimidation points. They will present with their circumstances, which will be assessed under the homeless legislation, and, where they apply for social housing, their housing needs will be assessed under the Rules of the Housing Selection Scheme. The Minister announced the removal of intimidation points from the Housing Selection Scheme on 28th January. As such, it is too soon to establish any specific new trends in homelessness / housing data. It should be noted that applicants presenting to the Housing Executive following the Minister’s announcement who have alleged intimidation, and those with a housing application prior to the Minister’s statement, who present with a change of circumstances precipitated by an incident or set of occurrences that would trigger an investigation for the award of intimidation points, will continue to be robustly investigated in line with existing policies and procedures. Applicants will continue to be assessed and provided with emergency assistance under the homelessness legislation. Their circumstances will also be assessed under the Rules of the Housing Selection Scheme, including an investigation under Rule 23, prior to its removal. The number of cases awarded 200 points under Rule 23 and the number of cases under investigation under Rule 23 will be closely monitored during these transitional arrangements.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department is working with Libraries NI to (i) reduce the carbon footprint of its buildings; and (ii) transition to renewable energy sources where possible.
answer_text: Libraries NI is a member of my Department’s Climate Change working group, which is made up of members from across the Department and a number of our Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs). This Group was formed in recognition of the wide range of skills and business areas that need to come together to share resources, knowledge, and skills on environmental issues. The 2024-25 Climate change action plan is available on the Department’s website at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2024-12/dfc-climate-change-action-plan24-25.pdf The Action Plan outlines the Climate Change Mitigation, Adaptation and Behavioural Change activities that the Department and its ALBs, including Libraries NI, plan for 2024-25.This year I was able to provide Libraries NI with a capital budget allocation of £4,511k that has allowed the completion of a range of energy efficiency, carbon reduction and environmental improvement projects. These include the installation of low energy LED lighting, photovoltaic solar panels, and biodiversity habitat improvements across the Libraries NI estate and the recently opened Fivemiletown Library, which in its construction has incorporated measures to minimise energy use, eliminate pollution, preserve, and enhance biodiversity and conserve water resources. Libraries NI also received funding from the Department for the Economy’s Energy Invest to Save Programme and my officials facilitated Libraries NI to deliver three innovative pathfinder projects at Ballymoney, Bessbrook and Killyleagh libraries. These projects have delivered the first accredited EnerPhit (Passivhaus Institute) standard public buildings in Northern Ireland, revitalising and upgrading existing building stock to provide exemplar low energy facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the work to enable broadcasting in courts.

To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department is working with National Museums NI to (i) reduce the carbon footprint of its buildings; and (ii) transition to renewable energy sources where possible.
answer_text: National Museums NI is a member of my Department’s Climate Change working group, which is made up of members from across the Department and a number of our Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs). This Group was formed in recognition of the wide range of skills and business areas that need to come together to share resources, knowledge, and skills on environmental issues. The 2024-25 Climate change action plan is available on the Department’s website at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2024-12/dfc-climate-change-action-plan24-25.pdf.  This Departmental Action Plan outlines the Climate Change Mitigation, Adaptation and Behavioural Change activities that the Department and its ALBs, including National Museums NI, plan for 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the role of Libraries NI in delivering environmental education to schools, youth groups and the wider public.
answer_text: Libraries NI (LNI) provides a wide range of environmental education and learning opportunities to schools, youth groups and the wider public. Libraries NI signed up to the Green Libraries Manifesto in August 2023. Signatories of the manifesto commit to a set of common principles with an emphasis to put sustainability at the heart of their work and planning, to embrace innovation that will help change current practices and to grow and share knowledge.		It is for this reason that LNI is a member of my Department’s Climate Change working group, which is made up of members from across the Department and a number of our Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs). This Group was formed in recognition of the wide range of skills and business areas that need to come together to share resources, knowledge, and skills on environmental issues Further details on initiatives delivered by LNI can be found in the 2024-25 Climate Change Action Plan available on the Department’s website at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2024-12/dfc-climate-change-action-plan24-25.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his Department has had with Libraries NI to explore partnerships with environmental organisations to deliver green initiatives in library spaces.
answer_text: Libraries NI is a member of my Department’s Climate Change working group, which is made up of members from across the Department and a number of our Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs). This Group was formed in recognition of the wide range of skills and business areas that need to come together to share resources, knowledge, and skills on environmental issues. The group promotes partnership working towards climate-change initiatives not only across the Northern Ireland Civil Service but also with our partner environmental organisations in the wider community.	The 2024-25 Climate change action plan is available on the Department’s website at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2024-12/dfc-climate-change-action-plan24-25.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether libraries are being encouraged to expand their collection of environmental books, resources, and digital content to support public education on environmental issues.
answer_text: Libraries NI is a member of my Department’s Climate Change working group, which is made up of members from across the Department and a number of our Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs). This Group was formed in recognition of the wide range of skills and business areas that need to come together to share resources, knowledge, and skills on environmental issues. 	The 2024-25 Climate change action plan is available on the Department’s website at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2024-12/dfc-climate-change-action-plan24-25.pdfThis year I have been able to provide £2.7 million towards book stock funding which has helped Libraries NI provide a range of reading material on all matters, including environmental matters, for people of all ages which includes books, newspapers and digital resources. Libraries NI signed up to the Green Libraries Manifesto in August 2023, signalling a commitment to follow a set of common principles with an emphasis to put sustainability at the heart of their work and planning, to embrace innovation that will help change current practices and to grow and share knowledge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there are any initiatives to promote careers in (i) aviation; and (ii) airport management, to pupils in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My officials have advised that they are not aware of any initiatives that promote careers specifically in aviation or airport management to pupils in Northern Ireland.Information about career pathways and insights into work across different sectors is an important element of careers related learning.  In support of this, every post-primary school in Northern Ireland is a member of an Area Learning Community (ALC) with a careers sub-group which provides a mechanism for schools to work together on issues such as engaging with local employers and developing joint careers events for their pupils.  Both the ALCs and the Northern Ireland Schools and Colleges Careers Advisors (NISCA) run conferences at which different sectors and employers provide information and advice to careers advisors and teachers to help them better support their learners.In addition, officials in the department’s 14-19 Strategy Team regularly engage with local businesses and industry groups on careers-related matters and share careers information with schools.  They can be contacted via 14-19strategyteam@education-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there are any initiatives to promote careers in (i) aviation; and (ii) airport management for those wishing to study in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department’s Economic Infrastructure Division is currently developing an aviation policy, which aims to bring together all aspects of the aviation sector across departments and agencies and to promote better domestic and international connectivity across the region. In the area of Apprenticeships there are a range of industry led sectoral partnerships that are responsible for the wide range of pathways across our economy.  The aviation and airport occupational areas sit within the Hospitality and Tourism sectoral partnership. Aviation frameworks were withdrawn on 31st December 2023, due to lack of qualification availability, and to date, there has been insufficient industry demand to justify the development of replacement provision. This position will be kept under review.More broadly, my Department’s Careers Service offers impartial and personalised advice and guidance on an all-age basis, provided by professionally qualified Careers Advisers. The Careers Service provides an online Careers A-Z tool, offering instant and flexible access to up-to-date information on careers, skills, labour market information and qualifications, to help inform and develop the career planning and management of young people and adults.  The A-Z tool includes information on careers in the aviation sector.The Careers Service also has a dedicated Careers Occupational Information Unit, which tracks developments across local sectors and produces careers information resources that aim to raise awareness of careers opportunities available locally. Publications, to include careers information on aviation and airport management, will issue in the next business year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of Environmental Farming Scheme agreements currently in place, broken down by (i) wider level agreements; and (ii) higher level agreements.
answer_text: The Environmental Farming Scheme (EFS) is a voluntary agri-environment scheme that supports farmers and land managers to carry out environmentally beneficial farming practices on agricultural land.  Each agreement lasts for 5 years.EFS is made up of three levels - Wider, Higher and Group.A total of 6,387 EFS agreements have been entered into across eight tranches since the EFS opened for applications in July 2017.  These were 4,426 Wider agreements and 1,961 Higher agreements.  A total of 4,464 agreements from Tranches 1, 2 and 3 have now completed their 5-year term.At present there are 1,923 active EFS agreements; 704 EFS Wider agreements and 1,219* EFS Higher agreements.  A further 70 EFS Higher Tranche 8 offers of agreement will issue early February 2025, which, if all accepted, will bring the total agreement numbers to 1,993. *This figure includes 132 Tranche 8 agreements
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of hectares; and (ii) the percentage of agricultural land, currently in the Environmental Farming Scheme.
answer_text: The Environmental Farming Scheme (EFS) is a voluntary agri-environment scheme that supports farmers and land managers to carry out environmentally beneficial farming practices on agricultural land.  Each agreement lasts for 5 years.	EFS is made up of three levels - Wider, Higher and Group.EFS differs from previous agri-environment Schemes in that it is not a whole farm scheme.  For EFS Wider, applicants select which options and fields to enter the scheme.  For EFS Higher, only those fields identified as designated sites or areas of priority habitat may be considered for entry. The June 2024 Agricultural Census calculated the total hectares of agricultural land within Northern Ireland at 1,040,392 hectares.  Currently, over 55,700* hectares of agricultural land are part of an active EFS agreement.  This is equivalent to 5.4% of all agricultural land within Northern Ireland. *Further EFS Tranche 8 offers of agreement will issue in early February 2025 containing approximately 5,550 ha of agricultural land, which will add an additional 0.5% of all agricultural land within Northern Ireland to the above figures.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of Environmental Farming Scheme wider level agreements that have ended since this level was withdrawn in 2023.
answer_text: Since 2017, 4,420 businesses entered into Environmental Farming Scheme (EFS), Wider Agreements across six Tranches.  The last EFS Wider Agreements, Tranche 6, began in January 2023 and are due to complete in December 2027. 		The number of EFS Wider Agreements in each Tranche are detailed in the following table:		EFS Wider Tranche	Start Date	Completion Date	Number of Agreements	1	03 Jul 2017	31 Dec 2022	912	2	01 Jan 2019	31 Dec 2023	1,310	3	01 Jan 2020	31 Dec 2024	1,494	4	01 Jan 2021	31 Dec 2025	256	5	01 Jan 2022	31 Dec 2026	124	6	01 Jan 2023	31 Dec 2027	324	        Total			4,420		Since January 2023, 2,804 EFS Wider Agreements have come to an end (Tranche 2 and 3 agreements).  In addition, 912 Tranche 1 agreements ended prior to 2023.   Therefore, a total of 3,716 farm businesses have completed their 5-year EFS Wider agreements.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Mobuoy consultation.
answer_text: The public consultation is an important step and there has been significant work ongoing to prepare for this. The consultation will give all interested parties the opportunity to comment on the draft strategy, I have approved that we move to public consultation, we intend to launch the consultation in early spring.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how his Department is working with the Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport to improve mobile phone signal across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Telecommunications policy is a reserved matter, since February 2023 the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT) have held the policy responsibility on improvements to mobile connectivity. The Department of Digital Culture Media and Sport previously held this remit.			DfE officials maintain regular contact with DSIT on all telecommunications matters ensuring that all local issues are given appropriate consideration for any policy decisions taken regarding the improvement of mobile connectivity here.		My officials and DSIT officials also jointly participate in several initiatives which will help improve mobile connectivity here. For example, to help address the wider challenges faced by the Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) regarding telecommunications infrastructure rollout, my officials led on the development of the cross sectorial Mobile Action Plan for Northern Ireland (MAP NI) and established the Barrier Busting Taskforce, which is the delivery vehicle for the recommendations made by the MAP NI. More details about MAP NI can be found at economy-ni.gov.uk.		My officials also contributed towards the Shared Rural Network (SRN) project which was developed by the MNOs and Building Digital UK (BDUK), an executive agency of DSIT. SRN aimed to deliver 95% 4G coverage and enable rural businesses and communities to thrive. 		Although responsibility for delivery of SRN falls to the MNOs and BDUK, many local stakeholders sat on the NI SRN working group to make representations and provide a local perspective, which helped achieve local connectivity targets on schedule. 		Progress of SRN is measured by Ofcom, who recently announced that the SRN programme has significantly reduced the scale of partial not spots and consequently increased the areas where all MNOs provide coverage. More details about the SRN programme can be found at srn.org.uk.
