question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Mobile Action Plan for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Mobile Action Plan for Northern Ireland (MAP NI) was published on 23 June 2022. The delivery vehicle for the actions identified within the MAP NI is the Northern Ireland Barrier Busting Taskforce (NIBBT). Officials from my Department chair the NIBBT Oversight Group and the NIBBT Communication Subgroup. The NIBBT Communication Subgroup is delivering a programme of information to help inform citizens, address any perceived risks regarding new mobile technologies and outline the advantages, opportunities and benefits to the local economy.  I am confident that every effort to develop and deliver all actions that fall within my Department’s remit are being progressed at pace. The ongoing work streams  required to deliver other MAP NI actions by the NIBBT, are dependent on participation of all stakeholders who sit outside my Department. My officials are currently producing a milestone report on the progress and status of the NIBBT, which will be published in due course. To date the NIBBT Communications Subgroup has: Conducted surveys for Council Officials and Elected members and of the business community to help identify information needs; Established Digital Champions in each local council who have formed a network which meets regularly. Each Digital Champion acts as a single point of contact for mobile industry, engages with stakeholders and promotes collaboration within and across councils (MAP NI action 4); Delivered an information webinar for council elected members on 14 November 2024 (MAP NI action 3); andWorked with the Local Government Association (NILGA) on the production of a Guide to Connectivity for Councillors which is nearing completion (MAP NI action 3)Other work is ongoing to produce suitable material for citizens and the business community. The NIBBT is collaborating with Belfast City Council’s 5G Innovation Region Team on appropriate communication products.The NIBBT Planning Subgroup is currently comparing the planning landscape across Britain prior to deciding if action is required and the Publicly Owned Assets Subgroup is investigating the potential use of publicly owned assets by mobile infrastructure providers.  More information about MAP NI can be found at economy-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rationale behind the closure of Straidhavern Primary School, Crumlin.
answer_text: My rationale for the decision in relation to DP 731 Straidhavern Primary School is based on the detailed report given to me by my officials and is as follows:-“I have carefully read, examined and considered all of the papers and information pertaining to this proposal including all of the objections made. I acknowledge the important and valued role Straidhavern Primary School has played for those children and young people from the local community who attend the school including for those from some military families in the area. 	 	However, it is clear from the evidence provided to me in this report that there are significant sustainability issues and deficiencies at the school; coupled with the fact that there is already sufficient available places in other good schools in the area and against a backdrop of a declining school age population in the area; I believe it would be in the best educational interests of those children and young people at the school that I approve this proposal. 	 	It is important that the Education Authority should ensure that the small number of children remaining, and their parents, are supported through the transition to other schools in the area. Every effort should be made to minimise the disruption to children’s educational experiences and support, care and attention should be given to affected teaching and non-teaching staff who are impacted by the closure.”	 A copy of the report and my rationale can be accessed here: Development Proposal Decisions 2025

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his latest assessment of bringing forward flood alleviation plans following the recent flooding in Antrim town.
answer_text: My Department is currently in the process of commissioning a consultant to undertake a feasibility study to assess the flood risk from the Sixmilewater River to properties in Antrim, Muckamore and Dunadry. It is currently anticipated that this study should commence early this year and I can advise that such a study would typically take approximately 18 months to complete.The study aims to identify if there are any economically viable schemes to reduce the impact of river flooding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of winter pressures in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department’s winter preparedness planning commenced in March 2024, which was two months earlier than the previous year.Trusts worked with the Regional Control Centre in the months ahead to develop plans specific to each area.  Trusts submitted their Unscheduled Care Preparedness plans to SPPG in September 2024.A significant programme of work has been undertaken to reduce the potential risk of harm for patients who experience long waiting times in the emergency department.The Northern Trust opened a Surgical Assessment Unit (SAU) in February 2024 followed by an Acute Ambulatory Unit (AAU) for medical conditions in April 2024, to supplement the existing Frailty Direct Assessment Unit (DAU) at Causeway Hospital. These services are aimed at easing pressure on the Emergency Department (ED) by facilitating direct referral from GPs and the ambulance service. From October 2024, the DAUs in Causeway and Antrim also provide access to advice from Trust clinicians which can help maintain patients in their own home, or attend more appropriate services the same day/week. The Trust updated their DoS (Directory of Services) in October 2024 to include Causeway AAU and shared information with primary care to increase direct referrals.  This aims to increase the number of patients accessing alternative pathways to ED in an effort to reduce ED pressures and decrease ambulance handover delays.The Trust commenced a ‘Frailty in-reach to ED’ service in Antrim in October 2024. The Trust is also increasing care home capacity, working with the independent sector, to develop an enhanced model of care for people with greater needs, such as dementia. The Trust have also engaged with the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) and General Practitioners to increase support to care homes and prevent unnecessary admissions.The Trust continues to phase in the ‘Safer Patient Flow Bundle’ initiative across hospital wards and a frailty in-reach service in Antrim and Causeway EDs aimed at reducing non-elective length of stay. The Trust will also extend the REACH (Responsive Support, Education and Anticipatory Care with Care Homes team) initiative into winter, so that care home residents with recurrent readmissions to ED due to falls will be provided with a holistic assessment by the REACH team including a comprehensive falls assessment, a medication review and onward referral as required. This aims to reduce Emergency Department attendance and reduce hospitals admissions.From an operational perspective the Regional Control Centre sought assurances in November that Trusts had adequate plans in place to manage any pressures over the Christmas and New Year Period.  Actions taken in advance of the holiday period were to maintain capacity for front door, inpatient ward, key services (diagnostics) and discharge & community provision. With regard to demand on services, ED attendances and hospital admissions have been lower in 2024/25 than they had been in 2023/24. The reduction is n

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on Project Gigabit.
answer_text: The procurement for Project Gigabit commenced on 11 November 2024 with an invitation to interested suppliers to complete a selection questionnaire to demonstrate their capacity and capability to deliver such a contract.  Those suppliers who meet the published criteria of the selection questionnaire will be invited to submit a response to the requirements of the Invitation to Tender for the project. The procurement will seek a supplier to address an initial scope of approximately 11,000 premises here. My Department expects to award a contract for Project Gigabit in the coming months, subject to compliant bids being made, bids meeting the published criteria, and all necessary approvals being agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on required remedial work to the buildings at Maine Integrated Primary School, Randalstown.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department is addressing issues related to road safety for animals, particularly in areas with large wildlife populations.
answer_text: The Department can install signs to warn road users about ‘cattle’ (red triangle, white background and black picture of a cow), ‘wild animals’ (red triangle, white background and black picture of a deer), ‘wild horses and ponies’ (red triangle, white background and black picture of a horse), and ‘accompanied horses or ponies’ (red triangle, white background and black picture of a person on a horse).   At the development stage of any major road project, my Department undertakes a comprehensive Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA), as required by Part V of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993, to meet the requirements of the EIA Directive which often includes a Habitats Regulations Assessment, as required under the Conservation (Natural Habitats, etc.) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1995.As part of the EIA process for major roads projects the potential impacts on wildlife and, particularly, protected species are identified and where appropriate, mitigation measures are implemented to effectively manage these impacts. On recent major roads projects, mitigation included the provision of 65.8km of wildlife fencing and 37 wildlife underpasses. There was also 2.54 hectares of bat hop-over planting installed as part of the overall landscape design.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions he has had with the tourism sector to promote the use of public transport options for tourists.
answer_text: I recognise the important role public transport can play in promoting tourism here and will continue to engage with a wide range of stakeholders, including Translink, to ensure that public transport provides a viable option for both local and international tourists alike.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how his Department is promoting initiatives that encourage ethical treatment of animals within the tourism sector.
answer_text: Tourism NI supports and promotes several visitor experiences who work with animals as an integral part of their offer, delivering on the key visitor motivations of connecting with local people, authenticity and sustainability.Where experience providers or attractions would benefit from specialist advice on animal welfare or related issues, Tourism NI refers them to colleagues in the Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs who are best placed to advise on such matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement he has had with the Northern Ireland tourism sector on achieving a net zero future.
answer_text: In line with my four economic priorities and the Programme for Government, decarbonisation will form an integral part of the emerging 10-year action plan for tourism, which has been co-designed with the sector through the work of the Tourism Partnership Board which I established in September.Tourism NI is already engaging actively with tourism businesses to reduce their carbon impact. The suite of support includes:The Leaner & Greener programme which provides practical tools to help tourism businesses to reduce operating costs through more effective monitoring of energy, water, waste and food waste; An intense Tourism Business Sustainability Programme helps businesses to develop targeted action plans to reduce carbon, as well as provides guidance to tell their ‘sustainability stories’ to visitors. By March 2025, nearly 100 businesses across the North will have completed this programme; andmodules to develop sustainability skills on Tourism NI’s online learning platform, MyTourismNI. This platform is free to access for all tourism businesses.In order to further support tourism businesses on their journey to net zero, a tailored carbon calculator is being developed by the tourism agencies in Ireland. It is intended that the new calculator will be launched in Spring 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the public engagement initiatives undertaken by the Rivers Agency in the last three years.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorates have a range of public engagement activities. My officials regularly engage with members of the public during the development of our capital works scheme development, in locations, such as, Eglinton, Belfast and Portadown.  In addition, through the work of the Regional Community Resilience Group (RCRG), which is co-chaired by my Department and Local Government, there continues to be regular engagement with over 40 community groups in various locations across the North. These communities are primarily in flood risk areas and the work of the RCRG helps them prepare for, and respond to, weather related emergencies. Following the flooding in the South East in Autumn 2023, and as part of the review process, there were a number of public engagement events held in Newry, Portadown, Newcastle and Downpatrick. One of the recommendations from this review is to consider approaches to improving community awareness of flooding which will be taken forward by both my Department and other multi-agency partners.In addition, Rivers Directorate have also engaged with the education sector through attendance at the annual Ulster University science careers fair over the last three years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail which vaccines for travel are provided by the NHS, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: GPs are paid to provide advice and some travel vaccines under the General Medical Services (GMS) Contract as part of the adult vaccinations and immunisations service.  Patients are entitled to be supplied with vaccines by their own GP and not be charged for supply, advice or administration for the following vaccines for the purpose of travel: TyphoidCholera Polio Hepatitis A In addition adults who have not had a full course of some vaccinations usually given in childhood are entitled to vaccination regardless of travel plans.All other vaccines which fall outside the GMS Contract must be paid for privately. These vaccines include hepatitis B, Japanese encephalitis, meningococcal vaccine, rabies, tick borne encephalitis and yellow fever. The above contractual obligations are in place across Northern Ireland, irrespective of Trust area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures he is taking to improve animal welfare in the remainder of the 2022-2027 mandate, with particular reference to (i) regulating the rescue and rehoming sector; (ii) reviewing microchipping regulations; and (iii) regulating cat breeding.
answer_text: I am actively considering my animal welfare priorities for the remainder of the mandate, including the measures you have highlighted. To ensure the most pressing issues are addressed, I have already met with members of the Northern Ireland Companion Animal Welfare Group to outline my proposals and listen to their feedback.  Further meetings will be planned for early in the New Year including with local councils and the veterinary associations. Following this engagement, it is my intention to publish a programme of animal welfare reforms which I wish to see delivered within the time remaining in this mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on how his Department is supporting the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on the Clean Air Strategy.
answer_text: Air quality is the lead responsibility of the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA).As outlined in response to AQW 16204/22-27, the Executive’s overarching strategic framework for Public Health, Making Life Better (MLB), recognises that health and wellbeing is influenced by many factors, including the wider physical environment. MLB includes a commitment to protect and promote good health and wellbeing through working collaboratively to improve air quality.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department is implementing any measures to increase recycling rates in buildings used by their Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: The Executive Office (TEO) and its arm’s-length bodies adopt a number of measures to increase recycling rates, as follows:   TEO raises awareness through the publication of intranet articles and related briefings.  100% of the glass collected is sent to a contractor for recycling. 100% of the food waste collected goes to either Anaerobic Digestion or Composting.Where possible, stationery items will be re-used rather than disposed of.	 To reduce waste, plastic water cups have been replaced with recycled paper cups and reusable glassware provided in office kitchens.Where possible, when items need to be replaced, they are replaced with recycled/sustainable items.Recycling receptacles are placed throughout buildings to help reduce contamination and to make it easier for the contractor to separate and, where possible, recycle waste.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department is implementing any measures to reduce general waste in buildings used by their Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: The Executive Office (TEO) and its arm’s length bodies take a number of measures to reduce general waste, as follows:  TEO raises awareness through the publication of intranet articles and related briefings.  Where catering is necessary for external events or meetings, care is taken to ensure the minimum amount required is ordered.  Food waste is collected from buildings and repurposed through anaerobic digestion by the NICS contractor into fertilizer or biogas.  Where possible, stationery items will be re-used rather than disposed of.			 To reduce waste, plastic water cups have been replaced with recycled paper cups and reusable glassware provided in office kitchens. Where possible, when items need to be replaced, they are replaced with recycled/sustainable items.Recycling receptacles are placed throughout buildings to help reduce contamination and to make it easier for the contractor to separate and, where possible, recycle waste.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any actions they are taking to address climate change within the Executive Office.
answer_text: As is the case with all departments, The Executive Office is bound by the overarching obligation of the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 to achieve Net Zero by 2050.The Act has also placed a specific requirement on this department to establish the office of the NI Climate Commissioner. Once established, the Commissioner will play a critical role in overseeing our transition to a low-carbon economy.The regulations which will allow for the establishment of the Commissioner were recently considered by the Committee for Agriculture, the Environment and Rural Affairs, and the Committee for the Executive Office. They will soon make their way to the Assembly for consideration.The Executive Office is a relatively small department that does not have responsibility for policies and programmes capable of significantly reducing greenhouse gas emissions to address climate change in isolation, however, we do have measures in place to help reduce emissions and improve energy efficiency where we can.Official cars within the department have recently been switched to hybrid vehicles. In line with the NI Executive’s commitment to the COP26 Joint Declaration, the department will work towards all new cars being zero emissions by no later than 2035.In addition to this, our Departmental Premises Officers’ energy management and energy related responsibilities include implementing, within their respective buildings, departmental strategies and policies in relation to the efficient use of energy, which contributes to departments’ sustainability initiatives. This includes a commitment to raise awareness, through the publication of intranet articles and sustainability themed briefings. Sustainable behaviours are promoted through encouraging staff to travel by sustainable methods such as the Cycle to Work initiatives, car sharing, car parking spaces with electric charging points, and the mandatory use of recycled paper from legal and sustainable sources. Furthermore, Departmental Premises Officers in larger buildings have access to a building energy management interface to provide direct access to temperature variables and overtime settings.  This provides a facility for Premises Officers to fine tune temperature settings to reduce energy consumption, consequently reducing Helpdesk calls. Heating is thermostatically controlled so that radiators shut off when optimum office temperatures are achieved. Finally, Lighting within buildings is being converted to more energy efficient LED fittings to reduce energy consumption, and offices are being fitted with sensors to automatically turn lights off when no movement is detected.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department is implementing any measures to increase recycling rates in buildings used by his Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: Within Departmental buildings there are a range of measures in place to ensure recycling of waste takes place. In order to increase recycling rates where waste has been generated, staff are reminded of the need and benefits of recycling through signage and ongoing communication, and training is also available. Departmental officials are also engaging with the Department of Finance to investigate how to improve waste and recycling contracts.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department is implementing any measures to reduce general waste in buildings used by his Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: In the implementation of The Single-Use Plastic Reduction Action Plan my Department led on work, in partnership with the Department of Finance (DoF), to ensure the removal of all unnecessary single-use plastic from the government estate.  Staff are also reminded of the need to reduce paper waste through printing only when necessary. Where food, or other waste, is generated it is dealt with through DoF collaborative contracts or local government arrangements to ensure separation of waste streams to reduce general waste through recycling.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department is implementing any measures to increase recycling rates in buildings used by her Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer provided for AQW 18233-22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department is implementing any measures to reduce general waste in buildings used by her Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: I intend to publish a social value strategy in early 2025 which will outline the measures my Department will take to improve its practice, policies and procurement spend in relation to social, economic and environmental impacts. This will include how it will reduce its waste and increased the amount of recycling within its office estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his efforts to introduce Lucy's Law.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department is implementing any measures to increase recycling rates in buildings used by his Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: The Department promotes recycling with communications to staff on green housekeeping and appropriate use of recycling facilities, and initiatives including removal of bins for individuals, provision of recycling bins in central areas, the Single Use Plastics Reduction Programme, promotion of less printing and photocopying, removal of individual desk printers, and double-sided photocopying.Waste is managed by means of two official NICS Collaborative Framework contracts, i.e. Provision of Dry Recycling Services, and Provision of Non-Hazardous Waste Management Services, for the North of Ireland Public Sector.The Department’s Arm’s Length Bodies manage and have responsibility for their own buildings and estates including recycling policies and the two official NICS contracts are available for them to use.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department is implementing any measures to increase recycling rates in buildings used by her Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: The Department’s Outline Sustainability Strategy sets out our goals and supporting actions to reduce our carbon footprint.The Department of Justice and its agencies actively promote recycling with recycling bins available across our buildings.  Under our strategy, we continue to review levels of recycling with a view to setting targets for improvement, where appropriate, by 2025/26.We have an active information programme in place to encourage behavioural change with our staff, both in the workplace and at home. For example, we actively promote and encourage staff to participate in the NICS Removal of Single Use Plastic Initiatives, such as Plastic Free Fridays.  We also promote other key events such as National Recycling Week. Recycling activities also occur at each prison establishment, creating inmate activity and helping reduce the levels of waste that would otherwise be sent to landfill.  All accommodation areas have separate containers for general waste, recyclables and food waste.  All prisons have compactors, balers and other recycling equipment on site; which increase the recycling rates at each establishment this has increased recycling rates (paper, plastics, cardboard, metal, wood).  At HMP Maghaberry, the recycling facility recycles a range of plastics and other waste such as: recycling expired covid test kits, recycling decommissioned laptops to extract precious metals, the separate collection and disposal of Waste Electrical Electronic Equipment (WEEE), making briquettes from paper and sawdust and repairing old wheelchairs and bicycles for charity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department is implementing any measures to reduce general waste in buildings used by his Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: The Department and its predecessors have been promoting waste management with communications to staff on green housekeeping and appropriate use of recycling facilities, and initiatives including removal of bins for individuals, provision of recycling bins in central areas, the Single Use Plastics Reduction Programme, promotion of less printing and photocopying, removal of individual desk printers, and double-sided photocopying.Waste is managed by means of two official NICS Collaborative Framework contracts, i.e. Provision of Dry Recycling Services, and Provision of Non-Hazardous Waste Management Services, for the Northern Ireland Public Sector.The Department’s Arm’s Length Bodies manage and have responsibility for their own buildings and estates including waste management policies and the two official NICS contracts are available for them to use.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department is implementing any measures to reduce general waste in buildings used by her Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: The department’s Outline Sustainability Strategy sets out our goals and supporting actions to reduce our carbon footprint.  The Department of Justice and its agencies actively promote good waste management practices across our buildings with bins designated for the disposal of recyclable goods and general waste. Under our Sustainability Strategy, we continue to review levels of recycling and waste generation with a view to setting targets for improvement, where appropriate, by 2025/26.We have an active information programme in place to encourage behavioural change with our staff, both in the workplace and at home. For example, we actively promote and encourage staff to participate in the NICS Removal of Single Use Plastic Initiatives, such as Plastic Free Fridays.  We also promote other key events such as National Recycling Week. Good waste management practices are carried out at each prison establishment, creating inmate activity and helping reduce the levels of waste that would otherwise be sent to landfill.  All accommodation areas have separate containers for general waste, recyclables and food waste.  All prisons have compactors, balers and other recycling equipment on site; which increase the recycling rates at each establishment this has increased recycling rates (paper, plastics, cardboard, metal, wood).  At HMP Maghaberry, the recycling facility recycles a range of plastics and other waste such as: recycling expired covid test kits, recycling decommissioned laptops to extract precious metals, the separate collection and disposal of Waste Electrical Electronic Equipment (WEEE), making briquettes from paper and sawdust and repairing old wheelchairs and bicycles for charity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is implementing any measures to reduce general waste in buildings used by his Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: The Department of Health, based in Castle Buildings, takes a number of measures to reduce general waste and increase recycling rates including:-Provision of segregated and labelled plastic, aluminium cans, paper and general waste disposal bin stations to allow to allow the appropriate disposal of general waste and to encourage recycling;The return of used ink toners to the manufacturer for recycling;Awareness raising through posters, leaflets and audio-visual information on reducing personal waste produced in the office and reducing dependence on single-use plastics by highlighting reusable alternatives that minimise unnecessary waste and pollution; and Use of the NICS contracted  waste management contractor for collected waste to be repurposed through anaerobic digestion into fertilizer or biogas.In response to the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 the department has requested the Health and Social Care Sector to:- review levels of recycling/residual waste and set targets for improvement; andRaise awareness of excess consumption to reduce waste at source and explore opportunities for lower carbon methods of waste disposal.The Health and Social Care (HSC) sector as waste producers must comply with relevant waste legislation. Guidance to support the HSC  in complying with regulations is provided by Health Technical Memorandum (HTM) 07-01. All the HSC Trusts have waste management policies to support the management of waste including regular training and environmental (waste) audits at ward / department level to ensure appropriate waste segregation leading to very high levels of reported recycling/waste recovery. Further measures include all waste tenders, resulting in waste service contracts having Social Value Considerations. This requires the contractor to think about how wider social, economic, and environmental benefits can be incorporated within the services provided to the Trusts to minimise general waste.To further improve recycling rates the Northern Trust are collaborating with a manufacturer of face masks to deliver a mask recycling pilot project. At the UK level the department is working with the Department of Health and Social Care in support of its Design for Life Strategy for medical devices. This strategy is aimed at delivering circularity in medical device manufacture in a way that enables them to be reused, remanufactured or recycled, preserving their value for as long as possible.

answer_text: The Department of Health, based in Castle Buildings, takes a number of measures to reduce general waste and increase recycling rates including:-Provision of segregated and labelled plastic, aluminium cans, paper and general waste disposal bin stations to allow to allow the appropriate disposal of general waste and to encourage recycling;The return of used ink toners to the manufacturer for recycling;Awareness raising through posters, leaflets and audio-visual information on reducing personal waste produced in the office and reducing dependence on single-use plastics by highlighting reusable alternatives that minimise unnecessary waste and pollution; and Use of the NICS contracted  waste management contractor for collected waste to be repurposed through anaerobic digestion into fertilizer or biogas.In response to the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 the department has requested the Health and Social Care Sector to:- review levels of recycling/residual waste and set targets for improvement; andRaise awareness of excess consumption to reduce waste at source and explore opportunities for lower carbon methods of waste disposal.The Health and Social Care (HSC) sector as waste producers must comply with relevant waste legislation. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is implementing any measures to increase recycling rates in buildings used by his Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: My Department and its arm’s-length bodies have installed recycling facilities encompassing separate receptacles across many of its premises.  Posters are prominently displayed in DfI buildings encouraging good practice and providing advice to staff on general recycling.Where appropriate, my Department will continue to promote, encourage and implement recycling initiatives across its premises. One such initiative in DfI HQ at James House, is the removal of under-desk waste bins to encourage staff to use the recycling stations provided to help increase the amount of recycling in the building.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is implementing any measures to reduce general waste in buildings used by his Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: My Department and its arm’s-length bodies have installed recycling facilities across many of its premises to reduce general waste.  Posters are prominently displayed in DfI buildings encouraging good practice and providing advice to staff on general recycling.Where appropriate, my Department will continue to promote, encourage and implement waste reduction initiatives across its premises. One such initiative in DfI HQ at James House, is the focus on eliminating single use plastics by encouraging staff to use sustainable products to reduce the amount of waste we send to landfill.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is implementing any measures to increase recycling rates in buildings used by his Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: Premise Officers within the buildings occupied by my Department encourage staff to separate waste streams so appropriate recycling can take place.Whilst this issue is an operational matter for Arm’s Length Bodies, I have sought the requested information from them. Some of the measures implemented by the Arm’s Length Bodies for which my Department is the sponsor body include:the segregation of recyclables from general waste and using signage and guidance to encourage recycling amongst staff; promoting positive environmental improvements in delivering public services programmes; and moving administration processes fully online thus negating the reliance on paper-based procedures. The Chief Executive of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive has advised that ‘alongside searching for alternative ways to increase recycling levels, the NIHE converts recovered waste into Refuse Dry Fuel which can then be used for creating electricity’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will change the relevant procedures to enable multi-use sites, containing both short-term respite and residential beds, to expand provision of respite services.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is implementing any measures to reduce general waste in buildings used by his Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: Staff within the buildings occupied by my Department are encouraged to separate waste streams so appropriate recycling can take place. Premises Officers within these buildings monitor the situation in compliance with all statutory and non-statutory obligations.  Staff are encouraged, via regular internal communications and information sessions, to consider how they can contribute to the Department’s climate and sustainability goals, for example by reducing reliance on single use plastics. Some of the measures implemented by the Arm’s Length Bodies, for which my Department is the sponsor body, include:  promoting the principles of reduce, reuse, recycle and recover;moving to online systems thus eliminating hard-copy printing and disposal;setting targets for positive climate action change; andconverting waste into Refuse Dry Fuel that is then used for creating electricity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 9111/22-27, for an update on the replacement of the pedestrian footbridge at Muff Glen, Eglington.
answer_text: Since I corresponded with you in April 2024 via AQW 9111/22-27, I can update as follows. Forest Service has continued to take forward necessary statutory and procurement considerations in conjunction with DfI’s Rivers Agency and Construction & Procurement Delivery of the Department of Finance. A number of options have been developed for the footbridge and a topographical survey has recently been completed to inform decisions on the most sustainable solution. Officials from the relevant parties are now working together to draw up the agreed specification, for consideration by the planning authorities, which subject to achieving consent can then proceed to tender.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department is implementing any measures to increase recycling rates in buildings used by his Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: My Department and its arm's-length bodies (ALBs) are fully committed to increasing recycling rates. DE has onboarded to a range of collaborative frameworks established to recycle, including but not limited to:the repair, reuse, removal and recycling of furniture items;the provision of dry recycling (paper, glass, cardboard, tin); andthe removal of protectively marked material for recycling.DE and our ALBs also worked closely with colleagues in the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to eliminate unnecessary single-use plastic across our organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department is implementing any measures to reduce general waste in buildings used by his Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: My Department is implementing a number of measures to reduce general waste in buildings used by my Department and its Arm’s-length Bodies (ALBs). With the introduction of hybrid working my Department has reviewed its accommodation requirements, reducing its overall size and the subsequent waste produced.  My facilities management staff promote, co-ordinate and implement green housekeeping measures, to reduce the levels of waste our buildings.DE and our ALBs endeavour to recycle as much as possible to minimise the overall volume of general waste being sent to landfill.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department is implementing any measures to prevent food waste in buildings used by his Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: My Department utilises the Department of Finance (DoF) collaborative contract for waste collection from its office buildings. This is implemented through the use of food caddies to separate and recycle food waste. In buildings not covered by this contract the relevant local council food waste recycling procedures are utilised. Where food is prepared on-site, at canteens etc, separate arrangements are in place through the relevant contracts.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider increasing the cost for a dog licence.
answer_text: At this juncture, I have not made a decision to review dog licence fees, but should I do so, any progress will be subject to the resources I have available and the time remaining in this current mandate. I acknowledge that the fee has remained at the same level since it was amended in 2011, and any proposal to review dog licence fees here will involve close engagement with local councils.I am planning to meet shortly with key stakeholders, including local councils, to explore animal welfare priorities for my Department until the end of the mandate. These discussions will include the possibility of increasing the dog licence fee.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider increasing the fine for individuals who do not license their dogs.
answer_text: The penalty for keeping a dog without a licence is a fine of up to £1000 or a fixed penalty ranging from £50 - £80.I consider the current fines for keeping a dog without a licence to be adequate and therefore have no plan to increase it. I am planning, however, to meet shortly with key stakeholders, including local councils, to explore animal welfare priorities for my Department until the end of the mandate and I am open to considering any stakeholders views on this matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department is implementing any measures to prevent food waste in buildings used by her Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: The Department of Justice and its agencies seek to match supply with demand in order to minimise food wastage at source.For example, within our prison establishments prisoners pre-order their choice of meal from menus supplied to them.  These include making food choices available such as healthy eating and also options to suit those who require meals on religious or dietary grounds (including vegetarian, vegan etc.). Individual meals are produced with careful consideration given to portion control and calorific value.  In addition, regular Food Forums provide opportunities for prisoners to provide feedback on the menus to ensure they remain appetising and appealing.  All this helps to ensure that we minimise any potential wastage of food.Prisoners and kitchen staff are also encouraged to separate any residual food waste from other recyclable waste streams.  The separate food waste is stored at collection centres on each site and transferred to an Anaerobic Digester Plant by a waste collection contractor, which is then used to generate heat and electricity. The NI Prison Service receive a monthly report to identify and monitor the amount of food waste collected.In terms of the responsibly of disposing of any waste, the Department of Justice and its agencies can make use of the Department of Finance Collaborate Contract for Non-Hazardous Waste Management Services.  This service provides for the collection, removal, transportation, disposal and recycling of non-hazardous waste which includes food waste.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will commit to funding the Belfast Wide Early Years pilot to the end of the 2024-2025 academic year.
answer_text: The Executive has not yet set the 2025-26 DE Resource baseline budget and whilst no firm commitment can be given, I would hope to continue to fund the pilot until the end of the academic year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on work pursuant to the High Street Taskforce Delivering a 21st Century High Street report.
answer_text: The report presented to us by the High Street Task Force represents a genuine collaboration across many sectors, and we would like to thank the Members of the High Street Task Force for their work.  The scope of the 14 recommendations span the responsibilities of a number of departments, and we wrote to Ministerial colleagues at the Departments for Communities, Economy, Finance and Infrastructure on October 10th, asking them to consider the recommendations and determine the way forward, bringing matters to the Executive for consideration as necessary.A number of responses have been received to date and we look forward to seeing how the recommendations are taken forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is implementing any measures to prevent food waste in buildings used by his Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: My department and its arm’s-length bodies have installed recycling facilities, including food waste receptacles, across many of its premises.  Food caddies are provided for food waste which are then collected by a third-party contractor to be recycled.  Posters are prominently displayed in DfI buildings encouraging good practice and providing advice to staff on food wastage and general recycling.  In addition, refrigeration facilities are provided to improve the longevity of food and other perishable goods such as dairy products, sauces and jams.Where appropriate, my Department will continue to promote, encourage and implement recycling initiatives, including for food waste, across its premises. One such initiative in DfI HQ at James House, is the removal of under-desk waste bins in an effort to encourage staff to use the recycling stations provided to help reduce the amount of waste we send to landfill.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is implementing any measures to prevent food waste in buildings used by his Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: The majority of my Department’s sites do not have a catering facility and as such, food consumption is generally limited to personal usage, resulting in minimal waste; of the three sites that do offer catering facilities, food waste is managed by the contracted supplier.  My Department promotes Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) food waste reduction initiatives through the dissemination of information published on the internal staff Intranet.	Some of the Arm’s Length Bodies for which my Department is the sponsor body have put in place initiatives to reduce food waste in their buildings. These include separate food waste bins in staff areas and donating surplus food to charities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department is implementing any measures to prevent food waste in buildings used by her Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: There are no on-site catering facilities in any buildings used by my Department or its arm’s length body, therefore food waste is generated from any excess food brought into the building by staff.  In accordance with the Food Waste Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015, food waste is collected separately to other waste streams. Compostable bin liners are used to collect food waste which allows it to be processed by the contractor to create a biomethane fuel for vehicles and bio-fertilizer which is supplied to local farms for use in food production.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department is implementing any measures to prevent food waste in buildings used by his Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: The Department produces very little food waste in either its operations or from staff working in its buildings, therefore my Department is currently not implementing any measures to prevent food waste in the buildings it uses.  Our partner organisations, Stranmillis University College, Construction Industry Training Board, Invest NI and Tourism NI implement basic measures within their facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is implementing any measures to prevent food waste in buildings used by his Department or its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: The Department of Health, based in Castle Buildings, Stormont Estate takes a number of measures to prevent food waste, as follows:Fridges and microwaves are available to allow appropriate storage and reheating of food by staff;Where catering is necessary for external events or meetings, care is taken to ensure the minimum amount required is ordered; andFood waste is collected and repurposed through anaerobic digestion into fertilizer or biogas by the NICS waste management contractor. Whilst best endeavours are taken to avoid wastage by Health and Social Care Trusts(HSCTs), food production will naturally incur additional wastage as the exact number and type of meals required versus the number of meals ordered varies in a dynamic hospital environment i.e. through unpredictable admission and discharge numbers. The following measures, to prevent food waste, are being implemented across each HSCT.South Eastern HSCT Measures are taken to avoid food wastage at procurement, storage, preparation, ordering and post service stages, specifically - Menu choices and portion sizes are coded by dieticians, this is to ensure patients receive a nutritionally balanced meal and avoid malnourishment. In units where bulk frozen meals are used, choices are ordered in small and large pack sizes to match the number of meals requested, and thus reduce the number of individual portions wasted.Where possible, vegetables are purchased pre-prepared, this reduces the risk of food poisoning, but also reduces food production waste. Stock is rotated to ensure First In First Out approach is taken.Meals are ordered as close to service time as possible (1 meal ahead) to reduce plate waste and they are currently exploring electronic bedside ordering, which will refine this process further.Belfast HSCTThe catering department have robust processes in place that allows staff to only order the amount of food that is required giving careful consideration to perishables. Menu reviews and food waste audits are also conducted on a regular basis and systems such as Saffron are used to help manage menus, recipes and stock control which assists in minimising the risk of waste. Southern HSCTA Quality Improvement Project Group has recently been established to review and improve all hospital patient meal ordering processes and to  progress the implementation of an electronic meal ordering system to reduce food wastage.Western HSCTElectronic bedside meal ordering has been implemented, in partnership with nursing staff, where patients are shared a visual image of the meals on offer and more recently a very successful “Lighter Lunch” initiative which was designed with input from dietetics and service users. In addition, the Trust’s Catering Team continue to seek feedback from patients and clients on their menus which in turn supports ongoing changes/improvements. Northern HSCTFood wastage is monitored on the Saffron catering system and electronic tablets are used to order and supply patient meals whi

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department is implementing any measures to prevent food waste in buildings used by their Department or its ALBs.
answer_text: The Executive Office and its Arm’s Length Bodies take a number of measures to prevent food waste, as follows:fridges and microwaves are available to allow appropriate storage and reheating;where catering is necessary for external events or meetings, care is taken to ensure the minimum required is ordered; andfood waste is collected from buildings and repurposed through anaerobic digestion by the NICS contractor into fertilizer or biogas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has considered implementing a demolition levy to address the growth of embodied carbon emissions from the built environment.
answer_text: My officials are constructively engaging with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA), as the lead department on climate matters, on the role that the tax system might be able to play in supporting our climate ambitions. Officials will also shortly be meeting with their counterparts in Scotland and Wales to discuss potential common areas for fiscal reform that would support the transition to net zero and accelerate climate action. While no specific consideration has been given to a demolition levy at this point, I am happy to consider any climate proposal related to taxation brought forward by Minister Muir in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the charging of electric bicycles is being considered in the plans to improve the electric vehicle charge point network.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle (EV) charge points is a commercially led operation. Responsibility for the maintenance, development and utilisation of those charge points rests solely with the Charge Point Operators (CPOs).Individual CPOs are best placed to advise whether the charging of electric bicycles is being considered within their wider charge point network plans.My officials continue to engage with EV owners and provide advice to CPOs on operational matters.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the appointment of a Climate Commissioner.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the DfI Rivers 2020 to 2030 Asset Management Plan.
answer_text: The 10 year Asset Management Plan 2020-2030 is a key strategic document that my Departments Rivers Directorate have used to inform the Capital Investment needed in our infrastructure over the next 10 years as well as looking at the long term management of the assets referred to in the Plan.The 10 year Asset Management Plan identified 114 recommendations of which 32 are already complete with another 45 currently in progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department gathers feedback from schools on the effectiveness of animal welfare education initiatives.
answer_text: The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment is responsible for producing resources to support delivery of the curriculum.  This includes resources on animal nutrition, behaviour and welfare which provide young people with opportunities to explore the life and health sciences sector in Northern Ireland in the context of animal welfare.  Schools have the autonomy to use a range of resources in their delivery of the statutory curriculum and my Department does not monitor the use of specific resources in individual schools. In relation to careers, quality careers education and guidance, which is a statutory requirement in schools, provides young people with information on options and the available pathways into higher education, further education, training and employment.  This will help students to make informed choices about their future career, offering insight into various professions and educational requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to (i) increase vegetarian and vegan meal options in hospitals; and (ii) incorporate more local, sustainable produce into  hospital meals.
answer_text: Belfast HSC TrustBelfast HSC Trust are currently testing vegetarian and vegan products which will increase the range of meals we can supply.Frozen food is kept to a minimum. The HSC Trust currently use locally sourced raw fruit and vegetables and locally farmed raw meats, including pork, beef and fish. Raw chicken is sourced from Europe through a local supplier. (All meats are purchased raw and cooked from fresh in our kitchens. No cooked meats are purchased). Our breads, dairy produce, eggs and coffees are all from local suppliers and all other ingredients and produce are supplied from local STS approved ethical and sustainable suppliers.The Trust have recently added an exciting selection of vegan and vegetarian options to our menu range which were developed by our in-house chefs using local produce. The Trust also have a dedicated section which makes dysphagia dietary meals which are suitable for patients and clients with swallowing difficulties. This is unique for the Belfast Trust.New dysphagia products are also under development. All single use food packaging used in Belfast Trust restaurants are compostable, thus adhering to our drive to be more sustainable.Western HSC TrustMenus remain under regular review which includes service user feedback, however there are no plans currently to increase the current offering.  The Western Trust food is purchased via regionally tendered contracts and suppliers are determined by the adjudication criteria for the contract.    Northern HSC TrustThe Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT) supplies a choice of vegetarian and vegan meals on all patient menus. The Trust will also provide an alternative vegetarian or vegan meal if patients require something different to the menu.Vegetarian and vegan choices are also on offer in all our dining rooms.The NHSCT takes part in the Love British/Love Northern Irish Food initiative, with the Loughview Restaurant in Antrim Area Hospital winning the award for the best participant in British Food Fortnight in 2022. This was in recognition of all the work the team had done in celebrating Northern Irish food during the national food celebrations and for inspiring other Trusts to get involved.The aim of Love British/Love Northern Irish Food is to encourage people to seek out British/Northern Irish Food when they are shopping or eating out and in doing so to discover the delights of the diverse and delicious food produced in Great Britain & Northern Ireland. They want to create a vibrant domestic market for British/Northern Irish food that gives farmers and food producers the confidence to invest and develop their businesses because they will see that there is a demand for their produce.The focus is on getting local produce into our hospitals and we have collaborated with the other Trusts to create a simple menu, which only uses Northern Irish produce to create dishes with local ingredients. The group is about sourcing and showcasing the best of local Northern Irish

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has to (i) increase vegetarian and vegan meal options in schools; and (ii) incorporate more local, sustainable produce into school meals.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operational delivery of school meals and this includes the provision of menu options to meet the relevant nutritional standards for school meals. It has confirmed that it has no current plans to increase vegetarian and vegan options in its school meal menus.Primary schools are able to opt for either a one or two choice menu for school lunches. If one of these choices were to be replaced by a vegetarian/vegan option, the EA would be unable to meet the required nutritional standards. To meet the needs of those children and young people who wish to follow a vegetarian or vegan diet, this can be requested through the Special Diets procedure which can be obtained from the school they attend. All post-primary school canteens operate a cafeteria style where a range of vegetarian and vegan food options are made available at hot food serveries, sandwich bars and salad counters on a daily basis. The EA is committed to procuring local, sustainable produce where there is sufficient capability and capacity in the market to meet EA’s requirements. Of the EA’s current food suppliers, 90% are based in Northern Ireland. The EA encourages its larger suppliers to supply local products were possible. The EA conducts pre-market engagement activities prior to every food tender to assess the market’s capability and capacity to meet the EA’s requirements and to identify interested suppliers. The findings from these pre-market engagement activities inform each specific procurement strategy. All pre-market engagement activities are publicised on eTendersNI and all local suppliers are encouraged to participate and engage with EA prior to any tender process commencing.EA Catering Service and EA Commercial Procurement Service are also actively working with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs as part of its Food Strategy Framework which includes an activity to explore the feasibility of increasing public sector procurement of local food.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has worked with animal welfare organisations in relation to aspects of the curriculum which relate to animal welfare and responsible dog ownership.
answer_text: The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment is responsible for producing resources to support delivery of the curriculum.  This includes resources on animal nutrition, behaviour and welfare which provide young people with opportunities to explore the life and health sciences sector in Northern Ireland in the context of animal welfare.  Schools have the autonomy to use a range of resources in their delivery of the statutory curriculum and my Department does not monitor the use of specific resources in individual schools. In relation to careers, quality careers education and guidance, which is a statutory requirement in schools, provides young people with information on options and the available pathways into higher education, further education, training and employment.  This will help students to make informed choices about their future career, offering insight into various professions and educational requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether education around animal welfare is being linked to career paths in veterinary science.
answer_text: The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment is responsible for producing resources to support delivery of the curriculum.  This includes resources on animal nutrition, behaviour and welfare which provide young people with opportunities to explore the life and health sciences sector in Northern Ireland in the context of animal welfare.  Schools have the autonomy to use a range of resources in their delivery of the statutory curriculum and my Department does not monitor the use of specific resources in individual schools. In relation to careers, quality careers education and guidance, which is a statutory requirement in schools, provides young people with information on options and the available pathways into higher education, further education, training and employment.  This will help students to make informed choices about their future career, offering insight into various professions and educational requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether education around animal welfare is being linked to career paths in veterinary science.
answer_text: In relation to the six Further Education colleges specifically, the veterinary and animal management provision incorporates animal welfare and animal science subjects delivered through a combination of theoretical, practical and experiential learning. Throughout the relevant curriculum, there are specific units relating to animal welfare, health and ethics which can be applied to a range of career pathways in veterinary science. It should also be noted that the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs has overall responsibility for the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise and hence may be able to provide further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the connection between animal welfare and public health.
answer_text: Neither my Department nor the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA)  has commissioned any research to determine if the ownership of a companion animal generates measurable improvements in the health and wellbeing of the persons who own the animals but research and analysis undertaken by several UK universities, suggests that in certain circumstances, owning a companion animal may have positive impacts on mental health, such as mitigating feelings of loneliness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is considering introducing no idling zones outside schools in order to reduce air pollution.
answer_text: Legislation under the Motor Vehicles (Construction and Use) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1999 sets out when the driver of a vehicle should stop an engine while stationary for the prevention of noise or exhaust emissions. The Highway Code also states that drivers must not leave a vehicle engine running unnecessarily while stationary on a public road. Enforcement of the Regulations is a matter for the PSNI. I recognise the impact vehicle idling can have on air pollution, especially outside of schools. I welcome the steps being taken by some local district councils through the 'Engine Off, Prevent the Cough' campaign, supported by the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs through the Local Air Quality Monitoring grant, which aims to educate the public about the impact of keeping their engines running while waiting in and around schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the funding and resources that are available to local communities for flood resilience projects; and (ii) how communities can apply for this support.
answer_text: My Department currently administers the Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme (HFPGS). This scheme, which remains open to new applications, covers 90% of the costs, up to a maximum of £10,000, of the total survey and estimated installation costs. The homeowner is required to make a contribution of 10% of the survey and estimated installation costs and any additional cost that may be incurred above £10,000. Further information on the HFPGS is available on my Department’s website at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/homeowner-flood-protection-grant-scheme-application-packIn addition, my Department, along with Local Government, is co-chair of the Regional Community Resilience Group (RCRG). This group works closely with local communities affected by flooding to help them prepare for, and respond to, severe weather events including flooding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the progress of flood alleviation schemes that are currently underway; and (ii) how these schemes are being prioritised.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate currently has over 50 separate potential flood alleviation schemes on its forward looking Capital Works Programme (CWP), with approximately half of these already in progress at various different stages. Details of these schemes are available on my Department’s website at:   https://storymaps.arcgis.com/stories/32cb28e2dc744243bd2d2c48e7245008.  In terms of prioritising flood alleviation schemes, the benefits, in terms of “flood damage avoidance”, must outweigh the actual costs of constructing and maintaining the works for it to be considered an economically viable scheme which can be taken forward.Cost viable schemes are scored and prioritised before being placed on the Capital Works Programme (CWP). The scoring mechanism broadly assesses the existing degree of flood protection, scheme economics, special considerations, and financial control.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of no idling zones in protecting those vulnerable to air pollution.
answer_text: My Department has undertaken no direct assessment of the impact of no idling zones on those vulnerable to air pollution. Air quality is the lead responsibility of the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA).The Executive’s overarching strategic framework for Public Health, Making Life Better (MLB), recognises that health and wellbeing is influenced by many factors, including the wider physical environment. Numerous studies point to the physical and mental health benefits of access to green spaces and better air quality. MLB includes a commitment to protect and promote good health and wellbeing through working collaboratively to improve air quality.An example of collaboration to date includes work with colleagues in DAERA to ensure that targeted effective air quality advice is issued to the public. The advice sets out steps which individuals can take to minimise their exposure to pollutants, or to reduce or alleviate any resultant symptoms. This advice is available on the NI Direct website: http://www.nidirect.gov.uk/air-pollution-and-health. The advice also includes a link to DAERA’s air quality website (www.airqualityni.co.uk) where members of the public can find out current or forecast air pollution levels in their area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department is collaborating with NI Water to monitor and improve water quality, particularly in relation to its impact on health.
answer_text: The safety of drinking water is of paramount public health concern for the Department of Health (DoH). As such the Department chairs the Drinking Water and Health Liaison Group which meets twice a year. This is a multi-agency group which considers public health issues associated with the drinking water supply. The Group draws its membership from the main stakeholder organisations including the Department of Health, the Public Health Agency (PHA), the Drinking Water Inspectorate, the Northern Ireland Public Health Laboratory, Environmental Health Northern Ireland and Northern Ireland Water (NI Water). One of the key outputs of this Group is a joint guidance document on ‘Drinking Water and Health’. This guidance sets out the roles and responsibilities of the key players, describes the wider context to the provision of safe drinking water, and details the arrangements and protocols in place to monitor compliance with standards and respond to an emergency or incident situation.DoH also works closely with DWI and has a role in the periodic, multidisciplinary review of the criteria set within the ‘Drinking Water and Health’ guidance for the notification of drinking water quality issues to PHA.In addition, the Public Health Agency (PHA) is a member of the multi-agency Blue-Green Algae Monitoring Group led by the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA), alongside NI Water and DWI. This Group is responsible for the production of an ‘Inter-Agency Blue-Green Algae Monitoring Protocol’. DoH is also supportive of the Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan produced by DAERA and agreed by the Executive.DoH welcomes the work being done by NI Water to raise awareness, for elected representatives, interested bodies and school pupils, as to the importance of the treatment processes used to treat raw water sources, such as Lough Neagh, to produce safe, clean drinking water.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to launch public health campaigns that raise awareness of the links between environmental issues, such as pollution and climate change, and health.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency has no current plans in place to develop campaign programmes to raise awareness about links between environmental issues and health.At this time, the Agency is actively exploring the priorities for its new corporate strategy, to be launched in 2025. In this context, the Agency is aware of the increasing need to raise awareness around a range of important health conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures his Department can put in place to deter birds from gathering on electricity lines above residential properties.
answer_text: NIE Networks has responsibility for electricity line design, construction and maintenance.  NIE Networks advises that customers who are facing issues with birds should contact NIE Networks’ customer helpline (03457 643 643) to be referred to the appropriate Customer Relations Manager for their region.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that new healthcare facilities are designed and built with sustainable, environmentally friendly practices.
answer_text: Health capital projects utilise a number of tools and standards in their procurement and construction phases so that they are both designed and built with sustainable and environmentally friendly practices to minimise impact to the environment. In the appointment of design teams, the design team submission is quality assessed against award criteria that includes their approach to sustainability.The design team and design process are reviewed utilising the Achieving Excellence in Design Evaluation Toolkit (AEDET) to provide a quality assessment of design and the designers at the various design stages. All capital construction projects greater than £2 million are required to be designed and constructed and assessed to the Building Research Establishment's Environmental Assessment Method (BREEAM) . The aim is for all projects to reach an excellence rating where this is shown to be achievable and value for money.Contractors delivering major capital projects also participate in the ‘Considerate Constructors Scheme’ which includes a category of ‘caring for the environment’ which is rated, monitored and scored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any initiatives his Department has that are aimed at promoting green spaces in healthcare facilities, in order to enhance mental well-being among residents.
answer_text: As part of its duties under the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 my Department, in conjunction with its Arms- Length bodies have developed an initial list of priority actions to support efforts to reduce carbon emissions. One action is for the health and social care sector to progress the development of biodiversity action plans to encourage biodiversity and provide natural environments for the mental health and general well being of all service users. Nature based solutions are being incorporated where possible and included in the benefits realisation plan for projects.Major capital projects are required to follow the Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Method (BREEAM) and aim for an ‘Excellent’ rating. Credits can be achieved by providing external areas to enhance the wellbeing of building/service users. Major Capital Projects are assessed through design stages using the Achieving Excellence Design Evaluation Tool (AEDET). This includes assessment under the ‘Impact’ category which encourage designs that maximise opportunities for access to usable outdoor space including green spaces.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 12351/22-27, for his assessment of the Carrier Bag Levy Annual Statistics 2023/24.
answer_text: Following my reply of 31st May 2024 to your AQW 12351/22-27, a further set of annual validated statistics were published on 22nd August 2024. These are the second set published since the introduction of the new legislation on 1 April 2022 (The Single Use Carrier Bags Charge (Amendment and Revocation) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2022), which increased the carrier bag levy charge from 5 pence to 25 pence and extended the scope to include all new carrier bags with a retail price of five pounds or less.I welcome the success of the current Carrier Bag Levy legislation, which has seen a further reduction of 5.3 million carrier bags from circulation. I will continue to monitor the annual statistics year on year to help inform any future review of the current legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department is working with (i) the Department for Communities; and (ii) other statutory and voluntary agencies to protect historic monuments from coastal erosion.
answer_text: Where sea defences form part of the structure of the road, my Department is responsible for the upkeep and maintenance of these defences as part of the fabric of the road network. Where there are historic monuments at risk from coastal erosion, the Department for Communities (DfC) is the lead department and, when requested, my Department will seek to provide support to any project led by them, subject to the availability of resources. The financing of any project associated with historic monuments is a matter for DfC.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans his Department has to make traffic lights more energy efficient.
answer_text: My Department is already committed to the use of energy efficient Extra Low Voltage traffic signal equipment that incorporates the use of LEDs with 44% of traffic signals currently utilising this technology.  All new traffic signal installations and upgrade sites are equipped with LED as standard and a programme is underway, subject to funding, to replace all remaining halogen bulb sites.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on his Department reaching net zero.
answer_text: A comprehensive update on the priorities and milestones my Department is progressing towards the decarbonisation of the economy has been published in DfE’s 2024/25 Business Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of social houses that are planned to be built in South Antrim in the next five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) is currently formulated on a 3-year basis. The information below is therefore provided on this basis. In the South Antrim Parliamentary Constituency:There have been 4 social housing completions to-date in 2024/25. There are currently 161 social housing units under construction. There are 189 social housing units programmed to start as part of the SHDP 2024/25 – 2026/27.
