Please note that the delivery of the SHDP is subject to Budget availability. Programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval. Additional schemes can also be added to the SHDP ‘in-year’ through the Competitive Land, Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / Off-the-Shelf properties. The programme formulation process for the SHDP 2025/26 – 2027/28 is currently underway, and further schemes may be added in the South Antrim Parliamentary Constituency in due course, subject to Departmental/Ministerial Approval”.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the publication of the Living With Water in Derry/Londonderry Plan for consultation.
answer_text: My Department is leading on the development of a Living With Water in Derry Plan, to help protect against flooding, enhance the environment and support economic growth. My officials have been engaging with key stakeholders during the development of the draft Plan, including Derry City & Strabane District Council, Northern Ireland Housing Executive, and the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs.  ‘A Vision for Living With Water in Derry’, a pre curser to the Plan, was published in May 2023. The draft Plan is due to be published for consultation later in the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the Office for Environmental Protection’s recent report entitled, A Review of Implementation of the Water Framework Directive Regulations and River Basin Management Planning in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the report and I will now take time to consider the report fully.   I recognise the challenges outlined in the report, the majority of which are for DAERA to lead on.  My Department will continue to work with the Office for Environmental Protection (OEP), to address the three recommendations in which DfI is specifically referenced, and will input to additional recommendations as required.		 In 2024/25, I provided NI Water with a total indicative budget allocation of almost £0.5billion, representing just under 40% of the total non-ring-fenced budget for my Department. This reflects the central importance of water and sewerage services to our society and to improving water quality.		My Department will continue to work closely with NI Water, DAERA and the Northern Ireland Environment Agency, to help deliver improvements in water quality and I will continue to raise the need for NI Water to have secure and sustainable funding, with the NI Executive and the British government, to help ensure the company can meet all its environmental obligations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to review the upper limits of the Housing Executive Disabled Facilities grant scheme.
answer_text: On February 1st 2023, the “Housing Renovation etc. Grants (Grant Limit) (Amendment) Order (Northern Ireland) 2023” came into operation which increased the upper limit on the amount of grant payable on award of a mandatory Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG) from £25,000 to £35,000. This same amendment also increased the maximum amount payable on the discretionary component of the Disabled Facilities Grant from £25,000 to £35,000, increasing the total potential award payable from £50,000 to £70,000. A Review of NIHE Administered Private Sector Grants, including the Disabled Facilities Grant, has recently been launched but is still in the early stages. This review will take a holistic view of the current operation of these grants and the upper limits are envisaged to be assessed as a component of this review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department reaching net zero.
answer_text: The Department of Health and its Arms-length bodies (ALB) are working to transition to net zero in line with the requirements of the Climate Change Act (CCA) (NI) 2022. The Department of Health continues to work with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA), the lead department for climate change, in the  development of the first Climate Action Plan (CAP) for NI. This CAP shall detail the policies, proposals, and actions for the first carbon budget period in line with the CCA (NI) requirements and support the transition to net zero. For this first CAP, my department and its ALBs have developed priority actions for the ALBs to take forward and help deliver carbon emission reductions. The actions are focussed on the efficient use of assets, resources and reducing resource demand.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to reintroduce the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: A bid for funding for a replacement scheme was submitted to the Department of Finance during this year’s budget setting process. The Reimbursement Scheme bid of £31million was part of a wider bid for £135million of additional funding to tackle waiting lists. It was intended that a new reimbursement scheme could have treated over 4,000 patients this financial year. This was in addition to a separate bid of £53million, within the wider £135million, specifically to address some 4,400 patients waiting over 2 years for orthopaedic procedures. Unfortunately, under the 2024/25 Budget, no additional funding has been provided for tackling our waiting lists. Instead of now being able to invest in such waiting list initiatives, my Department’s budget allocation for 2024/25 represents a 2.3% reduction in funding compared to our expenditure in 2023/24. This is a deeply regrettable and concerning position. As such, there is insufficient funding at present to reinstate a cross-border reimbursement scheme. The financial pressures across the Health and Social Care (HSC) system are certainly severe and the 2024/25 budget outcome falls far short of what is needed to tackle the unacceptable waiting lists. Therefore, my Department’s focus is on supporting those on suspect cancer and time critical waiting lists.While the £121m received in the June 2024/25 monitoring round makes some contribution to reducing the deficit position facing the HSC system, a significant shortfall still remains. As some £75m of this had already been factored into our 2024-25 financial plan, the remaining £46m will reduce the HSC Trust funding shortfall to £143m with a total gap of £293m. The financial position therefore remains very challenging and work continues across the HSC system to deliver the maximum possible savings and efficiency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on planned works for Templepatrick village, South Antrim, to alleviate the impacts of flooding.
answer_text: My officials have investigated flooding on the A57 Ballyclare Road and A6 Antrim Road, Templepatrick and discovered a potentially undersized pipe which crosses underneath the Ballyclare Road carriageway.  This pipe is likely to be a contributing factor of carriageway flooding and further detailed investigations of the drainage regime will be carried out in due course.Works previously undertaken to clear out the open drainage channel, which runs parallel to the Antrim Road carriageway, should however provide relief in the interim.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on planned works for Glenavy town, South Antrim, to alleviate the impacts of flooding.
answer_text: My Department are progressing a flood alleviation scheme in Glenavy which aims to reduce the impacts of flooding within the Chestnut Glen area.  The design is now in progress with anticipated construction commencing within the 24/25 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the recent steps his Department has taken to help ensure untreated sewage is not discharged into rivers in South Antrim.
answer_text: My Department’s role is to set policy and provide funding to NI Water in the form of a subsidy, in lieu of domestic water charges. In terms of funding for 2024-25, I have provided NI Water with a total budget allocation of almost £0.5 billion, representing just under 40% of the total non-ring-fenced budget for my Department. This reflects the central importance of water and sewerage services to our society.NI Water has advised that during the PC21 period (2021/22 - 2026/27), proposed investment of almost £12.2m is planned for wastewater infrastructure in South Antrim, to help reduce the risk of untreated sewage entering rivers in South Antrim. A breakdown of this proposed investment is set out in the table below. All future investment is subject to the availability of funding and  statutory approvals being in place. ProjectPC21 Estimated Investment (£’000)Glenavy Road Crumlin wastewater pumping station (WwPS) site investigation100Islandreagh WwPS upgrade4Dunadry WwPS upgrade 2,300Neillsbrook WwPS upgrade appraisal (complete)1,100Randalstown terminal pumping station (S01425) - screen under capacity5240,242 Lisnevenagh Road, Antrim - sewer study37The Grange wastewater treatment works (WwTW) - catchment sewerage investigations1,100Cranfield New WwTW - capacity relief project35Antrim Drainage Area - Glenavy Road WwPS, Crumlin2,700Antrim Drainage Area Plan - unsatisfactory intermittent discharge (Phase 1)200Whitehouse Drainage Area - Sandyknowes WwPS *3,000Whitehouse Drainage Area - Waverly combined sewer overflow *1,600Total Proposed Investment in PC2112,181* Investment spread across other constituencies

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will publish the new Housing Supply Strategy.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the current position in relation to a ban on the importation of (i) dogs and puppies with mutilations, including cropped ears and docked tails; and (ii) cats and kittens that have been declawed.
answer_text: There are no laws prohibiting the import into Northern Ireland of dogs and puppies with mutilations, including cropped ears and docked tails, or cats and kittens that have been declawed. In April 2024, UK Government Ministers had written to me to ask if I would support the extension of the Private Members Bill “Animal Welfare (Import of Dogs, Cats and Ferrets) Bill” to Northern Ireland, so that import of cats and dogs with mutilations could be prohibited here, as well as in Great Britain. I indicated that I was amenable but noted that the dissolution announcement meant that the Bill would no longer progress in that mandate. I am conscious that the Irish Government has already banned the import of dogs with cropped ears and that the European Commission has published a draft regulation which would outlaw mutilations to cats and dogs. Therefore, if there is an opportunity to work with the UK Government on new provisions in this area to further protect cats and dogs, I would be keen to explore this matter once again.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the benefits of increasing green space in Northern Ireland’s school estate.
answer_text: The Department considers external site areas to be of critical importance for a school, particularly in the education of children in an urban setting. Outside space is seen as an integral part of the school and an extension of the classroom and provides the opportunity for a school to satisfy the Department’s policy on Biodiversity.Currently there are no plans to increase green space provision in Northern Ireland’s school estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department plans to increase green space in Northern Ireland’s school estate.
answer_text: The site size required for a school is detailed in the Department’s Building Handbooks.  Currently there are no plans to change optimal site sizes to increase green space provision in Northern Ireland’s school estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the timescale of the June Monitoring Round.
answer_text: The Executive agreed the June Monitoring round on 01 July 2024, and I made a statement to the Assembly on the same day. A copy of my statement can be viewed on the Department’s website at https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/ministerial-statement-2023-24-provisional-outturn-and-2024-25-june-monitoring

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has plans to mark Clean Air Day on 20 June 2024.
answer_text: My Department has no specific plans to mark Clean Air day however we are supporting the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, who are responsible for the clean air strategy for NI, in their work in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department is implementing any measures to prevent food waste in prisons.
answer_text: NIPS encourages all prisoners and kitchen staff to separate food and recyclable waste streams at all prison establishments. Waste streams mixed with food are classed as general waste and cannot be recycled. Separated food waste is stored at collection centres on each site and transferred to an Anaerobic Digester Plant by a waste collection contractor, which is then used to generate heat and electricity. NIPS receive a monthly report identifying amounts of food waste collected.The catering departments actively seek the guidance of recognised dieticians and surveys are carried out annually and any relevant issues or suggestions raised are taken into account to help minimise waste.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how much funding will be allocated to the action plan being developed by the Green Energy Skills Industry Reference Group.
answer_text: Question 10 - AQO 649/22-27The Green Energy Skills Industry Reference Group, now known as the Green Skills Delivery Group, is an industry led sub-group of the NI Skills Council tasked with the development of a Green Skills Action Plan. My department will look to support the delivery of the action plan through its existing programmes as well as the new Skills Fund that I have recently established as part of the 2024-25 budget process.Green skills are critical to the delivery of my economic vision and I look forward to considering how best to support the Action Plan when it is submitted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on access to plant protection products.
answer_text: My officials, along with Defra officials and the Health and Safety Executive (HSE) in GB, continue to monitor plant protection product (PPP) availability and regulatory divergence between GB and NI.  Whilst we will endeavour to address the impacts faced by PPP users through domestic solutions where possible, it must be recognised that all actions must be taken according to the legislative framework within which we work.Where the EU and GB conditions of PPP renewal allow, and subject to the authorisation holder requesting the inclusion of NI within the scope of the authorisation, HSE continues to issue PPP authorisations common to both GB and NI enabling NI growers access to the same products as GB growers.I recognise that we are witnessing increasing commercial withdrawals of PPP authorisations and decisions not to bring new PPPs to the market in NI. My officials have been engaging with representatives of the crop protection industry and users of PPPs to discuss availability issues. I have asked officials to continue this engagement to help raise awareness of the authorisation application types, available for the NI market, as alternatives to standard applications, such as mutual recognition and parallel trade permits. An advice page has also been prepared which will be published on the DAERA website in the coming weeks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is seeking to improve space for bicycles on public transport.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:“Railway The current Translink bicycle policy was drawn up in conjunction with SUSTRANS and is similar to other Train Operating Companies in the UK and Ireland. Bicycles are carried free of charge on all rail services after 0930 hours Monday to Friday. Up to 4 full sized bicycles can be carried at any one time within the designated bicycle area.  Space is allocated on a first come first served basis.  There are no travel restrictions nor limit to the number of ‘folding bicycles’ carried on trains provided they are completely folded down.  Tandems, and tricycles are not permitted on any train. It should be noted that during periods of disruption where a bus substitution is required, bicycles will not be carried.  The current policy is designed to maximise the number of commuters on rail services during busy times, while affording cyclists the opportunity to take their bikes on trains outside of the peak flows. Changing the policy would have a significant impact on overcrowding at peak times, discouraging rail usage and would have a negative impact on revenues. This approach is complimented by improved cycle storage facilities at the vast majority of stations across the network and we encourage cyclists to use our secure bike compounds. Further details of the scheme can be found on the Translink website https://www.translink.co.uk/bike-fobs Bus Similar to the above, our policy for bus services is aimed at maximising space for commuters and luggage in the morning and providing priority to wheelchair users on all occasions.   When bicycles are carried as passengers’ luggage, they will be carried free of charge provided that suitable space is available and that they do not cause obstruction. Unfolded bicycles are not permitted on buses prior to 0930 Monday to Friday, given the need for commuter space. On Metro and Glider services, unfolded bicycles are not permitted at any time, but folded bicycles will be carried where there is sufficient space and provided they do not cause obstruction.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what initiatives are being implemented to support sustainable urban development.
answer_text: Furthering sustainable development is at the heart of the planning system and my Department’s Strategic Planning Policy Statement (the SPPS) which sets out regional strategic planning policy on a range of important matters, including urban development. The SPPS supports furthering sustainable development in the long term public interest and requires the integration and balancing of complex social, economic, and environmental factors by planning authorities when plan-making and decision-taking. NI Water, which is sponsored by my Department, is committed to working with developers to consider ways of accommodating sustainable urban development wherever possible. The Pre-Development Enquiry (PDE) process has recently been introduced as a measure to facilitate sustainable development. In addition, my Department is considering proposals for a legislative Bill which will include the provision of powers to issue future guidance or regulations on the approval and maintenance of sustainable drainage systems (SuDs).My Department is also leading on the delivery of the Living With Water in Belfast Plan which aims to help reduce flooding, enhance the environment, and facilitate economic growth in the city by investing in infrastructure to address capacity constraints in sewerage and wastewater treatment. The Plan promotes a more sustainable approach to water management, along with the benefits of blue / green infrastructure.  A Living With Water in Derry Plan, similar to the Belfast Plan is being developed and will be subject to a public consultation in due course

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures his Department is taking to reduce carbon emissions from the transportation sector.
answer_text: My Department is undertaking a range of actions that will support the transition to net zero in the transport sector. I recently advised Ministerial colleagues of my intention to bring forward legislation relating to the Zero Emission Vehicle Mandate / Vehicle Emissions Trading Schemes Order 2023, for the Assembly’s approval. These schemes will set sales targets for vehicle manufacturers in relation to the percentage of new zero emission vehicles they will have to sell.  In support of this, my Department is taking steps to enhance the electric vehicle charge point infrastructure through amendments to the planning system and providing matched funding to support Councils in accessing funding for On-Street Residential charging infrastructure. However, I acknowledge that we will require more than a transition to zero-emission and low-emission vehicles; managing transport demand and embedding behavioural change will also be of vital importance in reducing carbon emissions.Therefore, my Department’s approach to decarbonisation will focus on measures to reduce vehicle kilometres travelled and encourage walking, wheeling and the use of public transport. These measures will be supported by a number of overarching enabling actions such as the development of a Transport Strategy and a suite of Transport Plans. A bespoke Transport Emission Model has also been developed that will ensure robust monitoring of policy progression and assessment of the enabling actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department is supporting the development of green infrastructure, such as urban green spaces.
answer_text: My Department’s Regional Development Strategy (RDS) 2035 recognises the need to protect and encourage green infrastructure, such as parks, green spaces, and street trees within urban areas. The RDS also supports urban renaissance and seeks to ensure that environmental quality in urban areas is improved and maintained, particularly with adequate provision of green infrastructure.The Strategic Planning Policy Statement (the SPPS) which sets out my Department’s regional strategic planning policy on a range of matters also recognises the important role of the planning system and green infrastructure in helping to mitigate and adapt to climate change by, inter alia, working with natural environmental processes, for example through promoting the development of green infrastructure and the use of sustainable drainage systems (SuDs) to reduce flood risk and improve water quality.Given my Department’s broad functions and responsibilities in relation to water, transport and planning, I am committed to ensuring that it continues to play an appropriate supporting role in the provision of blue and green infrastructure, recognising the present resource challenges faced.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of installing electric vehicle charging infrastructure.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle (EV) charge points is a commercially led operation. Responsibility for the maintenance and installation of those charge points rests solely with the charge point operators (CPOs). In this commercially led market, my officials continue to engage with EV owners and provide advice to CPOs on operational matters. Latest statistics show there are 564 publicly available EV charge points in the North. This represents a growth of 45% in the total number of available EV charge points since April 2023. Individual charge point operators are best placed to advise you of their plans for future charge point installations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department is addressing the environmental impact of infrastructure projects, such as reducing construction waste.
answer_text: My Department’s approach to addressing  the environmental impact of our work is set out is our publication ‘Protecting, Enhancing and Restoring Natural Places: An Environmental Mission Statement for Infrastructure Development and Management’.The approach set out in this document along with the design standards, procurement methods and the provisions in legislation, seek to minimise the potential environmental impacts of infrastructure projects and ensure that there is effective management of waste during the construction phase of our works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what measures are being considered to meet social housing targets in rural areas.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, further to the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, for an update on Ministerial direction and control over disease awareness, preparedness and control in relation to animal and plant health.
answer_text: The Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 came into force on 12 April 2024. There are currently 19 pieces of EU legislation where the Secretary of State has not exercised his discretion not to exercise direction and control, that is, they are now subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State. A list of these has been deposited in the Assembly Library and can be accessed via the link below.https://www.niassembly.gov.uk/globalassets/documents/raise/deposited-papers/2024/dp1767.pdf From 17 May 2024, the Defra Secretary of state has exercised discretion in the areas of disease awareness, disease preparedness and disease control in relation to animal and plant health and these matters are now under my ministerial responsibility.This discretion should be understood in the context of the Secretary of State’s criteria for retaining direction and control, which includes:•	operational arrangements at Northern Ireland ports and airports concerning the movement of goods between GB and NI;•	policy developments and legislation that would impact movements between Great Britain and Northern Ireland; and•	new EU laws which replace regulations under Annex II of the Windsor Framework.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department's budget for 2024-25 will impact funding for community transport agencies.
answer_text: I recognise and value the contribution of all organisations that provide community transport services, and the impact that they have on some of the most vulnerable groups in society.I have made the decision to provide funding to community transport for 2024/25 at the total levels awarded for 2023/24. This is subject to the planned Equality Impact Assessment consultation on the budget and so this allocation is indicative at this stage. I do, however, expect to be able to confirm opening budget allocations, once I have taken final decisions following consideration of responses to the Equality Impact Assessment after an initial four-week period. My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department is supporting local cultural initiatives in South Antrim.
answer_text: My Department supports local cultural initiatives in South Antrim through the provision of funding and support for cultural programmes. This is normally distributed via my Department’s Arms Lengths Bodies such as the Arts Council, the Language Bodies, Museums, Libraries, and also in partnerships with, for example, local councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to increase the school uniform grant for 2024-25.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether work has commenced on the proposed Strategic Infrastructure Plan.
answer_text: The publication of a comprehensive and costed long-term infrastructure plan was a condition of the financial package that accompanied the restoration of the Executive. This will sit alongside the Sustainability Plan which also formed part of the financial package.Following engagement between officials in my department and counterparts in Treasury, more detail on the structure and contents of both Plans were included within the interim Fiscal Framework that was agreed on the 21 May. This also set a timescale for delivery of the documents.I have established a Budget Sustainability team within my Department to lead on the development of this work and initial discussions have taken place with the Strategic Investment Board to understand how the work that has been carried out to date by the body in developing the draft Investment Strategy can be used to inform the production of a Strategic Infrastructure Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to increase the carrier bag levy from 25 pence.
answer_text: My department introduced new carrier bag levy legislation in April 2022. It increased the carrier bag levy charge from 5 pence to 25 pence and extended the scope to include all new carrier bags with a retail price of five pounds or less. The primary objective remains to protect our environment by influencing customer behaviour to promote a significant reduction in carrier bag usage. Annual validated statistics are published each August, with complete figures based on the updated regulation available for the 2022/23 reporting year only.  More time is needed for comparative figures to be produced, which will help inform decisions around any future changes to the legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of electric vehicle charge points that will be available at the new Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: The new Belfast Grand Central Station will have 15 charging points when it is fully completed in Autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how his Department monitors ventilation levels in school buildings.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to questions AQW 12110/22-27 & AQW 12197/22-27, addressed collectively.   The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 7 June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to carry out a review of ventilation requirements in school buildings.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to questions AQW 12110/22-27 & AQW 12197/22-27, addressed collectively.   The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 7 June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider putting training in place for school staff on the creation of infection-resilient classrooms to improve absent rates in schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority provides schools with health and safety guidance to ensure school staff are able to create a safe and healthy classroom environment. Any efforts to improve regular pupil attendance at school are welcome. I am conscious of the number of challenges that are adversely affecting pupil attendance currently and my officials are reviewing and refreshing the existing attendance strategy in order to take account of these circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will introduce the provision of one CO2 monitor per classroom, similar to what is in place in England.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 12111/22-27.   The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 31 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department's actions to promote social inclusion and integration for those who are resettling in Northern Ireland from A8 countries.
answer_text: My Department does not have any schemes in place to help people from A8 countries settle in the UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of residential properties owned by his Department in South Antrim that are currently unoccupied or derelict.
answer_text: The Department of Health does not own any residential properties in the South Antrim constituency area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when his Department intends to use the remaining budget in the School Ventilation Fund.
answer_text: The Department of Education provided £2m earmarked capital funding in the 2021/22 financial year for CO2 monitors and ventilation needs across the schools’ estate, in response to the Covid-19 pandemic.This £2m allocation was spent in full, providing ventilation schemes, CO2 monitors and air filtration units across the schools’ estate.Additional funding has since been provided, as needed, for the provision of CO2 monitors and air filtration units.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how he is addressing the challenges of ageing infrastructure, such as bridges and public buildings, to ensure safety and functionality for the public.
answer_text: My Department has well established processes that apply to the design, construction, inspection and maintenance of infrastructure assets, such as bridges, to enable public safety risks to be managed. Staff in my Department are working hard to deliver these however their ability to do so is dependent on the resource allocated to my Department. You are aware that Departments have been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. As the value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to secure appropriate budgets so that the risk to the public is managed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department’s plans for upgrading the road network in South Antrim in the next five years.
answer_text: Upgrades to the road network are delivered through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme, the Local Transport and Safety Measures Programme, and the Active Travel Programme. My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. Available investment has not kept pace with the demand for upgrades to the network and consequently all potential schemes are assessed and prioritised, with only the highest priority considered for inclusion in the works programmes, the extent of which will depend on budget allocations. Work is currently ongoing to develop my Department’s capital work programmes for 2024/25. My Department is finalising its funding allocations, following confirmation of the 2024/25 budget settlement. Details of the planned works programmes will then be completed and included within the Department’s Annual Report to the local councils. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department is addressing the issue of housing and infrastructure development in urban centres, whilst preserving green spaces.
answer_text: The planning system plays an important enabling role in facilitating the delivery of housing. My Department’s Strategic Planning Policy Statement (the SPPS), requires councils, in taking forward their local development plans (LDPs), to facilitate an adequate and available supply of quality housing to meet the needs of everyone and promote more sustainable housing development within existing urban areas. This approach supports the need to maximise the use of existing infrastructure and services, and the creation of more balanced sustainable communities.The SPPS also recognises the benefits of open space (including green space). The SPPS’s key regional strategic objectives include, safeguarding existing open space; and ensuring that areas of open space are provided as an integral part of new residential development. The SPPS includes a presumption against the loss of open space to competing land uses.Zonings for future needs in LDPs should take into account, inter alia, accessibility to and from existing and proposed housing areas; the contribution that open space can make to the quality of the environment, sense of place and community life; the importance of protecting linear open spaces such as pedestrian and cycle routes, community greenways, former railway lines and river and canal corridors; making adequate provision of green and blue infrastructure; and, identifying and designating areas of open space which perform a strategic function, such as landscape wedges in urban areas.Councils are responsible for the determination of planning applications for housing development. In plan-making and decision taking councils will take account of advice from statutory consultees such as DfI Roads and Rivers and NI Water.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps her Department is taking to promote tourism in the South Antrim constituency.
answer_text: Tourism NI (TNI) has regularly promoted the South Antrim Area through its seasonal marketing campaigns which are targeted to specific market segments based on extensive research. One of the most recent pieces of promotional activity was the launch of a major Spring advertising campaign on 22 January 2024 which ran until 31 March.  The campaign featured TV, radio, press, press supplements, outdoor, social and digital advertising, all underpinned by a comprehensive PR strategy. South Antrim featured specifically in Causeway Coastal Route press advertorials as well as a 24-page supplement and bespoke radio stings.Planning is in train for the next campaign due to take place in autumn, and with regional balance one of my key priorities, TNI will ensure that regions are featured.On the DiscoverNorthernIreland.com website, TNI continually promotes County Antrim related content, with a dedicated landing page, of which Antrim Castle Gardens has featured as the lead image since the Spring’24 campaign. In total there are 215 tourism products in the South Antrim area featured on Discovernorthernireland.com. TNI also works with its partner Tourism Ireland to host international media, with South Antrim featuring regularly in many media itineraries, including the influencer Jordan Ezra of the Daily Telegraph who has more than 200K followers. Influencers Tom Trotter and Rhiannon Bailey also recently stayed at the Rabbit Hotel as part of their media itinerary. TNI has an ongoing programme of PR activity North and South, with tourism businesses and visitor experiences from South Antrim regularly promoted in PR content, media trips, and Feature profiling. There is a media familiarisation trip to the South with a journalist from the Irish Sun planned to the area in July 2024.TNI also works closely with the tourism industry in South Antrim and across the North to support the development of engaging and immersive tourism experiences.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail which historic environment sites purchase peat-based compost.
answer_text: Peat based compost is not purchased for use at State Care Monuments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail which local councils purchase peat-based compost.
answer_text: My Department does not hold any information relating to those councils that purchase peat based compost. Procurement by councils of products such as peat-based compost is an operational matter for District Councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the outcomes from the draft Equality Impact Assessment on Belfast Health and Social Care Trust's implementation plan to close Muckamore Abbey Hospital will be available to the public.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s consultation on the draft Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) of their implementation plan to close Muckamore Abbey Hospital closed on 30 April 2024.The Belfast Trust have advised me they are considering the responses received, including the feedback from a number of engagement sessions held as part of the consultation.  Once this work is complete, the Trust are planning to present a draft outcome report for approval at their Trust Board meeting on 12 June 2024.  Subject to approval, the Trust will then publish the report.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it will consider providing translation services to Members to assist them in carrying out their constituency duties.
answer_text: As you will be aware, the Assembly Commission has made very significant investments in the translation and interpretation of plenary business and committee business (on request and subject to demand) in both Irish and Sign Language. In addition, the Assembly Commission has translated information on the Assembly into multiple other languages. The Assembly Commission has also translated the Primary and 8-10 sections of the Assembly’s Education Service website into Irish.However, the Assembly Commission has no role in the provision of translation services to Members to assist them in carrying out their constituency duties. Instead, the Assembly Commission makes funding available to Members from their Constituency Office Operating Expenses (COOE) to cover the expenditure incurred in providing constituency services, including translation services. The Assembly Commission will shortly be considering amending the level of COOE funding in order to provide greater support for Members in undertaking their constituency duties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has reassessed the potential for the provision of a cycle route on Dublin Road, Antrim.
answer_text: In July 2021 my Department carried out a preliminary assessment of a proposal to upgrade the existing footway at Dublin Road, Antrim south of Balloo Manor.  Although this proposal did not score highly enough to warrant a more detailed assessment at the time, my Department will be further exploring the potential for provision of a cycle route on Dublin Road, in particular between Chapel Roundabout and Belmont Road. My Department is also developing an Active Travel Delivery Plan that includes a full update of our design guidance for active travel and active travel network plans for the largest towns and cities in the North, including Antrim. We are aiming to consult on draft proposals for the Active Travel Delivery Plan later this year. Once adopted, the plan will provide our teams with a firm basis for the prioritisation of active travel investment within our towns and cities for the next ten years and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the poverty statistics in the South Antrim constituency in the last five years; and (ii) what his Department is doing to reduce these figures.
answer_text: Official measures of absolute and relative poverty for NI are derived from the Family Resources Survey (FRS). Both measures can be presented on a before and after housing costs basis. Due to the uncertainty around estimates at lower levels, the Department does not present results for poverty at Parliamentary Constituency level.I am focused on working with my Executive colleagues, and meeting with sectoral delivery partners and key stakeholders, to develop a sustainable, deliverable Anti-Poverty Strategy which addresses the issues that make a tangible difference to people’s lives.I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in developing the Strategy and I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty in all its forms right across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what further active travel initiatives are being considered for the South Antrim constituency to reduce commuter travel time to Belfast city centre.
answer_text: The Department is currently developing an Active Travel Network Delivery Plan for the North, which includes a full update of our design guidance and proposed active travel network plans for our largest towns and cities. We plan to consult on draft proposals for the Active Travel Network Plan later in 2024, subject to successful completion of a Strategic Environmental Assessment. When complete, the plan will complement the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan and the Strategic Plan for Greenways to provide our teams with a firm basis for the future prioritisation of active travel infrastructure investment and delivery.In the interim, I would also refer the Member to the current council reports that provide details of schemes in delivery- https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/list-council-reports-202324

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline his plans for animal welfare policy in the 2022-2027 mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10351/22-27, whether he intends to introduce an out-of-hours GP service at Antrim Area Hospital.
answer_text: In October 2020, my Department published the No More Silos Action Plan which set out 10 key actions to ensure that urgent and emergency care services across primary and secondary care can be maintained and improved in an environment that is safe for patients and for staff. The work that commenced under No More Silos is now being taken forward as part of the implementation of the Urgent and Emergency Care Review which published in March 2022.The implementation plan resulting from this review will progress work in relation to the integration of GP Out of Hours services. The plan, which will consider the issues raised during the consultation, will be taken forward with stakeholders to ensure that local and professional issues are considered as part of planning and implementation.Currently my Department commissions Dalriada Urgent Care (DUC) to provide GP Out of Hours (OOHs) services across the Northern area.  DUC have an established GP OOHs base at Antrim Area Hospital.  The strategic direction, as noted above, is the integration of GP OOHs, with has a number of potential benefits as outlined in my response to you on 24 April 2024 in relation to AQW 10351/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department is implementing any measures to prevent food waste in schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) Catering Service has a number of successful initiatives in place to prevent food waste which include:An online advance meal booking system which operates across many primary schools.  This enables parents to book menu items in advance which is then used to inform the number of each item needing to be prepared.The introduction of harmonised nursery and primary school menus from 2022 across Northern Ireland.The maintenance of food waste records within the four-week menu cycle in both primary and post-primary schools. This information allows the EA to identify more popular food items by comparing items prepared to items served.  Use of past data on food sales in both primary and post-primary school canteens to more accurately anticipate demand for each product in order to inform meal production and reduce the quantity of food waste. The establishment of the EA Catering Menu Masters Group consisting of EA catering managers from across primary and post-primary settings who are  based in school meal kitchens who hold the knowledge of their customer base and are able to design menus 12 months in advance.In addition, the EA Education Information Solutions Programme is working to make available advance online booking systems to all primary schools. This will further reduce an already low percentage of food waste in both primary and post-primary settings. The EA has a contract in place with a waste collection business to manage food waste in schools where it is responsible for the catering.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is implementing any measures to prevent food waste in hospitals.
answer_text: All Trusts aim to minimise food wastage while also acknowledging it can never be completely eliminated.  Food wastage can occur through over ordering, over production or plate wastage. Each Trust has a range of measures in place to minimise the issue including:Meals are developed in conjunction with dieticians to be nutritionally balanced. Menu choices, portion sizes and specialist diet requirements across the many patient groups are continuously reviewed. Special dietary requirements are discussed with the patient when they order their meals.Patients are screened for malnutrition and may be offered support to eat or drink at ward level to ensure they are able to eat the meals provided. Patient menus often include the option for smaller portions to encourage patients with reduced appetites to choose a more suitable portion size for their needs e.g. Lighter Lunch pilot (Altnagelvin Hospital)Patient meals are ordered as close to service time as possible.Electronic patient meal ordering is being introduced in a number of Trust areas which also allows patients to visualise the meal and inform choices.Catering staff are trained on portion sizing and food stocks are monitored with date checks throughout the week to highlight any food that may be approaching its best before date. Catering Services undertake daily monitoring of food waste and adjustments are made to food production and/or menus depending on waste quantities. BHSCT has a contract in place, as part of a regional framework agreement for the collection and recovery of food waste.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has had any engagement with the retail sector on achieving a net zero future.
answer_text: My department has worked in partnership with Invest NI to deliver a new scaled phase of its Energy & Resource Efficiency (ERE) Programme for Business. The Programme includes a new Energy Efficiency Capital Grant Scheme (EECG) which is open to applications from a wide range of businesses across the North. Businesses across a range of sectors from retail to advanced manufacturing to hospitality will be eligible for the grant and enable them to invest in technologies to reduce energy consumption and carbon emissions. It will also provide businesses the opportunity to access funding that will help them transition to more environmentally sustainable practices.  In addition, decarbonisation is a core priority of my Economic Vision and as such, is a frequent topic of discussion at all my engagements, including those with the retail sector.  Details of all my external meetings will soon be published on the Open Data Portal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has plans to introduce or increase climate change and sustainability education in the curriculum for primary schools and post-primary schools.
answer_text: In the Northern Ireland Curriculum, climate change and sustainable development are topics covered within the curriculum at every level. At primary it is covered within the ‘World Around Us’ area of learning and at key stage 3, core learning in every subject must be taught in a range of contexts including education for sustainable development.The curriculum at key stage 4 encourages teachers to promote relevance of learning to life and work (including sustainability).Teachers can also contribute to education for sustainable development by providing frequent opportunities, within and beyond the classroom, to help young people develop dispositions and values such as curiosity, concern, respect, tolerance and personal responsibility in relation to a range of environmental and global issues.The Department of Education have also commissioned CCEA to greater embed sustainability and climate change education into the curriculum. This will involve the development of a progression framework and resource hub to draw together up-to-date resources and sources of support for teachers, pupils and young people in green growth and climate change knowledge, skills and understanding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the publication of an Environment Improvement Plan.
answer_text: By its nature, the Environmental Improvement Plan requires cross-departmental commitment and involvement and therefore requires Executive agreement.  Once this has been secured, the plan will be published.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline how budget allocations reflect the Executive's priority to protect and better manage Lough Neagh.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the plans to (i) upgrade existing bus shelters; and (ii) install new bus shelters in the South Antrim constituency.
answer_text: Translink has submitted a business case to my Department to replace/refurbish existing bus shelters as well as installing a number of new bus shelters across the public transport network. However, my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans he has to introduce an equine strategy.
answer_text: Following the commissioning of consultants by the Department in 2018 to review the equine industry in Northern Ireland (NI), a report was published in June 2019, which made a number of recommendations and set out themes for further consideration by government and wider industry.A cross-governmental steering group was set up in early 2020 to explore what action might be taken.  This included a focus on the development of an Equine Strategy for NI. The steering group produced a draft vision statement for a future Equine Strategy for consideration by returning ministers and was involved in progressing a number of actions linked to the proposed Strategy. My Department’s College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) completed an equine training and skills gap analysis to ascertain the current educational provision for those working or wanting to work in the equine industry and identify the training gaps. The Department of Health completed a number of equine assisted therapy and learning (EATL) pilots with more planned, subject to funding availability.Additionally, Outdoor Recreation NI was commissioned to scope out the existing riding trails in the pilot area of the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council and conduct a gap analysis which resulted in a report on the matter.Northern Ireland Departments are, however, currently working to deliver statutory obligations within an extremely difficult public expenditure environment.  Therefore, given these resource pressures, it is not possible to progress additional work on an equine strategy in the immediate future.The matter will however remain under review as resources may permit in time ahead.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of Parking Charge Notices issued for pavement parking in the Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council area in the last five years.
answer_text: The Footways (Prohibition of Waiting) Order (Northern Ireland) 2023 came into operation on the 3rd November 2023. To enable the enforcement of these new restrictions, several new contravention codes were created as follows:Code 34P - Parked on a footway adjacent to a Bus Lane.Code 47F - Parked on a footway adjacent to a Bus Stop / Stand.Code 48F - Parked on a footway adjacent to a Mandatory School Keep Clear.Code 49F - Parked on a footway adjacent to a Mandatory Cycle Lane  The number of PCNs issued in the Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council against these new contraventions since they were introduced in November 2023 are detailed in the table below. Contravention CodeTotal number of PCNs (3rd Nov. 23 - 18th Apr. 24)Code 34P121Code 47F54Code 48F0Code 49F4However, there are other contravention codes that have existed prior to November 2023 that relate to footway parking and the number of PCNs issued for these contraventions in the Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council between 19th April 2021 and 18th April 2024 are shown in the table below. Please note, as per the Department’s Retention and Disposal schedule, older PCN data is not retained. Contravention CodeTotal number of PCNs 46F - Parked on a footway adjacent to a clearway249299F - Parked on a footway adjacent to a controlled area at Zebra, Pelican, and Puffin crossings. 398Finally, it is not possible to detail the total number of PCNs issued to vehicles parked on a footway as parking restrictions marked on the road, for example yellow lines, also apply to the footway or verge. As PCNs do not record the location of the vehicle, it is not possible to distinguish between those PCNs issued to vehicles parked on the footway and those parked on the road. Parking on a footway causing an obstruction is not a decriminalised offence and responsibility for this remains with the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any financial support is available for schools who wish to update and repair their school playground facilities.
answer_text: Whilst minor repairs to playground areas and playground equipment is a school responsibility, if a playground surface has deteriorated to the extent that it is presenting health and safety concerns, a school can submit an unavoidable minor work application to my Department or the Education Authority for repair.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the plans on how hospitals in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust plan to meet their requirements as set out in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 places duties on Departments. The Northern Health and Social Care Trust as an Arms- length body of the Department of Health is supporting the duties on the Department by participating in the regional Sustainability Action Group to take forward work on the Department of Health’s initial climate priority actions. The Trust has also set up a Trust led Sustainability Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the number of pothole repairs that are outstanding in the Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council area.
answer_text: Road surface defects, including potholes are managed through the Department’s Maintenance Management System which records the total number of defects and the number outstanding for each Section Office area.The data is regularly published through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/surface-defects.Official Statistics on the condition of our roads are also now published through the Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics.  The 2022-23 publication (the first such publication) can be found at https://datavis.nisra.gov.uk/infrastructure/northern-ireland-road-network-and-condition-statistics-2223.html.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any environmental grants are available to schools who wish to provide eco-schemes and projects.
answer_text: There are no environmental grant opportunities currently available within my Department to schools which wish to provide eco-schemes and projects.  Any future funding opportunities will be advertised on the DAERA Grants and Funding Hub at: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/grants-and-funding
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the merits of introducing an out-of-hours GP service at Antrim Area Hospital.
answer_text: Co-locating GP Out of Hours services with a major Emergency Department offers a number of strategic and clinical advantages:1. Workforce resilience: the number of medical personnel present is significantly greater. A challenge for GP Out of Hours (GP OOH) services has been a the relatively small number of medical personnel available at any single site, with a risk that if a GP has to attend a patient's home, the GP OOH facility may be left unattended for a period. Cross cover is possible through co-location. 2. Increasing disease complexity is a feature of modern medical practice and feedback from GP representatives indicates that they are increasingly reliant on specialist advice as a result of multimorbidity amongst the older population, advances in diagnostic technology and pharmacology. Co-location benefits patients by making this specialist advice readily available.3. Co-location offers the opportunity for patients to report their symptoms once only whether their need is for GP advice or GP and specialist input. 4. Co-location of GP OOH services with Emergency Departments, will allow patients who require admission to hospital to avoid the discomfort of additional travel and further waiting time.5. The Urgent and Emergency Care Review (REVIEW OF URGENT AND EMERGENCY CARE SERVICES IN NORTHERN IRELAND (health-ni.gov.uk)) has proposed the establishment of a new integrated urgent and emergency care model, across all provider organisations. It is envisaged that service users will be able to access care through a single telephone number. This development will enable triage of patient symptoms and referral, if required, to the correct medical service (GP or specialist) without having to attend a GP followed by a specialist consultation at a separate site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what action her Department is taking to ensure that shared office spaces in NICS buildings have adequate air ventilation.
answer_text: My Department uses a Property Management Framework to ensure that all mechanical ventilation installations within the NICS office estate are appropriately maintained to the industry standard for building maintenance (SFG20). Advice on checking air quality using CO2 detectors has been provided to building premises teams and any concerns arising regarding air quality can be raised with DoF Properties Division for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the importance of agritourism to the overall economy.
answer_text: My assessment as Minister for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs is that agritourism is an area with the potential for growth in Northern Ireland. This can in turn support a more resilient primary agriculture sector by creating employment opportunities and additional household income for farm families whilst helping to keep many of the traditional family farming practices we are proud of in Northern Ireland alive.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the planned works for Ballyclare town to alleviate the impacts of flooding.
answer_text: To alleviate the risk of flooding from watercourse culvert screen blockage, my Department has planned work to install a remote monitoring system to a culvert screen adjacent to Green Road, Ballyclare and will also continue to undertake cyclical inspection and, as necessary, maintenance works on any designated watercourses and their associated infrastructure. In addition, my Department will continue to carry out routine cleaning of gullies in and around Ballyclare town centre.   The management of  water and wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the planned works for Antrim town to alleviate the impacts of flooding.
answer_text: To reduce the risk of flooding from rivers, my Department’s Rivers Directorate plans to continue its inspection and maintenance regime in relation to designated watercourses and their associated infrastructure. Flooding in Antrim Town will also be examined as part of a wider ‘Antrim Significant Flood Risk Area’ study, which is already on my Department’s Rivers Directorate 10-year capital works programme.  My Department’s Roads Division will continue to carry out routine cleaning and maintenance of DfI drainage infrastructure in Antrim.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the budgetary situation facing her Department in 2024-25.
answer_text: Alongside the Departments of Health and Education, my Department is facing the most significant level of budget pressures in 2024-25.    My Department has submitted Resource bids to the Department of Finance for pressures of approximately £444m in order to maintain existing services.  This includes £217m of stabilisation pressures arising from increased legal aid requirements, additional demand on courts, prisons and police services, pay and pension increases and inflationary costs.   It also includes exceptional pressures of £227m, outside the direct control of my Department, relating to legal claims arising from the PSNI data breach, holiday pay and McCloud injury to feelings.   My Department’s Capital needs are estimated at £122m in 2024-25 for inescapable and high priority projects. The support and funding of all our projects is key if we are to maintain a modern justice system, which protects the vulnerable and facilitates access to justice.  My Department is now facing a situation where:  Police numbers are at the lowest level since formation, and over 1,000, approximately 15%, below the 7,500 aspiration in New Decade, New Approach; the Prison population is now 500, 35%, higher than three years ago; and Legal aid payments are forecast to be 30% higher than the baseline budget.  There is now simply nowhere else that we can pull back on costs in order to meet our pressures, particularly as the majority of my Department’s Resource budget is truly inescapable with 69% staff costs and 30% statutory or contractually committed.  Until a budget is set for 2024-25 by the Executive, I cannot be definitive about the impact on my Department’s spending areas.  However, in the absence of sufficient additional funding being provided, I cannot currently foresee a situation where a balanced budget can be set without significant deterioration in public services and lasting damage to citizens.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has considered (i) freezing the student loan payment thresholds; and (ii) adjusting the student loan payment to match inflation.
answer_text: Prior to my taking office, the higher education student support package for academic year 2024/25 had already been determined.  There was, however, a 40% increase in the maximum student maintenance loan made available for the current academic year. I have asked my officials to update me on any work to date on a review of Higher Education Funding, as well as proposals to take it forward. I will be engaging with my officials to consider the current support available and determine whether this can be improved while taking account of the financial context for my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has considered raising the student maintenance loan payment to match inflation.
answer_text: Prior to my taking office, the higher education student support package for academic year 2024/25 had already been determined. I will be engaging with my officials to consider the support available in future years and determine whether this can be improved while taking account of the financial context for my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department carries out an inspection after roadwork repairs are completed.
answer_text: My Department seeks to ensure that approximately 5% of all defect repairs are inspected on a routine basis to ensure the correct standards have been complied with.  This ratio is considered to be proportionate to enable the quality of work and materials to be effectively monitored but the inspection frequency can also be increased if issues are identified in any particular area or with a type of work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there is a mechanism for reporting poor quality roadwork repairs.
answer_text: Members of the public can report a pothole or other road defect at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/services/report-pothole-or-other-surface-defect. The NI Direct call-centre also use the fault reporting system for submitting a fault on a customer’s behalf.Over the past number of years the system has been enhanced to provide additional reporting services, such as customer acknowledgement emails and a fault tracking function, that are designed to improve the overall customer service.The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there is a standards specification for roadwork repair.
answer_text: In relation to the criteria/standard that contractors are subject to, there is not a single specification that must be complied with.  My Department and its contractors use a variety of different materials and methods to repair defects, such as cut out patching; overlay patching; velocity patching; asphaltic concrete and hot rolled asphalt repairs. Each method and material has its own specific specification and method of application.  However, the following link provides details of the overarching standard which is applied to all openings such as those carried out by utility companies and repairs undertaken by my Department: Specification For The Reinstatement Of Openings In Roads

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of introducing traffic calming measures on Mill Road, Crumlin.
answer_text: At a recent Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council Policing and Community Safety Partnership meeting, held in Crumlin Integrated Primary School, my Department committed to undertaking a review of the traffic safety measures provided on the Mill Road in Crumlin.  This review is ongoing.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour. If all of us who are drivers do not change our attitudes towards speeding and careless driving, our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he intends to replace the removed street lighting at Town Parks North, Antrim, in the interests of public safety.
answer_text: My Department has an ongoing capital programme to replace street lighting systems which have been deemed to have reached the end of their lives.  When a replacement lighting system is installed, the area is assessed and new lighting is replaced in those areas that meet the Department’s policy for the provision of lighting.In urban areas, street lighting is provided in those adopted pathways which provide access to the front entrances of properties, or where they are identified as through routes with no reasonable alternative lit route. However, where a pathway is not adopted, as is the case at Town Parks North in Antrim, or is identified as only providing access to the rear of properties, any existing lighting is removed and will not be replaced.I understand the concerns some residents may have regarding the removal of existing lighting at Town Parks North in Antrim which had been assessed to have reached the end of its serviceable life.  However, I hope that you will appreciate that this policy ensures that our finite resources are directed at those areas where maximum benefit to the public can be derived, particularly where road or infrastructure safety is concerned, whilst also seeking to reduce future maintenance and energy costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she plans to review the personal injury discount rate in July 2024.
answer_text: The Damages (Return on Investment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 amended the Damages Act 1996 to assign the task of reviewing and setting the rate to the Government Actuary, provide a statutory methodology for calculating the rate and establish a timeframe for review of the rate. Under the legislation, a review of the rate must be started by the Government Actuary on 1 July 2024 and be completed within 90 days. In anticipation of that, my Department is presently considering whether any modifications are required to the statutory parameters according to which the rate is set by the Government Actuary and if required, will bring forward draft regulations for approval by the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he plans to bring the Northern Health and Social Care Trust in line with other Health and Social Care Trusts by implementing the Acute Care at Home Service for Older People.
answer_text: In March 2022, my Department published the Review of Urgent and Emergency Care. Under the recommendations of the review, a Task and Finish group was established, through an Intermediate care sub-group to examine the current challenges regarding hospital at home services, particularly how to ensure consistency of approach and outcomes of a high performing model. The outworkings of this group are at the later stages and a final report will be submitted to the Chief Nursing Office, as lead sponsor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the proposed closure of the site on 30 June 2024, what proposals are in place for Muckamore Abbey.
answer_text: As the owner of the Muckamore Abbey Hospital site, the Belfast Trust is responsible for planning and implementing the closure arrangements. The Trust are currently publicly consulting on a draft Equality Impact Assessment on their Implementation Plan for the closure of the site. The outcome of this process will inform any next steps that may be taken in relation to the future use of the site. The consultation is open until 30 April 2024 and can be accessed through the Belfast Trust’s website at Draft Equality Impact Assessment on Belfast Trust Implementation Plan to close Muckamore Abbey Hospital - NI Direct - Citizen Space I recognise that as we get closer to the anticipated closure date for the hospital there is a level of anxiety and unease amongst patients and their families about the future arrangements for their care, and my overriding priority is the safety and well-being of the remaining patients. The Regional Resettlement Oversight Board chaired by Dr Patricia Donnelly meets fortnightly to expedite discharge planning arrangements for those patients remaining in the hospital. Dr Donnelly also meets regularly with Departmental officials and individual Trusts in support of this work and to ensure progress with plans is maintained.To ensure there is adequate and appropriate alternative service provision in place after Muckamore closes, my Department is working with Trusts to deliver specialist Learning Disability Assessment and Treatment beds, as well as enhanced community preventative services.  Pending the outworking of this, officials have also recently written to Trusts to advise of an interim measure which will effectively regionalise the current Learning Disability specialist bed provision, and are working with Trusts to agree a process to operationalise this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what his Department is doing to improve disability access to Northern Ireland's State Care Monuments.
answer_text: The Department’s Historic Environment Division (HED) is actively engaged with a range of activities to improve access to our State Care Monuments. This includes assessments of physical interventions as well as the provision of printed and digital information to assist our visitors.Recently HED has partnered with Sign Language Branch to deliver signed tours for Dunluce Castle, Carrickfergus Castle and Grey Abbey. In 2023 Carrickfergus Castle received an Autism NI Impact Award as a result of a number of changes at the site, including the installation of a sensory room. HED are also working with the Royal National Institute of Blind People (RNIB) to deliver large print and braille guide cards with tactile images at Dunluce Castle and Carrickfergus Castle.HED’s capital construction proposals consider accessibility; evidence of this can be seen in the recent development of Ballycopeland Windmill where the visitor centre includes a changing places toilet facility.HED are currently developing their plans for 2024/25 and hope to continue to improve the accessibility at State Care Monuments where possible. This will include the provision of improved services as well as staff training and continued engagement with relevant organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what his Department is doing to promote biodiversity across Northern Ireland’s State Care Monuments.
answer_text: The Department’s Historic Environment Division (HED) cares for 186 State Care Monuments across NI. It is recognised by my Department that the management of these monuments provides opportunities to deliver environmental and quality of life benefits for local communities. State Care Monuments can be special places for biodiversity as many have been protected from development, particularly agricultural improvement, which has allowed the preservation of local habitats and species. Many sites support populations of birds, bats, and invertebrates. HED’s conservation management approach to the monuments in its care aims to promote biodiversity. For example, HED makes sure to seek advice from biodiversity specialists in advance of works at their sites to inform best practice approaches; and, continues to time works to avoid conflict with wildlife, for example roosting bats and nesting birds. It keeps its grassland management under review with a mind to increase biodiversity with reduced or discontinued cutting, including having ‘no cut areas’ while protected plant species are in bloom. HED also tries to ensure site interpretation panels consider interesting and historically significant local biodiversity.HED’s conservation-led approach continues to reflect the relationship of nature and heritage as environmental concerns, as articulated in the Regional Development Strategy 2035, and in Strategic Planning Policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has any plans to promote Northern Ireland's State Care Monuments to the public.
