answer_text: Officials in the Historic Environment Division (HED) will continue to use various channels to promote Northern Ireland's State Care Monuments. These include the Department for Communities website, the DiscoverNI website and the social media channels Facebook, X and Instagram. It particularly includes the social media campaign of Love Heritage NI that the Department for Communities manages. It aims to increase interest and appreciation for our Historic Environment, including State Care Monuments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the replacement of the pedestrian footbridge at Muff Glen, Eglington.
answer_text: My Department’s Forest Service is working with Derry City & Strabane District Council (DCSDC) and the Department for Infrastructure to complete the assessment of the overall need for and benefits of a replacement footbridge, and to establish the necessary statutory requirements and cost estimates for a new bridge.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what engagement his Department has had with the Department for Work and Pensions in relation to collating and consolidating pension schemes.
answer_text: Given what is, in effect, a single system of pensions across the UK, there is continuous and ongoing engagement between my Department and the Department for Work and Pensions on the full range of pensions issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans his Department has to increase the number of electrical vehicles sold.
answer_text: The Zero Emissions Vehicle Mandate was laid by the British Government, Scottish Government, and Welsh Senedd as the Vehicle Emissions Trading Schemes Order (VETS) in England, Scotland, and Wales in October 2023 and came into force in those jurisdictions from 03 January 2024. The scheme sets out a pathway to fully zero emissions by 2035 and puts in place quotas for ZEV sales on major motor manufacturers. Unfortunately, due to the absence of the Assembly, we were not able to join at that time. I am currently considering the North’s position on this. Whilst I consider this, the North of Ireland will continue to comply with suitably scaled existing CO2 Regulations on emissions from cars and vans. 	The North currently has around 1.27 million vehicles registered with circa 50,000 new registrations per year. Of these, there are currently in the region of 20,450 EVs registered. The proportion of new cars registered here using alternative fuels has risen to a high of 41% of all new cars sold in quarter one of 2023/24. This rise has coincided with a marked increase in publicly available electric vehicle charge points, which is helping to provide consumer confidence in the commercial market.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what actions his Department is taking to introduce recommendations to improve the travel experience for disabled passengers using Belfast International Airport.
answer_text: As Minister for Infrastructure, my powers relating to our three main airports are limited to the Airports (NI) Order 1994.  These are powers to control noise, control land in the interests of the safe and efficient use of airports, make byelaws, provide for airport constabularies and airport consultative committees and to give grants to assist capital expenditure. My Department has no legal vires under the Airports (NI) Order 1994 to instruct Airports regarding the passenger experienceBelfast International Airport is a private company responsible for ensuring it fulfils its legal requirements towards passengers with disabilities.  As such, you may wish to contact them directly with any questions you have regarding the passenger experience.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department’s wildflower policy.
answer_text: My department does not have a specific policy on wildflowers. Many wild plants receive protection through Article 14 and Schedule 8 of the Wildlife (NI) Order 1985.  In addition, many species of wildflowers benefit through the wide range of protected site mechanisms, such as Areas of Special Scientific Interest. Several other departmental strategies and initiatives, designed to protect and restore our natural environment, should also make a positive contribution to wildflower habitat.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his Department is doing to reduce the level of mental health issues experienced by stroke survivors.
answer_text: Access to psychological care is an important element of stroke treatment. All members of the stroke multidisciplinary team should be trained and engaged in supporting those with psychological problems following stroke. Each Health & Social Care Trust in Northern Ireland has a funded stroke specialist clinical psychologist to deliver direct patient care and support other multi-disciplinary team members. Stroke survivors can be also be referred to the emotional support service ‘STEPS’ delivered by the Stroke Association in Northern Ireland. Stroke survivors who experience other mental health conditions can be treated by primary care, secondary care stroke physicians or referred to mental health services if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether (i) commercial; and (ii) tourism signage can be erected on motorways in the interests of the local economy.
answer_text: The control and prohibition of advertisements on or near special roads, including motorways, is set out in Article 21 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993.The Department's approach to the provision of direction signs is in RSPPG E034 Permanent Direction Signs (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk), and the provision of ‘white on brown’ tourist signs is in RSPPG E029 The Signing of Tourist Attractions and Facilities (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to review the pay rate for NHS interpreters.
answer_text: Face-to-face spoken language interpreters are independent self-employed contractors who are not employed by the Health and Social Care (HSC) service but are paid for their service through the Business Services Organisation (BSO) at an agreed rate.HSC Interpreters must be registered with BSO and adhere to the HSC Terms of Engagement.  This stipulates an hourly rate of pay as well as a preparation payment.  The Department is aware that pay has not increased for a number of years and the impact this is having on the valuable service that interpreters provide. You will, however, be aware of the significant financial constraints within the wider Health Service.  There has been a number of correspondences to my office regarding this issue.  As a result, I have asked the Strategic Planning and Performance Group to review the rate of pay for interpreters.I will endeavour to do the best I can to come to a suitable resolution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department plans to add any additional reporting services to the NI Direct Portal.
answer_text: My Department provides a wide range of options on the NI Direct website for customers to report a road or streetlighting fault.  The list below shows the main fault group headings that each contain a range of sub-headings to select that best matches the issue when reporting a fault online.  The system automatically notifies and assigns these to the local DfI maintenance office.The NI Direct call-centre also use the fault reporting system for submitting a fault on a customer’s behalf.  The call-centre has some additional fault options that are not available on the website, such as reports of flooding, as this is best managed by them to ensure full details are provided, to reduce duplications, and to direct the these to the appropriate response agency. Over the past number of years the system has been enhanced to provide additional reporting services, such as customer acknowledgement emails and a fault tracking function, that are designed to improve the overall customer service.  However, we have no immediate plans to add further fault reporting options at this time.        FAULT GROUP HEADINGStreet lighting faultSurface DefectObstructionSpillagesSnow & IceIronworkCrash Barrier/ Guard-railSigns/road marksTraffic SignalsGrass/Weeds/HedgesDrainageRoadworksCrash Barrier/ Guard-railGraffiti, Fly-postersPermits to Work on the Road

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether he is aware of the Rivers Trust's State of our Rivers 2023 report; and (ii) to detail what his Department is doing to improve the quality of our waterways through Rivers Agency and Northern Ireland Water.
answer_text: I am indeed aware of the findings of the Rivers Trust’s report. The findings highlighted by the Rivers Trust were published by NIEA in 2021 as part of the Water Framework Directive Statistics Report, which included the information that no river water bodies in Northern Ireland achieved overall good status. At that time, NIEA explained that this was due to a change in the status reporting to include so-called ‘forever’ chemicals in the assessment.The work required is summarised in the draft third River Basin Management Plan for Northern Ireland. Following my consideration and review in light of the challenges posed by the occurrence of blue-green algal blooms in Lough Neagh, I will bring the Plan to the Executive for consideration in due course. 	The Department for Infrastructure (DfI) is aware of the State of our Rivers 2023 report and has regular engagement with The Rivers Trust when carrying out their Statutory obligations. DfI officials liaise with DAERA NIEA colleagues to identify opportunities for improvements in Water Framework Directive status when carrying out watercourse maintenance and fluvial flood alleviation works. This also allows opportunities for enhancement to be identified and considered throughout the processes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a timeframe in which his Department will resurface Victoria Road, Ballyclare.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme. Regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered. Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1M in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs.  This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need, with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. I have also allocated over £8M of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered.  This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year in the Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council area will be included in the Department’s Annual Report to Council in due course when future budgets are finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what inter-departmental discussions he has had in relation to the introduction of a walking strategy.
answer_text: I am committed to improving active travel for all in the North.  I want to create people-centred cities, towns and villages which look and feel cleaner and greener. My Department is taking a number of important steps to increase the delivery of high-quality pedestrian and cycling infrastructure.The Transport Strategy and transport plans currently being developed acknowledge the importance of active travel in the sustainable transport hierarchy approach with walking and wheeling considered first, followed by cycling, then the remainder of the modes.My Department is also currently finalising an Active Travel Delivery Plan. When complete, this plan will complement the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan and the Strategic Plan for Greenways to provide our teams with a firm basis for the prioritisation of active travel investment in future years.  The Plan includes a full update of the Department’s design guidance for both walking and cycling, recognising that there is significant commonality in the needs of these two groups. This design guidance will mark a step change in the Department’s commitment to providing safe, coherent and attractive active travel infrastructure to all those who choose to walk, wheel or cycle.Inter-departmental engagement is an important part of good government and my Department works with various Government Departments, Agencies and Councils in the development of our active travel infrastructure and promotion and I look forward to continuing to engage on these matters that offer significant cross cutting benefits to our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what inter-departmental discussions he has had in relation to the introduction of a walking strategy.
answer_text: The Department of Health is represented on strategic boards and implementation groups for cross-department sport and physical activity, active travel, and outdoor recreation, led by DfC, DfI and DAERA respectively. The benefits of walking as a mode of active travel and as a means of increasing physical activity have been discussed frequently in these forums.‘A Fitter Future for All’, the obesity prevention strategic framework for Northern Ireland, seeks to promote greater participation in all forms of physical activity, including walking and cycling, as physical activity plays a key role in improving population health and well-being. There are several cross-departmental objectives, outcomes, and indicators contained within the framework linked to walking as both a method of travel and as a physical activity. In support of A Fitter Future for All, the Public Health Agency commissions a suite of programmes to increase participation in physical activity including their ‘Walking for Health’ programme.A new strategic framework ‘Healthy Futures – A Strategic Framework to Prevent the Harm caused by Obesity, and Improve Diets and Levels of Physical Activity in Northern Ireland’ is currently in development and a public consultation on the vision, principles and themes closed on the 1 March 2024. Responses to this are being collated and reviewed and a post-consultation report will be published in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what inter-departmental discussions he has had in relation to the introduction of a walking strategy.
answer_text: In March 2022 the Department launched ‘Active Living’ the Sport and Physical Activity Strategy for Northern Ireland. As the strategy covers outdoor recreation, including walking, there is no requirement for a standalone walking strategy and as such I have not had any inter-departmental discussions on the matter.As part of the monitoring arrangements for Active Living, my Department has established a Cross-Departmental Strategic Delivery Group to oversee delivery of the Active Living Strategy and the group is comprised of representatives from all Government Departments.The Strategy’s main aim is to get more people, more active, more of the time with a focus on identifying and addressing barriers to participation in physical activity for the least active in our communities. The strategy recognises the positive impact that physical activity has on physical and mental health and the contributory role outdoor recreation, including walking, can have to encourage those who are inactive to take the first steps towards engagement in sport and physical activity.The Strategy was developed through co-design with a wide range of stakeholders including Government Departments, Councils, communities and the wider sports sector including those involved in outdoor recreation.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to provide an assessment of how it is progressing with its net zero targets.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what inter-departmental discussions he has had in relation to the introduction of a walking strategy.
answer_text: I recognise the benefit of walking as part of my Departmental responsibilities including outdoor recreation and am committed to working on an inter-departmental basis in terms of any potential walking strategy that may emerge.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action NI Water has taken to (i) mitigate; and (ii) prevent pollution in Lough Neagh.
answer_text: The issue you raised is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to review the School Transport Policy to include families who are currently excluded.
answer_text: A review of the Home to School Transport Policy commenced in 2018, however this was paused by the then Minister due to the pandemic and then suspended pending the outcome of The Independent Review of Education. My Department is currently considering the report published by the Independent Review of Education and in this context we will consider the utility of a new separate review of the policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has plans to change MOT appointments to every two years.
answer_text: I have agreed to explore with my officials the potential for biennial vehicle testing in the North. You may wish to note that a move to biennial MOT testing would require the development of primary legislation and would be subject to public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he can provide a timeline on when the bluetongue quarantine will be lifted.
answer_text: Movements of live ruminants from Great Britain to Northern Ireland can recommence when Great Britian recovers disease free status for Bluetongue.  The minimum period required before disease free status can be granted is two years from the date of the last positive case.		Movements may be permitted sooner should regionalisation for Bluetongue be pursued by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs and the devolved administrations with approval granted by the European Union.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action his Department is taking to support farmers who have had livestock prevented from entering Northern Ireland due to the bluetongue quarantine.
answer_text: DAERA officials continue to engage with colleagues across Great Britain on the next steps in respect of the Bluetongue disease control response. Stakeholders are being updated weekly via email and through virtual meetings on the position in Great Britian to inform the commercial decisions that Northern Ireland farmers might take.The DAERA website is updated regularly to inform industry on the disease picture in Great Britain. In addition, social media posts have issued to highlight the risk of a Bluetongue incursion and to advise keepers to be vigilant for clinical signs of the disease.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his Department's plans to address blue-green algae blooms at Lough Neagh.
answer_text: The situation in Lough Neagh cannot be addressed in isolation from the need to improve the quality of our environment more generally. It is my intention to bring a detailed action plan for Lough Neagh to the Executive. However, I will first bring a new Environmental Improvement Plan (EIP) to the Executive for consideration in March. The EIP, which is a plan for significantly improving our natural environment, will contain actions relevant to improving water quality across Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his Department’s plans to update the Defective Premises (Northern Ireland) Order 1975 in line with the Building Safety Act 2022.
answer_text: As part of the reorganisation from twelve to nine government departments in 2016 a Transfer of Functions Order was made and, as part of this, the Defective Premises (NI) Order 1975 was orphaned and incorrectly allocated to the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs.		As background, the Order was originally within the remit of the former Department of Housing, Local Government and Planning. Upon the dissolution of that department in 1976 its functions transferred to the newly created Department of the Environment (DoE) where the 1975 Order remained until it was subsequently transferred to Department of Agriculture, Environment & Rural Affairs by way of the 2016 Transfer of Functions Order.		 	It would be my view that this ought to rest with the Department for Communities where other related matters already reside. 		I have made representations to the Communities Minister to this effect, requesting transfer occurs as part of any forthcoming fresh transfer of functions order. I await the Minister’s reply.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his Department's options for taking forward actions in relation to the Mobuoy site.
answer_text: My next step is to take forward a public consultation on the draft Optimum Remediation Strategy. This consultation will present remediation options across the site that together form the draft strategy.  Following consultation, responses will be considered, and NIEA will determine any necessary amendments to the draft strategy, which will then be presented for the next stages of scrutiny and approval.A subsequent decision will be required for the adoption of the finalised strategy, which will inform a draft business case for remediation works subject to Department of Finance and Executive consideration.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what progress has been made by his Department towards the publication of an updated Environment Strategy.
answer_text: In the next few weeks, I intend to bring forward for Executive consideration and agreement a new environment strategy which can be adopted as Northern Ireland’s first Environmental Improvement Plan (EIP). The EIP will take a strategic approach to all the issues facing our environment, which will require collective action across government, private and public sector and in the community.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether a review is taking place in relation to his Department’s Northern Ireland Food Animal Information System.
answer_text: There is no review currently taking place in relation to the Northern Ireland Food Animal Information System (NIFAIS). As with all major projects, the NIFAIS project is subject to regular Gateway assurance assessments which are performed around key milestones. The next assessment will be scheduled prior to Stage 2 going live in 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department plans to fund the greenway from Ballyclare to Ballynure, which was originally part of the Doagh to Larne greenway proposal.
answer_text: The Greenway route from Doagh to Larne received a grant from my Department for a feasibility study in 2017, followed by detailed design in 2019. Greenway projects are generally council led, and responsibility for this project rests with Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council, with 50% funding available from my Department for the capital delivery of Greenways.My Department issued a significant letter of offer in March 2023 to the council for the construction of Ballyclare Town Greenway, which would link in with this overall Greenway route. Officials in my Department are working closely with councils, including Antrim and Newtownabbey Council, to better understand their 5-year greenway programme, and to develop an appropriate grant and support framework for the effective delivery of such programmes. The route between Ballyclare and Ballynure is being considered as part of this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to increase funding for local care home facilities.
answer_text: The Department annually reviews the regional tariff rate for residents placed in residential and nursing care homes. The regional tariff rate is a guide to what the HSC system considers to be fair and affordable, it is not a fixed price for a care placement.  This annual inflationary review process is underway and the sector will be advised as soon as the budget permits.My Department has established a Social Care Collaborative Forum to provide a formal mechanism for officials and representatives of the Social Care Sector (statutory, voluntary/community and private sectors), to work together as partners to build shared values and deliver improvements that will support and sustain social care now and into the future.  As part of its remit, the Forum will review proposals arising out of the consultation on the Reform of Adult Social Care including future charging arrangements within the sector. Progress against all aspects of the Forum’s work will be reported on and where specific proposals are being made in respect of charging for care these will be subject to public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has plans to (i) review; and (ii) replenish the afforestation scheme on land that is owned by his Department between Greystone Road, Antrim and the DfI Rathmore Depot.
answer_text: Officials in my department in conjunction with DAERA Forest Service, recently reviewed the afforestation scheme between the B95 Greystone Road and Rathmore Depot in Antrim. Following a survey, dead saplings totalling approximately 20% of the original volume were replaced with new saplings. At this location this is considered an appropriate volume considering the poor soil conditions. This site along with others will continue to be monitored until the trees have fully established.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether there are plans to develop outdoor learning opportunities in his Department’s forestry estate.
answer_text: I recognise the opportunities that our forest environment provides for outdoor learning.  The Department’s forests already make a significant contribution through organised school visits and facilitating their use by a wide range of outdoor activity and educational providers.  School visits to forests provide real-life opportunities to learn about our environment and the importance of its protection, sustainability and the role forests can play in addressing the challenges of climate change.  The facilities in forests, available in partnership with Councils and other providers, includes learning opportunities about nature, wildlife, biodiversity, historical and cultural environment and developing outdoor-based skills. I am happy to continue to work with the Education Minister and his team to explore how we build further on the opportunity that our forests and indeed other public amenities present to foster the interest in protecting and enhancing our natural environment that I know is shared by so many young people.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether progress has been made following the discovery of zebra mussels at Movanagher Fish Farm; and (ii) whether there are plans to stock the DAERA Public Angling Estate from this source in 2024.
answer_text: The presence of Zebra Mussels is still preventing the stocking of fish from Movanagher. 		I will be considering options for future stocking of the Public Angling Estate, including the 2024 season, in the coming weeks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department plans to review the impact on (i) aquatic life; and (ii) the natural environment caused by sand dredging at Lough Neagh.
answer_text: A study was previously commissioned by NIEA in 2016 on the implications of sand extraction on the Lough Neagh and Lough Beg SPA and Ramsar, found that although there was potential for localised effects of dredging on aquatic plants and animals.  I intend to meet officials in the time ahead to discuss the findings within this report.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on (i) the Forests for Our Future programme; and (ii) the interagency working on this scheme.
answer_text: Since 2020, 1,700 hectares of new woodland has been planted under the Forests for Our Future Programme. The Department has grant aided land owners to plant 1,632 hectares of new woodland on their land. The Department’s Forest Service has increased its forest estate by planting 68 hectares of new woodland. Officials are continuing to promote woodland creation and are working on an interagency approach with a range of parties across the sector. These key partners, including local councils, NI Water, The National Trust and The Woodland Trust have already planted about 90 hectares within the programme. Additionally, a number of current projects for planting around 200 hectares of new woodland are planned for planting in 2024/25.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what progress has been made by his Department towards the publication of a peatlands strategy.
answer_text: The draft Peatland Strategy is being finalised and I intend to bring this to the Executive with a view to publishing it later this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme; and (ii) what plans there are to extend the area covered by this scheme.
answer_text: (i) Since its launch in 2022, there has been excellent uptake of the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme. Over 90% of farm businesses in Zones 1 and 2 have registered for soil sampling and analysis. This includes over 340,000 fields in Counties Down, Armagh and Fermanagh, and the southern parts of Counties Antrim and Tyrone.  (ii) I plan to extend the Scheme by opening Zone 3 in June this year and Zone 4 in 2025, meaning that sampling and analysis across all counties will be completed in 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the distribution of the 2023-28 Environment Fund.
answer_text: The 2023/24 budget distribution for the 2023–2028 Environment Fund (EF) is over £7 million. This has primarily been distributed through the multi-year Strategic Strand of the EF with £260k also being distributed through a Water Quality Improvement Challenge Competition. Annex A sets out the details of Letters of Offer issued to date and provisional offers under the Strategic Strand of the EF for 2023/24.  	Distribution of EF funds to Strategic Strand projects in future years will be subject to budget availability. Further funding opportunities may become available during the lifetime of the Environment Fund, including through additional Environmental Challenge Competitions, again subject to budget availability.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department’s plan to publish a NI Waste Management Strategy
answer_text: A Northern Ireland Resources & Waste Management Strategy is currently under development, with a view to launching a consultation later this year. A date for publication will be set following the consultation and once responses have been analysed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the Household Waste Recycling Collaborative Change Programme; and (ii) whether this will be reintroduced.
answer_text: My Department’s £23m Household Waste Recycling Collaborative Change Programme (HWRCCP) assists local councils to transform kerbside recycling and Household Recycling Centre infrastructure and services to help deliver a circular economy in Northern Ireland. This is achieved by supporting capital projects that improve both the quantity and quality of household waste recycling collected. Reducing the quantity of residual waste will also reduce reliance on landfill and recovery. Funding of up to 50% eligible costs is available. To date, £4.9m has been made available for nine projects across six councils. Allocations of funding under the Programme must be made by 31 March 2026.Following the completion of a review of the HWRCCP, my officials have recently written to all council chief executives and directors of environmental services advising them of revised HWRCCP criteria and arrangements for submitting applications.  Further information on the fund and application process can be found on the DAERA website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what progress has been made by his Department towards publication of a Biodiversity Strategy.
answer_text: A Nature Recovery (Biodiversity) Strategy is currently under development, with a view to launching a public consultation later this year. Publication of a final agreed strategy will be dependent on the outcome of the consultation exercise.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what progress has been made by his Department towards publication of an Environmental Improvement Plan.
answer_text: In the next few weeks, I intend to bring forward for Executive consideration and agreement a new environment strategy which can be adopted as Northern Ireland’s first Environmental Improvement Plan (EIP). The EIP will take a strategic approach to all the issues facing our environment, which will require collective action across government, private and public sector and in the community.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to resurface Ballycorr Road, Ballyclare.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered. Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network. The 2023/24 resurfacing programme for the Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council area was presented to Council members and can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-council-report-antrim-and-newtownabbey-2023.PDF  The resurfacing programme along with other programmes for 2024/25 will be prepared when budgets are confirmed and will be presented to Council in due course.The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how often the exhibition material on display at the Ulster Museum is changed; and (ii) whether there is a review process in place for changing the materials on display.
answer_text: The exhibition material on display at the Ulster Museum and whether there is a review process in place for changing materials on display is an operational matter for National Museums NI.		You should, therefore, direct your question to National Museums NI at info@nationalmuseumsni.org.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how many people where admitted to Emergency Departments in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust in 2023; and (ii) how many people had to wait longer than the Trust’s target time for admission.
answer_text: Information on (i) the number of attendances at Emergency Departments (EDs) in the Northern Health and Social (HSC) Trust, in 2023, and (ii) how many of those attendances did not meet the target of being treated and admitted to hospital within 4 hours of arrival at ED, is provided in Table 1 overleaf. Please note that the number of attendances is based on new and unplanned review attendances only and does not equate to the number of patients treated, as a patient may attend emergency care departments more than once during each year.A new attendance refers to the first unplanned attendance by a patient for a new clinical condition at an ED / Minor Injuries Unit (MIU). An unplanned review attendance relates to any patient who returns to an ED / Minor Injuries Unit (MIU) without written instruction, for the same or related clinical condition within 30 days of the first (initial) attendance.The length of time a patient waits refers to the time between entering the ED and being logged in at reception, until leaving the ED via discharge or admission to hospital.Table 1: Number of Attendances (New and Unplanned Reviews Only) at Emergency Departments in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, in 2023, and the Number of those Attendances that did not meet the Target of being Treated and Admitted Within 4 Hours of Arrival at EDYearTotal AttendancesTarget Not Met (Number) 2023153,96624,956

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department's plans for the development of the Circle Line in the transportation network, including the proposed route and the projected timeline for implementation.
answer_text: The Department, in conjunction with the Department of Transport in Dublin, is in the process of finalising the All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR) which will guide the future development of rail in NI and help determine the priorities for the region. The Review’s recommendations include improving existing rail-airport connections at George Best Airport and reinstating the railway between Lisburn and Antrim to enable Belfast International Airport to be connected to the railway network. While these two recommendations, if implemented, will contribute to the proposed route of the Belfast Circle Line, the Department is not actively considering the Belfast Circle Line proposal currently.In terms of timescales for implementation of these two recommendations, once the AISRR is finalised and published, further work will be required to prioritise the recommendations, undertake feasibility studies and develop business cases before any decisions in relation to future investment are taken. I can however confirm that Translink have received funding through the Union Connectivity Review to undertake a feasibility study for the re-opening of the line between Lisburn and Antrim and that study has now commenced. It will consider both the potential passenger demand and operational model for the line, including the potential connection to Belfast International Airport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans there are to install street lighting on the Toome bypass.
answer_text: My Department’s policy on the provision of street lighting is set out in Roads Service Policy & Procedure Guide (RSPPG) E072. On rural motorways and trunk roads, street lighting is only provided at those roundabouts and junctions that meet this criteria and is not generally provided on the mainline.This policy ensures that lighting is provided in those areas where maximum benefit to the public can be derived, particularly where road safety is concerned, as well as reducing environmental impacts and future maintenance and energy costs.There are therefore no plans to install additional street lighting on the Toome By-Pass.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department plans to reinstate the Knockmore line as part of the rail network.
answer_text: The All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR), is considering how the rail network on the island of Ireland can improve sustainable connectivity between major cities, enhance regional accessibility, support balanced regional development and contribute to decarbonisation.Enhancing the integration of rail with other transport modes is one of 13 objectives within the Review. In support of this objective, the Review recommends reinstating the railway between Lisburn and Antrim to enable Belfast International Airport to be connected to the railway network. Following closure of the AISRR public consultation in September 2023, work is ongoing to finalise the Review and its recommendations. Once the Review is published, further work will be required to prioritise the recommendations and then to undertake feasibility studies and develop business cases to support future investment decisions. I can confirm that Translink have received funding through the Union Connectivity Review to undertake a feasibility study for the re-opening of the line between Lisburn and Antrim and the study has now commenced. It will consider both the potential passenger demand and operational model for the line.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will increase funding for the Supporting People programme.
answer_text: The Supporting People Programme is a vital service given the invaluable support it provides to vulnerable people, enabling them to have a better quality of life, to live more independently and to maintain their tenancies.I will ask my Department and NIHE to continue to engage with the sector through these challenging times to seek ways to ensure value for money. We must continue to ensure that the programme delivers real outcomes for service users – those most vulnerable in our society.   A key challenge for my Department is the ability of the NIHE to fund the Supporting People Programme given the current budget position.  Approximately 70% of the budget goes towards payment of the Supporting People staff across 850 services.  The Executive will agree and set the 2024-25 Budget and once the outcome is known I will consider how best to allocate the Department’s budget against key priorities.I am happy to confirm that the Supporting People Programme remains a priority for me.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he is considering introducing proposals to ban the XL Bully dog breed, similar to the ban in England and Wales.
answer_text: A proportionate and effective way forward to deal with potentially dangerous dogs is a key issue for me.I recognise the need to move quickly in this area and I am keen to engage with a wide range of stakeholders to hear their views, so that any decision I take will be based on the best evidence available.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 27312/17-22, for an update on the outstanding task orders at Crumlin Integrated College.
answer_text: The Education Authority has confirmed that all task orders raised between 1 November 2021 and 31 January 2022 are now complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 26722/17-22, whether a location for the Connect2 hub, planned for the Antrim and Newtownabbey area, has been secured.
answer_text: Given the ongoing implementation of the Estate Review and the limited uptake of the Phase 1 Connect2 hubs, there are no current plans to invest in additional hubs. This would include the proposed hub in Newtownabbey.The Civil Service has implemented a Hybrid Working policy which helps support regional balance by affording its workforce more choice about their working location allowing a mix of workplace, home, and remote working.As a major employer, the Civil Service recognises the potential benefits of hybrid working in contributing to regional balance; therefore, hybrid working is available, where possible, for all employment opportunities offered by the Civil Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department’s plans to implement a cycle route between Chapel Roundabout and Belmont Road, Antrim.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department’s plan to conduct a flood study for Antrim.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the number of pothole repairs that are outstanding in the Antrim and Newtownabbey area.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what pedestrian safety improvements are planned for Antrim Road, Glengormley.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Department’s plans to resurface Largy Road, Crumlin.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the provision of puffin crossing in Glengormley village centre.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the development of an Environmental Improvement Scheme for Glengormley village centre.
answer_text: The proposed Glengormley Town Centre Environmental Improvement Scheme is a high priority project for Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council and my Department. An Outline Business Case from Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council has been received by my Department and is under consideration.  However, there is a key interdependency for the proposed Scheme with the route of the Belfast Rapid Transport 2 (BRT2) project. The Department for Infrastructure is currently considering the feasibility of the BRT2 route going through Glengormley and until a decision is taken my Department cannot commit to funding the Glengormley Town Centre Environmental Improvement Scheme.My officials will continue to work closely with Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council and monitor the outcome of the decision-making process for the route of BRT2.Any final decision on the Environmental Improvement Scheme for Glengormley town centre will be dependent upon departmental budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the official discussions he has had with the Ulster Farmer’s Union on the Northern Ireland Protocol Bill.
answer_text: I have not had any official engagement with the Ulster Farmers’ Union to discuss the Northern Ireland Protocol Bill. However, I am repeatedly on record saying that the Northern Ireland Protocol is unworkable for businesses and unnecessary.		I welcome the progression of the Northern Ireland Protocol Bill through Parliament since its introduction on 13 June, and I await the outcome of the next stage, the House of Lords Committee stage, with four sittings scheduled between now and 07 November 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what assessment his Department has made on the implications of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill on reducing the environmental impact of fishing, as regulated under EU Regulation.
answer_text: Following the introduction of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill in Parliament on 22 September 2022, officials in Marine & Fisheries Division have commenced a review of the marine and fisheries legislation to identify what falls within scope of the Bill’s provisions. 		This initial review will consider the purpose of the retained marine and fisheries legislation, and the need to sunset, preserve without change or reform it. This will include whether the legislation is required to meet international obligations that relate to fishing and the marine environment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what assessment he has made of the adequacy of the current animal welfare protections for fish farmed in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I am aware that officials in the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) have been considering a number of improvements that could be made to the welfare of farmed fish at the time of killing.  		In addition, the Animal Welfare Committee, which advises my Department and other UK administrations on welfare at the time of killing, is due to provide an update on their opinion on the welfare of farmed fish at the time of killing.  My Department will consider the findings from that report as well as any Defra proposals to determine if legislative changes are required in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department intends to introduce legislative protections for animals at the time of killing that ensures farmed fish are spared avoidable pain, distress or suffering during their killing and related operations.
answer_text: I am aware that Ministers in the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) have been considering a number of improvements that could be made to the welfare of farmed fish at the time of killing.  		In addition, the Animal Welfare Committee, which advises my Department and other UK administrations on welfare at the time of killing is due to provide an update on their opinion on the welfare of farmed fish at the time of killing.  My Department will consider the findings from that report as well as any Defra proposals to determine if legislative changes are required in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what assessment his Department has made on the implications of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill on the labelling and traceability of fishery and aquaculture products, as required under EU Regulation.
answer_text: Following the introduction of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill in Parliament on 22 September 2022, officials in DAERA's Marine and Fisheries Division, liaising with the Foods Standards Agency in Northern Ireland and Local Council Environmental Health Departments, have initiated a review of how the Bill may impact relevant legislation and delivery of official controls in Northern Ireland.This initial review will consider the need to sunset, preserve without change or reform the retained legislation on the labelling and traceability of fishery and aquaculture products.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether a taskforce has been established for the purpose of co-ordinating a response to the spread of highly pathogenic avian influenza.
answer_text: A formal taskforce to co-ordinate response to HPAI in wild birds has not been established. However, DAERA has ongoing engagement with key stakeholders in Northern Ireland and with equivalent bodies across the UK regarding impacts of this virus on wild birds.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what recent estimate his Department has made of the level of plastic pollution in (i) waterways; and (ii) rivers in Northern Ireland, in the latest period for which the data is available.
answer_text: My Department undertakes statutory monitoring in waterways, including rivers, to meet the requirements of the Water Framework Directive as enshrined in Northern Ireland legislation. We do not monitor levels of plastics in waterways, as there is no statutory requirement to do this, and therefore no data are available.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will make an assessment of the impact that excluding glass from the scope of the forthcoming Deposit Return Scheme for drinks containers will have on recycling rates.
answer_text: I refer the member to the response to AQW 1045/22-27.	Officials are currently finalising the Government response and impact assessment for the 2021 consultation on a Deposit Return Scheme, for publication alongside Defra and Welsh Government. The impact assessment to the 2021 consultation included a review of the inclusion / exclusion of glass. I will keep the exclusion of glass from DRS under review to ensure glass containers are meeting the required recycling targets.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to ensure that every person living in Northern Ireland has access to sufficient affordable food.
answer_text: I am very concerned about the serious cost of living challenges that people in Northern Ireland are facing as the impacts of energy prices and rising food prices are being felt right across our communities, especially by the most vulnerable in our society. Simply, we must be able to eat and heat our homes.  I am committed to protecting Northern Ireland’s food supplies making sure that everyone can access safe quality food at prices they can afford. My Department is committed to developing and implementing processes which aim to address issues which may affect the supply of food to Northern Ireland, including a long term solution for food movements to Northern Ireland.I wrote to the former DEFRA Secretary of State, Ranil Jayawardena MP on 07 September 2022 outlining a number of concerns including the impact of the cost of living crisis on NI industry and citizens and the Northern Ireland Protocol. I have urged that we work collaboratively to develop pragmatic and proportional solutions to provide surety to the people of NI.I have continued to engage regularly with UK Government at a senior level and drive home the importance of national collaboration on these issues.My Officials have significant ongoing engagement with industry stakeholders, Defra, other devolved administrations and the Department of Agriculture, Food and Marine (ROI) on those cross-cutting issues relating to reducing the impacts of volatility in price and supply of key industry inputs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what assessment his Department has made on the implications of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill on habitat restoration in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The UK Government has recently introduced the Retained EU Law (REUL) (Revocation and Reform) Bill which proposes that retained EU Law is repealed, amended or extended from 31 December 2023. My officials are considering the potential impact of the REUL Bill along with colleagues across the NICS and in other UK jurisdictions.  I can confirm that Habitats Regulations fall within the scope of the REUL Bill.It is my intention that Northern Ireland continues to have a clear statutory basis for protecting the environment and human health, as has been the case throughout the changes resulting from the UK’s withdrawal from the EU.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what assessment his Department has made on the implications of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill on animal welfare standards in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill was introduced to Parliament on 22 September 2022. Officials across the Department are engaging with officials in other administrations on this Bill. As the Bill is at an early stage in its progression through Parliamentary process, it will be subject to further discussion, debate and amendment.You will be aware, however, due to the Northern Protocol, EU legislation relating to animal welfare continues to apply in Northern Ireland. Furthermore, the welfare of animals in Northern Ireland continues to be protected by the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the introduction of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill, which risks removing restrictions on the use of decontaminants on meat, what assessment his Department has made on the implications of this proposed legislation on food standards in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, contaminants in food is the responsibility of the Food Standards Agency (FSA). As your enquiry relates to this area it should be directed to FSA.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what assessment has been made of the impact a vacancy in the ministerial office post 28 October 2022 will have on departmental (i) decision making; and (ii) spending.
answer_text: If there is no Minister in office after 28 October, decisions and spending that require the approval of a Minister cannot be made.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what capacity is remaining in Cottonmount Landfill Site.
answer_text: I can advise that Cottonmount Landfill, Mallusk operates under Permit P0090/05A and has an estimated remaining capacity of approximately 3,646,644m3 as of the latest survey on 31 March 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what capacity is remaining in Mullaghglass Landfill Site.
answer_text: I can advise that Mullaghglass Landfill operates under Permit P0107/05A and has an estimated remaining capacity of approximately 4,191m3 as of 01 October 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 25511/17-22, to provide an update on his Department’s plan to increase the maximum fine for littering to £200, following the public consultation which closed on 18 January 2022.
answer_text: It is my Department’s intention to amend the Environmental Offences (Fixed Penalties) (Miscellaneous Provisions) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2012 to increase the maximum penalty capable of being specified by a District Council:under Article 6(6A)(a) of the Litter (Northern Ireland) Order 1994 for the offence of littering; andunder section 44(1)(a) of the Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 for the offence of contravention of a dog control order relating to dog fouling, from £80 to £200.The amending Regulations, which will be subject to the negative resolution procedure before the Assembly, are currently undergoing scrutiny with Departmental Solicitors and it is anticipated that they will be laid before 31 December 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 25512/17-22, to provide an update on his Department’s progress in achieving a new Eco-Department accreditation.
answer_text: The proposal to seek DAERA’s accreditation as the world’s first Eco-Department is included in the draft Environment Strategy. This is an undertaking which will underpin my Department’s commitment to continuous improvement.  To achieve this accreditation DAERA must undergo a rigorous 7-step assessment process which is overseen by The Foundation for Environmental Education. Successful certification can typically take up to 24 months to complete.Officials from several parts of my Department are currently engaged in an initial scoping of the resources and funding required to advance this important initiative, and I am pleased to be able to confirm that Keep Northern Ireland Beautiful is assisting in taking this important work forward in its capacity as the Northern Ireland organisation responsible for both the Eco-Schools and Eco-Campus initiatives.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 30570/17-22, to provide an update on plans to protect and restore Randalstown Forest following stakeholder consultation.
answer_text: Following consultation with stakeholders Forest Service has concluded the review of plans for forests in the Antrim Forestry Planning Area, which includes Randalstown forest.Forest Service received 55 stakeholder responses which informed the review of the forest plans. Following revision of the plans these were made available to stakeholders, including the plan for Randalstown forest, in late March 2022. These plans are now available to be viewed on-line in an accessible Storymap format via the DAERA website, Forestry Planning page. The Randalstown forest plan reaffirms the overall commitment to progress the forest further towards continuous cover forestry. This includes regenerating trees over the longer term whilst maintaining and protecting areas of high conservation value such as ancient and native woodland, deadwood habitat and veteran trees.Delivering the objectives of this plan will involve a further programme, within the next 5 years of gradually removing selected trees from the woodlands to improve the resilience of the forest and the diversity of tree species and age range.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what discussions his Department has had with the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs regarding the impacts of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill for the fisheries sector in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Bill was introduced to Parliament on 22 September 2022, Officials across the Department are engaging with officials in other administrations on it.  		As the Bill is at an early stage in its progression through Parliamentary process, it will be subject to further discussion, debate and amendment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what recent assessment his Department has made on the use of mineral fertilisers in reducing input costs.
answer_text: Total deliveries of fertiliser in 2022 reduced by 35% compared with the first half of 2021.  However, it is too early to detect a long-term trend as quarterly deliveries have shown volatility in the past.  A decline in use was expected in the short term given that fertiliser prices have increased by around 150% in the first six months of 2022 versus the same period in 2021.  These price shocks are outside the control of the Department and farmers, and consequently it becomes more important that fertiliser is used as efficiently as possible in combination with other nutrients.		The College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE), under direction from the Rising Cost Taskforce, has established a task and finish group to scope out relevant knowledge transfer relating to nutrient use efficiency topics and delivery methods for early spring 2023. The membership of this group is from CAFRE, Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute, NI Grain Trade Association, Agri-Search and Ulster Farmers’ Union.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what discussions his Department has had with the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs regarding the impacts of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill for the agriculture sector in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Bill was introduced to Parliament on 22 September 2022, officials across the Department are engaging with officials in other administrations on it.  		As the Bill is at an early stage in its progression through Parliamentary process, it will be subject to further discussion, debate and amendment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following the recent announcement that the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs has awarded 22 projects funding through the Landscape Recovery scheme, will he be implementing a similar scheme in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Following a period of public consultation, I announced 54 policy decisions on Future Agricultural Policy for Northern Ireland, including 10 related to the Farming with Nature Package. The Farming with Nature Package is being developed through co-design with stakeholders, and in line with the principles stated in the consultation document.  The overall Package may eventually offer measures broadly similar to some within the Landscape Recovery Scheme but will be tailored to meet the unique needs of Northern Ireland landscapes and habitats.  My officials are presently working to develop a number of Test and Learn Pilots which will help identify solutions and recommendations to inform future scheme design and continue to work closely with key stakeholders to ensure these will have the desired impact.A number of landscape projects are currently being undertaken through the current Environmental Farming Scheme’s Higher Level Group projects.  These projects are facilitated by environmental organisations, funded by my Department, and deliver large scale environmental benefits for breeding waders, water quality and general habitat in focused locations.My Department also provides funding to not-for-profit organisations through the Environment Fund to secure delivery of strategic environmental outcomes including the development and implementation of landscape scale management and restoration of priority habitats.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has met with his UK Government counterparts in relation to the easing of immigration regulations for agri-food workers to address labour shortages in the sector.
answer_text: Since the introduction of the new immigration system in January 2021, reports of labour shortages and concerns have been consistently raised by our agri-food stakeholders in NI as well as by stakeholders across the UK.  As a result, there has been ongoing discussion and engagement on the matter, including at the most recent Inter-Ministerial meeting in July 2022. Ultimately, immigration policy is a reserved matter and as such my primary focus has been ongoing communication with the top levels of Government in Whitehall to highlight the severity of the problems facing our agri-food sector. I have made several representations to my relevant UK counterparts on the issue including raising concerns in person as well as providing written correspondence.  In January 2021 I provided an in-depth evidence paper to the Home Office which analysed both the seasonal and non-seasonal migrant labour need in the NI agri-food sector and over the past 20 months I have continued to highlight the issues and proposed solutions outlined in this document. I last met in person with the then Defra Secretary of State George Eustice MP on 08 June 2022, to discuss a number of issues facing the NI agriculture sector, including labour challenges as I understand fully the impact that the immigration system is having on the supply of labour in our agri-food sector. I also wrote to the new Defra Secretary of State Ranil Jayawardena on 07 September 2022 regarding the challenges and opportunities facing the NI agri-food sector raising labour concerns.  I received a response to this correspondence on 29th September 2022 stating that the United Kingdom is committed to becoming a high-skilled, high-wage economy and more must be done to attract British workers into the sector by offering training, career options, wage increases, and increased investment in automation technology.While the UK Government’s consistent response to labour concerns has been their immigration policy will not change and that all sectors requiring labour should focus their efforts on recruiting domestic workers and investing where possible in automation, they have introduced some changes in recent months to their visa routes. For example, in June 2022 it was announced that the Seasonal Agricultural Workers (SAW) scheme quota would increase from 30,000 working visas to 40,000 and that a fifth of these additional visas would be allocated to the seasonal poultry sector. Furthermore, the chancellor Rt Hon Kwasi Kwarteng MP delivered his 2022 Growth Plan speech in late September 2022 and committed to providing a planned statement on immigration policy in late October 2022/early November 2022 as part of this plan.  It is hoped that this statement will include detail on help address the labour shortage issues across the economy including the agricultural sector.I plan to keep pressing on the issue and my officials are currently collating evidence highlighting the significant labour shortage concerns in our agri-food se
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what assessment he has made of the impact the cost of living crisis is having on the number of family pets being given to animal rescue and rehoming organisations.
answer_text: I am aware of the significant financial pressures households may encounter due to the increased cost of living and the potential for this to impact on the number of family pets given to animal rescue and rehoming organisations. My Department has regular engagement with animal welfare organisations and local councils. This will ensure that it keeps abreast of any animal welfare issues that may arise because of the cost-of-living crisis.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 2934/22-27 and given that the publication of the Peatland Strategy will require Executive approval, what impact will the absence of an Executive have on peatland restoration in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The publication of the Northern Ireland Peatland Strategy will require Executive approval.  Whilst it is anticipated that several actions in the draft Strategy could progress in the absence of the Executive, a number are likely to require cross Government agreement to implement.  My Officials are continuing to support ongoing peatland restoration projects in Northern Ireland, which may complement the Peatland Strategy Implementation Plan when it has received Executive approval.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following the recent announcement that the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs has commissioned Natural England to assess the vulnerability of seabird species in light of the pressures they are facing and to propose actions to address them, whether he will consider commissioning similar research on the seabird population of Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Northern Ireland hosts internationally significant breeding seabird populations, which are subject to rolling annual monitoring. Comprehensive UK and Ireland seabird censuses take place roughly every 15 years to provide robust metapopulation data. The fourth national UK seabird census took place between 2015 and 2021 and data are currently being analysed prior to publication. 		DAERA has been developing a Northern Ireland Seabird Conservation Strategy to support resilience and recovery in local seabird populations and help protect the marine ecosystem on which they depend. The Strategy is intended to go out to consultation in 2023 and will drive further research needs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what discussions he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure on (i) the contribution of allotments to communities; and (ii) the steps to take to promote allotments through the planning system.
answer_text: I have had no specific discussions with the Minister for Infrastructure relating to the contribution of allotments to communities or their promotion through the planning system. My Department has however provided funding through the Rural Development Programme to Rural Partnerships in support of planning and development of allotments in the Lagan Valley and Plumbridge areas.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what advice his Department (a) has issued; and (b) is planning to issue to minimise the risk of future outbreaks of avian flu in Northern Ireland this year; and (ii) what other steps he is taking to minimise the risk of outbreaks in 2023.
answer_text: My Department has regularly issued numerous communications on the need for farmers to ensure good biosecurity and protect their flocks from an avian influenza (AI) incursion.  Following our most recently confirmed case of an AI incursion at Castle Espie, County Down, we have increased our targeted engagement on this matter with direct meetings with stakeholders and an increase in our departmental messaging, again, highlighting the need for excellent biosecurity measures to protect the Northern Ireland (NI) flock.  Recent communications have also focused on my announcement of the introduction of an Avian Influenza Protection Zone (AIPZ) which came into effect here on Monday 17 October 2022.We will continue to meet as necessary with stakeholders and continue to promote key messages on various media platforms in the higher risk winter months ahead, in the hope it assists to minimise the risk of any further  incursions in NI.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) when was the last Affordable Warmth Customer Satisfaction Survey conducted by the Housing Executive; and (ii) what were the results of this survey.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The last Affordable Warmth Scheme Customer Satisfaction Survey, conducted by the Housing Executive, was carried out summer 2023; the report was finalised late 2023.  This 2023 survey was the fourth report for the Affordable Warmth Scheme.  Like the previous 2020 survey, it shows high levels of satisfaction among those who received help under the Affordable Warmth Scheme. Overall, and in line with previous surveys, the majority of respondents (96%) were satisfied / very satisfied with the Scheme. The majority (91%) of respondents said their standard of housing had improved since measures were installed.The 2023 survey report will be available on the Housing Executive’s website shortly.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.
