question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) when was the last Warm Homes Customer Satisfaction Survey conducted by the Housing Executive; and (ii) what were the results of this survey.
answer_text: The Warm Homes Scheme was replaced by the Affordable Warmth Scheme in 2014.  The last Warm Homes Customer Satisfaction Survey was carried out by the Housing Executive in 2013.The full 2013 survey is available on the Housing Executive’s website at Warm Homes Customer Satisfaction Survey 2013 (https://www.nihe.gov.uk/Documents/Research/Warm-homes-survey/2013-warm-homes-customer-satisfaction-survey)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to improvements in home energy efficiency across the Housing Executive’s housing stock, what progress has been made to deliver the whole house solution, as recommended in the Cavity Wall Insulation Inspection Report carried out by South Eastern Regional College.
answer_text: Following the SERC Cavity Wall Insulation Inspection Report and the subsequent larger Cavity Wall Insulation study undertaken by the BBA, the Housing Executive has undertaken some pilot retrofit projects which use a whole house solution including the fabric first methodology. These schemes will help inform the development of the Housing Executive’s Energy Efficiency Strategy, which will promote sustainable retrofit, reduce fuel poverty and increase the thermal comfort for tenants.It should be noted that rolling out this approach across the Housing Executive’s stock would require a very considerable level of funding.The Housing Executive and the Department for Communities will continue to work together to provide a sustainable solution to funding stock investment needs, thereby enabling delivery of programmes such as retrofit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to outline what plans his Department has to develop a highly pathogenic avian influenza in wild birds response plan for Northern Ireland, as recommended by the United Nations Convention on Migratory Species Scientific Task Force on Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza in wild birds; and (ii) to provide a timescale for the delivery of such a response plan.
answer_text: Many of the key constituent parts of a HPAI response plan for wild birds are and have been in place: targeted carcass collection and testing, consistent messaging and advice, and scoping of surveillance and population data evidence needs. Building population resilience has to date been focused through the development of a NI Seabird Conservation Strategy, reflecting HPAI impacts on breeding seabirds over the summer.	The inherent mobility of wild birds limits the range of actions that can be usefully taken, but officials are assessing further options in relation to additional biosecurity measures that may benefit wild bird populations. 			While all these actions are ongoing, there is currently no timescale to develop a formal response plan for HPAI in wild birds.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many school age children have been diagnosed with dyslexia in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on when a pupil received their diagnosis.  The total number of school age children recorded by primary, post primary and special schools with dyslexia for the last five years can be found in the table below.  Academic YearPupils recorded as dyslexic2021/228,2392020/219,3102019/209,8612018/1910,1212017/189,803Source: Annual School Census

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what assessment her Department has made of the impact on children’s wellbeing of receiving an early diagnosis of dyslexia.
answer_text: My Department’s vision is that EVERY child and young person is happy learning and succeeding and for children who require different levels of support, it is vital that they get the right support from the right people at the right time and in the right place.  The current End to End (E2E) review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) has 12 workstreams, one of those is in relation to pupil support services and part of this workstream will be examining how effective pupil support services are for children with SEN.  The Review will have a focus on improving outcomes for all children with additional support needs and ensuring that they receive appropriate intervention at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps he is taking to ensure mature students with learning disabilities receive support and encouragement to access higher education.
answer_text: I believe higher education opportunities should be available to all, regardless of social or economic background, including mature students with learning difficulties.Widening participation in higher education continues to be a priority for my Department. We want to ensure that those most able but least likely can access and benefit from the higher education that is right for them. To facilitate this all HE providers are required to spend a minimum of 10% of additional fee income on widening participation activities.The Department has identified specific target groups for widening participation, including students with disabilities and adult returners. Each year all higher education providers must produce a Widening Access and Participation Plan (WAPP) for Departmental approval detailing the support available to all targets groups. This support can take the form of bursaries or scholarships; however it also includes outreach and retention activities to support disadvantaged students in their journey into and through higher education.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department will (i) review; and (ii) increase the support it offers to claimants whose capability for work is affected because they are disabled or have a health condition, including people who have dyslexia.
answer_text: The Department is fully committed to supporting those with disabilities and health conditions to progress towards, move into and stay in employment. Our wide ranging provision supports customers through both specialist and pan-disability approaches, and includes Workable, Access to Work, the Condition Management Programme, £1.5m in match funding for European Social Funded Programmes, specialist pathways within JobStart, and a range of more general labour market interventions to help remove barriers to employment, such as childcare.We also collaborate closely with partners in central and local government, and those within the voluntary and community sector. Our Labour Market Partnerships form an essential link for collaborative and localised commissioning of labour market provision, with disability identified as one of six key thematic areas.The Department has an ongoing programme of review and continuous improvement across its provision and services. For example, work is well underway to refresh our Access to Work and Workable programmes into a new In-Work Support provision.In addition, my Department has led development of a new Disability and Work Strategy, which will set a long term commitment to increasing the rate of disability employment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps she is taking increase access to diagnostic assessments for parents who have concerns that their child may have dyslexia.
answer_text: The Education Authority Literacy Service (EALS)  does not provide diagnostic assessments for parents; however, the service provides a broad range of training, information and suggested activities and resources to help parents and schools to support literacy development https://send.eani.org.uk/support-services/literacy.The Special Educational Needs (SEN) Code of Practice is needs led and not dependent on a pupil having a diagnosis of dyslexia.  Pupils experiencing literacy difficulties should be recorded on the school SEN register under the category Cognition and Learning. Where a parent has a concern in relation to their child’s literacy, they should, in the first instance, discuss this with their child’s school.  All schools have access to a designated EA Educational Psychologist (EP) to consult with on these matters.  Currently, if following assessment by an EP a request is made to the EALS for support, this may be in the form of advice and guidance, small group support or individual support.  This will depend on the level of need.The supports and resources available from all EA SEND services to support schools in the implementation of the SEN Code of Practice are set out on the EA SEND Plan of Arrangements.  Further details can be viewed here:https://send.eani.org.uk/ea-plan-arrangementsIf any parent or school has a specific query, they can contact the service at any time by e-mailing literacyservice@eani.org.uk or phoning 028 9448 2228.As part of the End to End (E2E) Review of SEN, all pupil support services delivered by the EA are being reviewed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to inform the public about the threats posed by invasive species, including Japanese knotweed.
answer_text: DAERA officials regularly provide online and in-person invasive species training for a variety of organisations, such as NGOs, other government departments, councils, schools, colleges and volunteer groups. 	The Invasive Species Northern Ireland website is managed by Departmental officials. It provides full guidance on confirming species identification, management techniques and legislation. Associated social media streams provide up to date news and information. Posters, guidance leaflets, almost 80 species identification guides and many other useful resources, such as warning signs for giant hogweed, can be downloaded from the website. 		The Department has supported and adopted British Irish Council (BIC) public awareness campaigns such as Check Clean Dry, Be Pet Wise and Be Plant Wise. 		Every year Departmental officials join officials from England, Scotland, Wales, Ireland, Isle of Man and The Channel Isles to take part in the Invasive Species Week event. Throughout the week many projects and awareness campaigns are promoted and highlighted via social media, television and radio. This year, my officials made several appearances in local media talking about a variety of species including the dangers of giant hogweed.	It should be noted that Japanese knotweed was not selected by the European Union as a Species of Union Concern. This means the Department has no authority, under current domestic legislation, to enforce its removal although basic advice is provided.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what engagement he has had with the telecoms industry on improving the telecommunications network in rural areas of Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has allocated £20m to Project Stratum, a £197m project which is successfully delivering giga-bite capable broadband to 85,000 predominantly rural homes across Northern Ireland.  My officials have direct engagement with Fibrus, the service provider for Project Stratum, through the Project's governance and oversight arrangements.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what additional staffing resource has been required to work on the proposed Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill.
answer_text: To date, no additional staff resources have been brought into the Department to work on the Bill. However, an assessment of the work to be undertaken and associated staff resources is currently being undertaken with colleagues across the NICS.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the provision of cycle lanes on the Scullions Road, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: My Department is developing a footway/cycleway scheme on Scullion’s Road and Sandyknowes Roundabout to link Ballyhenry Road with Mullusk. The first phase, which is programmed to start later this year, involves the construction of a segregated cycleway and footway on the existing footway and verge between Mallusk Road and a point 130m south of Sandyknowes Roundabout.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps he is taking to ensure that the School Milk Subsidy Scheme provides non-dairy substitutes for children with a dairy intolerance.
answer_text: The operation of the School Milk Subsidy Scheme is governed by retained EU legislation, which provides that the subsidy is payable for the supply of drinking milk and other specified milk-based products.  Primary legislation would be needed to bring the supply of non-dairy alternatives within the scope of the Scheme, I have no current plans to bring such legislation forward.  Lactose free and lactose reduced milk are permitted within the rules of the Scheme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the plans to upgrade or install new bus shelters in the South Antrim constituency.
answer_text: As noted within my to AQW 2567/22-27, Translink submitted an Economic Appraisal to my Department for the installation of a number of new bus shelters and refurbishment of existing bus shelters across the North including 12 within the South Antrim area. This Economic Appraisal is being considered in line with the necessary approvals process to ensure it represents value for money for the public purse.Subject to approval, the delivery of this project is an operational matter for Translink, and any further queries should, therefore, be directed to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health if he will introduce a scheme to provide free formula feed to mothers living with HIV who have been advised to formula feed exclusively from birth.
answer_text: The Belfast Health & Social Care Trust (BHSCT) currently provides infant formula milk to mothers living with HIV for their baby in the first six months of life. This service is available to mothers living across NI, centrally facilitated through the BHSCT Genito Urinary Medicine (GUM) service.The service provides formula of the mother’s choice and is arranged through the antenatal coordinator who arranges delivery of the formula to the mothers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department is taking to ensure that HIV and sexual health services are (i) fully funded; and (ii) meet the needs of local communities.
answer_text: My Department has been working hard to address the service gaps and pressures within HIV and sexual health services across all 5 Trusts in Northern Ireland.  The PrEP service in NI was established three years ago in 2018/19 with the use of non-recurrent transformation monies into both the Belfast and Western Trust on a pilot basis.  In 2020/21, due to Covid related pressures the Belfast Trust service ceased at the end of March 2020 with staff being redeployed to support COVID-19 related pressures.  As a result, all patients accessing the Belfast Clinic were advised they could be repatriated to their Trust of residence which escalated the strategic regional direction to develop HIV PrEP services locally as a 5 Trust pilot and this approach was widely supported by patients and clinical teams.Learning from both Belfast and Western Trust clinics throughout the pandemic included an acknowledgement that the local lab services struggled to cope with additional PrEP work at a time whenever Covid pressures were rising.  A solution had been secured by accessing lab support services with the online STI Testing Service “SH:24”.  Again, this was funded through non-recurring transformational monies and was deemed as vital in the delivery of both WHSCT and BHSCT PrEP pilot services. Newly developed monitoring reporting indicates that as at 31 March 2022 there were 1,399 patients on PrEP across NI HSC Trusts.And in looking at online STI access as at 31 March 2022 there were 34,854 requests for a range of conditions including HIV, Chlamydia, Gonorrhoea, Syphilis and Hep B& COver 40% of NI Online STI users had never been to a GUM clinic and a further 15% had not been to one for over a year.  This means that the service reached a different group of people than traditional GUM servicesI am pleased to be able to confirm that £1.8m of recurrent funding has been made available in 2022/23 to help address demand for HIV and sexual health services in Northern Ireland.  The aim of this funding is to support PrEP clinics in each Trust and to fund 24/7 access to STI online testing In terms of meeting the needs of local communities my Department draws on various sources to inform improvements in service delivery such as the annual HIV report as produced by PHA; service user engagement through the SHIN (Sexual Health Information Network) and in linking with the staffing groups involved in HIV care including doctors, nursing staff, health advisors, health care support workers, specialist pharmacy staff, social workers, specialist psychology staff and administration staff.  My department also refers to the policy context when considering service gaps and priorities.  These include the Sexual Health Promotion and Action Plan 2008 – 13, 2015 addendum; SHAP (Sexual Health Action Plan) progress report; RQIA report; HIV elimination Plan/UN 95-95-95 as well as the CEDAW (SRH) recommendationsI acknowledge that there has not been a Sexual Health Strategy since the previous strategy

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any planned improvement works on the foot paths on Scullions Road, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: My Department is developing a footway/cycleway scheme on Scullion’s Road and Sandyknowes Roundabout to link Ballyhenry Road with Mallusk. The first phase, which is programmed to start later this year, involves the construction of a segregated cycleway and footway on the existing footway and verge between Mallusk Road and a point 130m south of Sandyknowes Roundabout.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what discussions her Department has had with the Department of Health on preventing cold-related deaths this winter.
answer_text: Officials have been working with colleagues in the Department of Health and other Departments to put in place measures to address this issue in the coming months.  This includes preparations for the severe weather working in conjunction with Housing Executive to support homeless people who are vulnerable to the impact of cold weather through their Emergency 3 Tier Incident Plan which covers incidents such as severe weather.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what recent discussions he has had with his UK Government counterparts on the potential impact of lifting the ban on fracking in England will have on the UK’s target of reaching net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050.
answer_text: There have been no discussions with UK government counterparts on this issue as the decision on fracking applies to England only. It will be up to a Northern Ireland Executive Committee to determine local onshore petroleum licensing policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what estimate her Department has made of the number and proportion of eligible households that did not receive Pension Credit in the 2021/22 financial year.
answer_text: My Department has published experimental statistics on the uptake of Pension Credit.  The latest publication was released on 01 September 2022 and provides estimates for the 2019/20 financial year.  This publication can be accessed at Pension Credit: Estimates of Benefit Take-up - 2019/20 | Department for Communities (communities-ni.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) how many full-time equivalents are employed by his Department at each civil service grade; and (ii) for each grade what proportion are (a) women; (b) people from ethnic minority backgrounds; and (c) disabled.
answer_text: The number of Full Time Equivalents (FTE) employed in DAERA at each Civil Service Grade and the proportion of women, people from ethnic minority background and disabled are shown in the table below.GradeFTEFemale FTE   (%)Ethnic minority background FTE      (%) 1Disabled FTE (%) 2G5+2930.000G62835.70*G727247.104.8DP49244.1*4.3SO71445.0*5.6EOI54334.305.3EOII41147.0*7.1AO41267.71.212.1AA6652.2*12.1Industrial18712.40#Total315444.40.56.2FootnotesEthnic origin data is missing for 241 DAERA staff	Any staff for whom disability information is missing (58% of DAERA staff at 1 January 2022) have been allocated to the ‘No disability declared’ category. 	* Number of cases too small to publish percentage (i.e. below 5).# Percentage has been suppressed, to avoid disclosing another number that is too small to publish.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many pensioners were in absolute poverty in 2020/21; and (ii) what assessment has her Department made on the number of pensioners who might be living in poverty in 2022/23.
answer_text: (i) Official measures of absolute poverty are derived from the Family Resources Survey (FRS). Absolute poverty can be presented on a before and after housing costs basis. Data collection in 2020/21 was impacted by COVID-19 restrictions resulting in a heavily reduced sample size. This has led to additional uncertainty around the estimates, particularly for smaller groups. As such, for 2020/21, only figures relating to the whole population are published. In 2019/20 there were estimated to be 40,000 pensioners living in absolute poverty before housing costs, and 29,000 pensioners in absolute poverty after housing costs.(ii) Official estimates of poverty for 2022/23 will be released in Spring 2024.Further information regarding the above statistics can be found at the link below.Northern Ireland Poverty Bulletin 2019-20 | Department for Communities (https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-poverty-bulletin-2019-20)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department is taking to prevent cold-related deaths this winter.
answer_text: The link between living in cold, damp conditions and a number of illnesses, including poor mental health, respiratory disease, and premature mortality has long been accepted. Cold homes can cause excess deaths and exacerbate many illnesses, in addition to potentially delaying recovery from serious conditions. Cold housing also increases the risk of accidents and injuries in the home. In addition, low home temperatures can also impact on infants and children, and those living with chronic conditions such as osteoarthritis, neurological conditions, stroke and dementia, respiratory and cardiovascular disease.The Affordable Warmth Scheme is the Executive’s main Scheme to target and assist low income owner-occupiers and householders who rent their home from a private landlord who are most at risk of fuel poverty. The Department for Communities announced changes to the Affordable Warmth Scheme eligibility criteria, to take effect from July 2021, in order to help more households who are in fuel poverty, by increasing the income threshold and by dis-applying certain disability benefits from the calculation of income. The Public Health Agency (PHA) contributes to a cross sectoral, multi-disciplinary approach across a number of organisations and divisions which supports the NICE Guidance on ‘Excess winter deaths and morbidity and the health risks associated with cold homes’. The guidelines offer strategic and practical recommendations for health and social care and other community organisations, to support vulnerable people who have health, housing or economic circumstances that increase their risk of harm.In addition, the PHA funded regional Keep Warm Pack scheme was developed to provide immediate, short-term support to help those most vulnerable and at risk of fuel poverty to stay warmer at home during cold weather. The scheme is co-ordinated by the PHA with the support of a range of local government, Health and Social Care Trusts, and community and voluntary partners. Over the past five years a total of 40,145 packs have been distributed to those most in need. This winter, it is anticipated that circa 8225 packs will be distributed across NI.The PHA is also responsible for the implementation of the annual winter vaccination programme throughout the season, which includes both flu and COVID-19 booster vaccinations this year. This includes a mass media advertising campaign promoting the importance of getting vaccinated.  The PHA is currently urging everyone who falls into an eligible group to get the vaccinated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the legislation, that falls under his Department’s remit, that will be subject to revocation or reform under the proposed Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill.
answer_text: The Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill was introduced on 22 September 2022.Officials are assessing the legislation that may fall under my Department’s remit that will be subject to revocation or reform under the Bill.  Initial estimates are that over 600 separate pieces of legislation could be affected by the Bill and the exercise will therefore take some time to complete but I can assure the Member that it is being conducted as expeditiously as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the legislation, that falls under his Department’s remit, that will be subject to revocation or reform under the proposed Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill.
answer_text: The Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill was laid in Westminster on 22 September 2022. My officials are currently developing a list of Retained EU Law (REUL) captured by the scope of the Bill that fall under my departmental remit.  To date approximately 500 pieces of REUL have been identified across roads, transport, railways, water & drainage and planning.  Work is ongoing to finalise a comprehensive list.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact of the increase in household bills on pet welfare.
answer_text: I recognise the significant financial pressures households currently face and the potential for this to impact on pet welfare. My Department has ongoing engagement with animal welfare organisations and local councils. This will ensure that it keeps abreast of any animal welfare issues that may arise as a result of rising household bills.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what discussions she has had with the Chancellor of the Exchequer on ensuring households in Northern Ireland can access financial support to improve (i) insulation; and (ii) the energy efficiency of houses.
answer_text: My Department continues to deliver the Affordable Warmth Scheme which is targeted at low income households most at risk of fuel poverty in the owner occupied and private rented sectors.  This Scheme provides grant aid to improve energy efficiency measures within a property including loft and cavity wall insulation, heating and windows replacement. My officials continue to engage with DfE officials who are leading on plans for the delivery of a multi-year energy efficiency programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what engagement he has had with his UK counterparts on the Global Ocean Alliance 30by30 initiative; and (ii) what assurance he received from the UK Government that it will deliver on the commitments made under this initiative.
answer_text: I have had no direct engagement with my UK counterparts regarding the Global Ocean Alliance 30by30 initiative.My officials are in regular communication with Defra officials regarding preparations for COP15, and in leading the Global Ocean Alliance 30by30 initiative, the UK is playing a leading role in negotiating for the target of at least 30% of the global ocean under protection by 2030.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans his Department has made to ensure Northern Ireland’s participation in COP15 in October 2022.
answer_text: The second part of COP15, the Conference of the Parties to the UN Convention on Biological Diversity, will now take place 7-19 December 2022 in Montreal, Canada.  A new post-2020 Global Biodiversity Framework to safeguard nature will be finalised and adopted at the conference, setting outcome goals and related action targets to reverse biodiversity loss by 2030 and achieve the 2050 vision of “living in harmony with nature”.  		DEFRA leads for the UK at the pre-COP working groups, with support from the Joint Nature Conservation Committee (JNCC), the advisor to the Government on nature conservation. There is ongoing liaison between DEFRA, my officials and other devolved administrations to formulate the proposed UK position at COP15.		My officials are awaiting further details on the programme of events and UK representation at the conference.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what engagement his Department has had with the Task Force on Displacement, established in 2015 at COP21.
answer_text: My Department has not had any engagement with the Task Force on Displacement which was established at COP21. Displacement is not a policy area my Department is responsible for.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to ensure that Northern Ireland makes a fair contribution to the climate finance commitments made at COP26.
answer_text: The international climate finance commitments made at COP26 are being taken forward by the UK Government, as the Signatory Party to the COP, through the pre-existing UK International Climate Finance (ICF) support mechanism. As such my Department while supplying the objective will take its lead from His Majesty’s Government.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to ensure that Northern Ireland makes a contribution to the delivery of the commitments made by the UK Government in the Global Ocean Alliance 30by30 initiative.
answer_text: My Department is currently developing the new Biodiversity Strategy for Northern Ireland through a co-design process with stakeholders.  One of the key targets in this strategy is that by 2030 at least 30% globally of land areas and sea areas are conserved through effectively and equitably managed, ecologically representative and well-connected systems of protected areas and other effective area-based conservation measure and integrated into the wider landscapes and seascapes. Currently, there are 48 Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) which occupy 38% of the Northern Ireland inshore region and this has been assessed as very close to reaching the objective of delivering an ecologically coherent network in Northern Ireland. My Department has developed fisheries management measures for MPAs in the Northern Ireland inshore region and is introducing legislation to bring these measures into effect.  The implementation of these will see further protection for key features within these sites. A review of the Marine Protected Areas Strategy for Northern Ireland has also commenced through a facilitated co-design process with key stakeholders, and this will explore options for the inclusion of Highly Protected Marine Areas (HPMAs) and Other Effective area-based Conservation Measures (OECMs) within the Northern Ireland inshore region.  It is anticipated that the consultation of the MPA Strategy will take place during 2023.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what engagement he has had with local Councils in relation to animal welfare issues caused by unexpected firework noise and pollution.
answer_text: My Department engages regularly with local councils in relation to matters of animal welfare.  This provides an opportunity for discussion on any issues that may arise from the use of fireworks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what engagement he has had with animal welfare charities in relation to animal welfare issues caused by unexpected firework noise and pollution.
answer_text: At this time of year, my Department issues guidance on the steps that can be taken to minimise the impact firework activity can have on animals. Its messaging is, where possible, co-ordinated with that of animal welfare charities. Engagement is ongoing between my Department and the Ulster Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals to co-ordinate the messaging issued to the public this Halloween.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs in relation to the distress and anxiety firework noise and pollution causes to animals, what consideration has the Minister given to developing a public awareness campaign on the safe use of fireworks.
answer_text: My Department undertakes a campaign annually, via social media, in the lead up to Halloween to raise awareness of the impact firework noise can have on pets and provide guidance on how to minimise any distress and anxiety that may be caused to them.  My Department will be proceeding with messaging for this year’s campaign in the coming weeks.The Department of Justice is the licensing authority for those seeking to use fireworks in Northern Ireland.  Information and guidance on the safe use of fireworks is available on the NI Direct website.  This includes the Firework Safety Code which advises those using fireworks to keep pets indoors.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the level of funding and support provided by her Department for the preservation and restoration of heritage sites.
answer_text: My Department supports heritage sites, both through directly caring for the 186 State Care Monuments under the ownership or guardianship of the Department and through external funding schemes for heritage.  The external funding for heritage sites available from my Department in 2022/23 is set out at: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/topics/historic-environment/historic-environment-funding-grants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans his Department has made to ensure Northern Ireland's participation in COP27.
answer_text: Following on from COP26 Officials in my Department have continued to engage with the UK Government and other Devolved Administrations in relation to planning for COP27 to ensure that Northern Ireland continues to support the UK Government and is factored into their delegation planning.The UK Government has indicated that it does not expect any new or significant announcements to be made at COP27 with a focus towards the delivery of existing agreements.  As such a significantly lower attendance of world leaders is expected at COP27 than attended COP26.  Considering these factors, in combination with consideration of the associated emissions and costs of travel, I have requested that my Department explores attendance in a modest capacity. Officials in my Department will be engaging with other Departments to establish their interest in attendance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department has taken to help address the impact of rising fertiliser prices on food production.
answer_text: Northern Ireland producers are facing unprecedented rises in input costs, in particular fertiliser.  We continue to monitor the impacts on farm businesses and have a range of mitigation actions in place.   		The ‘Rising Cost Taskforce’ (led by College of Agriculture Food and Rural Enterprise with cross industry representation) continues to ensure the consistency of advisory messages and provides practical help and support.  It has recently completed a follow-up survey of farm businesses and the information gathered is helping to inform targeted messaging over the winter months.  Further practical information sessions to consider the continuing increasing input costs will be delivered by my officials over the winter months. 		I recognise that there continues to be volatility in the market and the overall situation remains difficult given the scale of the input cost rises.  To help assist our local agri-industry with cash flow, I issued full Direct Payments of £286.9million to 97% of eligible applicants, 6 weeks earlier from 1 September 2022.		I am encouraged by the very successful uptake of the £45 million Soil Nutrient Health Scheme that I launched earlier this year. The scheme will provide nutrient information to farmers on every field they farm and will ensure better matching of nutrient inputs to crop needs and will raise the quantity and quality of grass, which remains the cheapest form of feed.  		My Officials continue to have significant ongoing engagement with industry stakeholders, Defra, other devolved administrations and the Department of Agriculture, Food and Marine (ROI) on those cross-cutting issues relating to reducing the impacts of volatility in price and supply of key industry inputs. 		I continue to engage regularly with UK Government at a senior level and drive home the importance of national collaboration on dealing with the impacts of the Ukraine crisis on our agri-food industry and future food security.  I have written to and met with the former Secretary of State, Rt Hon George Eustice MP on a number of occasions to reiterate my concerns regarding the unprecedented increases in input costs which is impacting the agricultural sector across all of the UK.  I have restated those concerns to the new Secretary of State for DEFRA, Ranil Jayawardena MP and requested an urgent meeting.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what assessment her Department has made on the adequacy of the powers of the Child Maintenance Service to help prevent financial coercion through the non-payment of child maintenance.
answer_text: The Child Maintenance Service does not have specific powers relating to financial coercion.  If however, a Receiving Parent is not receiving the full maintenance payments they are entitled to, the case can be moved from the Direct Pay service to the Collect and Pay service. The Child Maintenance Service can then use administrative and legal powers available, up to and including enforcement powers to secure payment.		The Child Maintenance Service constantly reviews and improves its processes and services, including the approach it takes to supporting customers who are experiencing or have experienced domestic abuse, including financial coercion. The Child Maintenance Service provides mandatory domestic abuse awareness training to all staff, and caseworkers are trained to be aware of, and if possible, identify any instances of abuse and signpost these customers to specialist domestic abuse organisations. This is supported through additional guidance related to domestic abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) when the COP26 Devolved Administrations Ministerial Group last met; and (ii) did he attend this meeting.
answer_text: The COP26 Devolved Administrations Ministerial Group last met on 9 February 2022. I can confirm that I attended that meeting.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assessment his Department has made on the length of waiting times for female domestic abuse survivors needing to access specialist mental health support.
answer_text: Mental health support in Northern Ireland is available to anyone who needs it based on clinical need. All who need care and treatment, which may include female domestic abuse survivors, will be provided with services that are clinically appropriate in line with the regional stepped care model.It is recognised that mental health services are under considerable pressure and some people have to wait long periods of time for access to these services.  The Mental Health Strategy 2021–31 therefore introduced 35 actions to reform our mental health services. It is hoped that full delivery of the strategy will mean waiting times can be reduced and people can have easier access to services when they need it. The regional Domestic and Sexual Abuse Helpline is also available to support the mental wellbeing of survivors of domestic abuse. The Helpline is a confidential, Freephone service, funded jointly by my Department, the Department of Justice and the Department for Communities. Fully trained Helpline staff are there to listen and offer emotional support 24 hours a day, 365 days per year. It is important that those affected by domestic abuse reach out and seek help from support services. My Department continues to raise awareness and work is underway to develop a new Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy following the completion of a Call for Views exercise earlier this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to address staffing shortages in the veterinary sector.
answer_text: There is currently a shortage of vets within my Department and more widely, in both the private and public sectors, across all of the United Kingdom.  I am aware of the difficulties veterinary employers in the local agri-food sector, including my own Department, have encountered and continue to experience, recruiting veterinary staff with the necessary skills for this work. It is not within the gift of the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to address this shortage across the whole veterinary sector, although I am pleased that veterinarians have now been added to the UK Government’s Shortage Occupation List, facilitating their recruitment from outside the UK.  The Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons (RCVS), which is responsible for the veterinary profession, held a Workforce Summit in November 2021 looking at potential solutions to the workforce crisis and subsequently released a report in February 2022, which identified six key themes around the issue of workforce shortages. My officials have been engaging with the RCVS in their work addressing the specific areas of recruitment and retention of vets across the UK.However, there is an increasing acceptance that Northern Ireland requires a more assured supply of veterinarians in the long term, than is available from historical sources.  There are a number of options for achieving this which merit further exploration, and last year I commissioned, from the Strategic Investment Board (SIB), a Baseline Report and Situational Analysis of Veterinary Services in Northern Ireland.  The SIB has completed their report, which is being actively considered. In the meantime, my priority is ensuring we have sufficient vets to carry-out the vital government veterinary services in our meat processing plants, in our field offices delivering disease control programmes, and providing support to the agri-food industry. My Officials continue to explore all available avenues to recruit vets, including taking on permanent veterinary staff through NICS Recruitment and temporary vets through the agencies on the NICS Agency Framework.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the establishment of the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture, as required by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 does not set a timescale for establishing the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture. The purpose of the Fund will be to provide advice and financial assistance to the agriculture sector to help it deliver its contribution under proposals and policies which are included within Climate Action Plans. The first priority therefore is to develop and agree those policies and proposals. We must not simply rush into starting a Fund without understanding what areas need support in transitioning. Some actions may be financially positive for the agriculture sector and therefore it is important to target funding at the areas where transition will be more difficult or costly. So, while this is an area which is being considered by my officials, it needs to be developed and considered in the context of delivering some of the other key requirements of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022. It is important to highlight that my Department has provided funding support to the agricultural sector over many years, to assist in the transition towards lower emission farming practices and the sequestration of carbon through various schemes including the Farm Business Improvement Scheme (FBIS), Environmental Farming Scheme (EFS) and the Countryside Management Scheme. Further to this, I have launched the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme and am supporting a range of further initiatives through the Green Growth strategy. This type of support will continue to be a priority for my Department as it delivers its functions under the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the establishment of the independent office, known as the Northern Ireland Climate Commissioner, as required by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: As stipulated in section 50 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, responsibility for the establishment of the independent office, known as the Northern Ireland Climate Commissioner, rests with the Executive Office (TEO).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the establishment of the Just Transition Commission, as required by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: While the Act does not specify a timeframe for establishing the Just Transition Commission, I have identified this as a priority for my Department. Work has commenced on the complex process of establishing a Just Transition Commission for Northern Ireland. My Officials are currently identifying what type of public body the Commission would be in order to identify the correct process to establish it and how to best support the work of the Commission, for example with a Departmental-based secretariat. Engagement is also taking place with other departments and representatives involved in other just transition commissions to learn from their experiences. I hope you find this information useful.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when his Department intends to launch a public consultation on the carbon budgets, as required by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: My Officials have commenced work to set the first three 5-year carbon budgets for Northern Ireland. They have formally written to, and have met with, the UK Climate Change Committee to seek advice on the first three carbon budgets and this engagement process is ongoing.In line with the requirements of the Act, my Department will carry out a public consultation of not less than 16 weeks on the proposed carbon budgets. I aim to be in a position to consult on the first three carbon budgets over the second quarter of 2023.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what consultation his Department has conducted with stakeholders on the development of the Clean Air Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Stakeholder consultation has been central to the development of the Clean Air Strategy for Northern Ireland. 		My Department launched a Discussion Document for public consultation from 23rd November 2020 to 15th February 2021, in advance of developing the first Clean Air Strategy for Northern Ireland. The Discussion Document presented evidence and research on a range of ambient air pollutants. It also outlined current policy and legislation and the measures currently in place to control air pollution. The Discussion Document posed questions around pollutant source activities, with the aim of promoting discussion and the exchange of ideas. Views were sought from all interested stakeholders. 		The Department published the synopsis of consultation responses to the Clean Air Strategy Discussion Document in June 2022. Comments received have formed the basis of the actions proposed in the strategy.		A cross Departmental stakeholder group, the Clean Air Strategy Inter Departmental Steering Group, has now been established and comprises of officials from DAERA, Department for Infrastructure, Department for the Economy and the Department of Health. Other Departments may need to be involved depending on the final agreed scope of the strategy. The group will meet regularly over the coming months to agree the Clean Air Strategy actions.   		A public consultation on the draft Northern Ireland Clean Air Strategy is planned for the end of December 2022. In order to proceed, this will require approval from the NI Executive.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what consultation his Department has conducted with stakeholders on the Biodiversity Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Biodiversity Strategy for Northern Ireland is being developed through a co-design process of engagement with a wide range of key stakeholders. The department has engaged with government departments, councils and other public bodies. Organisations representing marine and land users as well as environmental non-governmental organisations have been engaged over the past few months. The initial feedback from these discussions will inform two larger stakeholder events planned for October, where we will work with stakeholders to create and refine Northern Ireland specific targets that will align with the outcomes required by the Convention of Biological Diversity.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 797/22-27, (i) whether he anticipates the implementation of the Peatland Strategy will require cross-Departmental approval; and (ii) what impact the absence of a functioning Executive will have on the rollout of the associated Action Plan.
answer_text: The publication of the Northern Ireland Peatland Strategy will require Executive approval.  An Implementation Plan for the Strategy is in the early stages of development. Whilst it is anticipated that many of the actions in the draft plan could progress in the absence of the Executive, certain actions are likely to require cross Government agreement to implement.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what engagement his Department has had with the Cabinet Office regarding the ‘Retained EU law dashboard’; and (ii) whether his Department has submitted a request for the dashboard to allow users to filter legislation by devolved administrations.
answer_text: The Executive Office acts as the interlocuter between NICS Departments and the Cabinet Office on matters concerning Retained EU Law.		It has not been necessary to submit a request for the dashboard to allow users to filter legislation by devolved administrations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Clean Air Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Stakeholder consultation has been central to the development of the Clean Air Strategy for Northern Ireland. 		My Department launched a Discussion Document for public consultation from 23rd November 2020 to 15th February 2021, in advance of developing the first Clean Air Strategy for Northern Ireland. The Discussion Document presented evidence and research on a range of ambient air pollutants. It also outlined current policy and legislation and the measures currently in place to control air pollution. The Discussion Document posed questions around pollutant source activities, with the aim of promoting discussion and the exchange of ideas. Views were sought from all interested stakeholders. 			The Department published the synopsis of consultation responses to the Clean Air Strategy Discussion Document in June 2022. Comments received have formed the basis of the actions proposed in the strategy.		A cross Departmental stakeholder group, the Clean Air Strategy Inter Departmental Steering Group, has now been established and comprises of officials from DAERA, Department for Infrastructure, Department for the Economy and the Department of Health. Other Departments may need to be involved depending on the final agreed scope of the strategy. The group will meet regularly over the coming months to agree the Clean Air Strategy actions.   		A public consultation on the draft Northern Ireland Clean Air Strategy is planned for the end of December 2022. In order to proceed, this will require approval from the NI Executive.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department or the Education Authority has considered placing a Specialist Education Unit in Crumlin Integrated College and if this has been considered, to provide a copy of the report outlining the decision.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has statutory responsibility for the planning of Special Educational Needs (SEN) provision.As part of its wider Area Planning strategy, the EA developed and published SEN Area Planning Frameworks for Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (SPiMS) and Special Schools.  When considering schools for establishment of specialist provision, the EA will seek to ensure that they satisfy the six criteria of the Schools for the Future: A Policy for Sustainable Schools (SSP), allowing for individual circumstances where appropriate.  Accordingly the relevant criteria from the SSP have been reflected in this Framework.The EA has confirmed that the SEN Placements Team has established a number of SPiM classes in order to provide a sufficient number of SPiM places for pupils with a Statement of SEN.These strategic Frameworks support the implementation of the new 5-year Special Education Strategic Area Plan (“Planning for Special Education Provision” 2022-2027) (SESAP) and related Operational Plan (OP) activities and all documents can be viewed using the following links to the EA's website. SPiMS: Framework for Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (eani.org.uk)Special Schools: Framework for Special Schools (eani.org.uk)Special Education Strategic Area Plan 2022-2017Operational Plan 1 2022-2024 I would expect any SPiMS provision proposed by the EA to be considered in accordance with the criteria and associated indicators in the SPiMS SEN Area Planning Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, when her Department or the Education Authority decides to close a rural primary or post-primary school, is that body required to produce an impact assessment on the rural area/community.
answer_text: On publication of a Development Proposal (DP) and where rural considerations are engaged, the Education Authority (EA) as part of its assessment of evidence confirms that they have carried out a Rural Needs Assessment.  The EA sets out its analysis of suitable, alternative, good-quality rural provision.  Advice provided to inform a decision takes account of any pertinent statutory duties and, for smaller schools which tend to be located in rural areas, due regard is given to the requirements of the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016.  Rural proofing has been a requirement for all Government departments in Northern Ireland since 2002 and has been an integral part of the Area Planning process.  In 2016, the commitment to rural proofing was strengthened with the introduction of the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016.  The Act places a duty on Government departments to have due regard to rural needs when developing, adopting, implementing or revising policies, strategies and plans and when designing and delivering public services.  A Rural Needs Impact Assessment is recommended in the Department of Environment, Agriculture and Rural Affairs (DEARA) guidance but not a statutory requirement.“Schools for the Future: A Policy for sustainable schools” - the Sustainable Schools Policy (SSP) is the cornerstone of the Area Planning process.  The SSP recognises the needs of rural communities and this is reflected in:-a lower enrolment threshold for primary schools serving rural areas (minimum enrolment of 105 compared to 140 for urban areas);an accessibility criterion which provides guidance on home to school transport times; anda “strong links with the community” criterion recognises the central place a school has for many communities, both urban and rural.I would like to see vibrant sustainable rural education provision, this will require genuine collaboration across all sectors.  I have specifically asked my officials to develop a programme to support sustainable education provision in rural areas. This is outlined in my Written Statement to the Assembly in August 2021.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what assessment his Department has made of the cost to residential leaseholders in Northern Ireland of ensuring tall buildings meet (i) building; and (ii) fire safety regulations following the implementation of the Fire Safety Act 2021.
answer_text: The Fire Safety Act 2021 applies in England and Wales only. The equivalent Fire Safety Regulations for buildings in operation here are the responsibility of the Department of Health, which has advised that the costs to residential leaseholders is not its responsibility.  My Department is responsible for the local Building Regulations. Building Regulations do not apply to existing ‘tall buildings’ unless the requirements in building regulations are triggered by alteration or extension to the existing building. Building regulations will apply to any newly erected tall building and also any tall building created as a result of a material change of use.Implementation of the Fire Safety Act 2021 will have no impact on ‘tall buildings’ meeting building regulations here (including the fire safety standards contained therein). Hence an assessment of cost to residential leaseholders has not been necessary here.On 1 April 2022 the Department amended Building Regulations introducing the requirement that combustible materials cannot be used on the external walls of certain tall buildings. This so called ‘ban on combustible materials’ replicated similar bans introduced in England and Wales. A Regulatory Impact Assessment (RIA) for the new requirement was compiled which estimated the total costs to industry rather than attributing these.  The RIA highlights modest costs to industry and to district councils responsible for enforcing building regulation requirements and are available here: Consultation on amendments to The Building (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2022 | Department of Finance (finance-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what recent steps her Department has taken to address sexual harassment in the hospitality sector.
answer_text: My Department has responsibility for legislation on gender discrimination and sexual harassment in the workplace, but these provisions are not specific to the hospitality sector and compliance with the legislation is a matter for individual organisations.Investigations of sexual harassment complaints in the workplace fall to industrial tribunals or the Equality Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department plans to change the Northern Ireland planning system to mitigate fire risk in the context of the Grenfell disaster.
answer_text: A Building Safety Expert Panel, led by Department of Finance, is progressing work to ensure that citizens here are afforded the same level of fire safety in all high rise residential buildings, as in the other devolved administrations of the UK. My officials are fully involved in the considerations of the Expert Panel, including the implications for the planning process and other key regulatory regimes, such as building regulation. I am determined that the planning system will play its part in this important work and this will become clearer as the Panel progress and finalise their work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in context of the cost of living crisis, what measures are being taken to tackle rural poverty.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, with reference to the Montreal Convention, what consultation he has had with the Secretary of State for Transport on increasing the amount of compensation a disabled person can claim for damaged mobility equipment whilst travelling on British airlines.
answer_text: Civil Aviation policy and regulation in the UK is largely a reserved matter which rests with the Department for Transport (DfT) and the Civil Aviation Authority (CAA).  Under the Montreal Convention 1999 (the Convention), airlines are liable for any damage to mobility equipment, including wheelchairs.  My Department has no power to remove the maximum limit set by this Convention and it is also outside the scope of consumer protection law.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what recent consultation he has had with airports in Northern Ireland in regard to improving the travel experience for disabled passengers.
answer_text: Civil Aviation policy and regulation in the UK is largely a reserved matter which rests with the Department for Transport (DfT) and the Civil Aviation Authority (CAA).  However, the Consumer Council for Northern Ireland (CCNI), an Arm’s Length Body of my Department, works with Belfast International Airport, George Best Belfast City Airport and City of Derry Airport to facilitate their Accessibility Forums. The Accessibility Forums are attended by a range of organisations that represent consumers with a disability or reduced mobility. Meetings of the Forums held in the three airports are used to audit the special assistance provided to passengers and help airports gain a better understanding of the issues faced by passengers requiring special assistance. The audits identify areas where special assistance works well and provides the opportunity to consider and implement recommendations made by Accessibility Forum members.   Any recommendations for improvement to services or facilities made by CCNI are followed up with progress reports from the airports at regular meetings of the Forum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 583/22-27, whether the Northern Ireland Environment Agency tested only the water for pollution or whether adjacent land was also tested for contaminants.
answer_text: In order to assist Antrim and Newtownabbey Council with their enquiry following the dogs’ deaths, water samples were taken to investigate the possible presence of harmful algae and a broad sweep of organic substances. As the focus of the investigation concentrated on the water environment, no samples were taken on the adjacent land which would be considered outside of the remit of NIEA Water Management Unit.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what temporary variations have been awarded in Locality North, broken down by (i) school; (ii) sector; and (iii) the number of pupils for each year, for the past five years.
answer_text: The Temporary Variation (TV) information requested for schools in Locality North, each year, for the past five years has been placed in the Assembly Library.Locality North consists of District Councils: Causeway Coast and GlensMid & East AntrimDerry City and StrabaneAntrim and Newtownabbey

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any proposed investment in foot path provision on the Glenavy Road between Crumlin and Glenavy.
answer_text: I am pleased to be able to confirm that design work to provide a cycle footway on the Glenavy Road between Crumlin and Glenavy is currently underway. Subject to the successful acquisition of land and availability of finance, the scheme is provisionally programmed for delivery in the 2023/24 financial year. Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many pupils currently attend (i) primary and (ii) post-primary schools in Locality North who do not qualify for a bus pass because they do not attend their closest school of type, broken down by school, for each year for the past five years.
answer_text: The Education Authority only assesses whether a pupil qualifies for transport assistance as part of the application process and therefore data is only available in relation to pupils who have applied for transport. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps his Department is taking to improve footpath provision on the Hillhead Road, Ballyclare.

If it meets the criteria scheme proposals will be prioritised and considered for possible inclusion in a future works programme.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 29742/17-22, to provide an update on the installation and refurbishment of bus shelters in South Antrim.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many (i) bus stations; and (ii) rail stations within the South Antrim constituency do not have tactile paving for blind and partially sighted passengers.
answer_text: The design, construction and maintenance of bus and rail stations is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question in relation to tactile paving at stations within the South Antrim constituency to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on the delivery of the part-time 20mph speed limit programme for 2021-22.
answer_text: Phase 1 of the part time 20mph zones project comprised of 352 signs at 103 schools.  All 352 signs have been installed and commissioned.Phase 2 of the project comprises of 377 signs covering a further 112 schools.  To date, 119 of the 377 signs have been installed and commissioned.  It is planned that 270 signs will be commissioned by the end of August, with the remaining signs completed in the early autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 19687/17-22, to provide an update on his Department’s plans to estimate the carbon stocks stored in (i) grasslands soils; and (ii) above ground biomass, across Northern Ireland’s farmed landscape.
answer_text: In March of this year, I was pleased to launch the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme (SNHS) for Northern Ireland. This is an innovative soil sampling and carbon analysis scheme. The SNHS contains a number of elements including baselining the carbon stocks in farmed soils and above ground biomass (AGB) across Northern Ireland. The SNHS will be rolled out in stages using a zonal approach, over the next four years. My Department opened the scheme to farmers in the first (Zone 1) of four zones on 27 May 2022. Following full roll out in year four (2026), baseline estimates of the carbon stocks in farmed soils and AGB will be available at NI level, and have been provided at farm level to participating farmers. In addition to the SNHS, I launched a Dairy Carbon Audits programme in conjunction with the dairy companies, led by the Dairy Council Northern Ireland and CAFRE. Details of this pilot were announced at The Winter Fair in December 2021. Initial progress has been positive and I look forward to the initial findings that will inform both the dairy sector and DAERA on future policy decisions in this area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 16819/17-22, to provide an update on the feasibility of providing parklets in Ballyclare.
answer_text: Parklets are council led initiatives, in which my Department acts as a consultee and facilitator.  It is the responsibility of the respective council to consider the feasibility of and gauge the level of local stakeholder support for each parklet application.  The Department will then be consulted on the proposal and assist with their implementation, where practicable.To date my Department has not received any applications for the installation of parklets from Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on the feasibility study for the Doagh to Larne greenway.
answer_text: The greenway route from Doagh to Larne received a small grant from my Department for a feasibility study in 2017 followed by a detailed design in 2019.  Greenway projects are Council led projects, so responsibility for this project rests with Antrim and Newtownabbey Council.I am very keen to increase active travel opportunities that will encourage a safer, cleaner travel culture and make a real difference to people’s lives.  I am committed to working with all Council areas in identifying and delivering active travel and greenway projects for 2022/23. I have written to councils inviting them to submit proposals for support for greenway and active travel schemes that will be ready to commence this year, with a view to my Department providing up to 50% funding towards the capital costs of greenways and 100% for Active Travel projects in 2022-23.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps she is taking to support refugee children with special educational needs in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department provides funding to schools with newcomer pupils and to the Education Authority’s Intercultural Education Service (IES) which supports schools, newcomer pupils and their families, including children from the Refugee community.Asylum Seeker and Refugee (ASR) children and young people are eligible for school places and statutory assessment in the same way as all other pupils. The IES provides advice, guidance and support to ASR families when contacted by their accommodation providers or support services. Where it is felt that a child’s special educational needs are such that they cannot effectively be met within the resources ordinarily available to a school, the EA may decide to conduct a Statutory Assessment as provided for within The Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many primary schools in the South Antrim constituency accessed (i) the Active School Travel Programme; and (ii) the Cycling Proficiency Scheme, in the last year.
answer_text: My Department does not hold participation figures for Active School Travel Programme and Cycling Proficiency Scheme on a constituency level but rather as a whole across the North.The most recent set of participation figures of schools are for the financial year 2021/22 and are detailed below:FIGURES FOR 2021-2022Cycling Proficiency Scheme252Active School Travel Programme209

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many asylum seekers have been eligible to access general practice services in Northern Ireland in the last year.
answer_text: A person who is not ordinarily resident is a visitor and access to healthcare in Northern Ireland is provided under the Health and Personal Social Services Provision of Health Services to Persons Not Ordinarily Resident Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 (“the Regulations”).  The Regulations provide that some categories of visitor are exempt from charge.  The categories of visitor that are exempt from charge include asylum seekers therefore all asylum seekers are entitled to access publicly funded healthcare in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how many refugee children have been placed in contingency accommodation in Northern Ireland in the last year.
answer_text: Issues around immigration and asylum are excepted matters and do not fall within my Department’s remit.  My Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many refugee children have been eligible to access school education in Northern Ireland in the last year.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the adequacy of accommodation available to refugees with disabilities in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Executive Office have strategic responsibility for refugees and asylum seekers and coordinate our response with the Home Office.My Department is responsible for the operational delivery of specific refugee resettlement programmes such as the former Syrian Vulnerable Persons Resettlement Scheme, the Afghan Relocations and Assistance Policy and the Afghan Citizens Resettlement Scheme.  The Housing Executive inspects all properties made available under these schemes to ensure they are of an appropriate standard and size prior to their being offered.  Where the Housing Executive is made aware in advance that an individual has a disability, that also forms part of the initial matching process.  Where, after arrival, accommodation is found to be unsuitable for any reason all possible measures are taken to address these concerns as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the mechanisms her Department uses to cooperate with the Home Office on monitoring devolved services for asylum seekers.
answer_text: Issues around immigration, including accommodation and support for asylum seekers, are excepted matters and do not fall within my Department’s remit.  The Executive Office has strategic responsibility for interactions with the Home Office and chairs the Strategic Planning Group (SPG) on Refugees and Asylum Seekers. Officials from my Department attend the SPG.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department has received guidance from the Home Office in respect to operating the full asylum dispersal system in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Issues around immigration and asylum are excepted matters and do not fall within my Department’s remit.  The Executive Office (TEO) have strategic responsibility for refugees and asylum seekers and coordinate our regional response with the Home Office. Officials in TEO are currently engaging with Home Office on the implementation of this policy and will continue to engage with all relevant departments through the Strategic Planning Group on Refugees and Asylum Seekers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps her Department is taking to support asylum seekers who raise complaints concerning poor housing conditions to the accommodation provider.
answer_text: Issues around immigration, including accommodation and support for asylum seekers, are excepted matters and do not fall within my Department’s remit.The Home Office has contracted Mears to provide accommodation for asylum seekers locally.  This contract falls outside my Department’s control. My Department funds Housing Rights, a registered charity, to provide housing advice and assistance to members of the public, including asylum seekers.The Law Centre is funded to provide a range of advice and information services including advice on complex legal issues that anyone, including asylum seekers, migrants or refugees, can avail off at no cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the adequacy of the Northern Ireland Strategic Migration Partnership's role of promoting partnership between the Home Office and devolved administrations, with reference to the Independent Chief Inspector of Borders & Immigration: An inspection of contingency asylum accommodation report.
answer_text: Issues around immigration and asylum seekers, are excepted matters and do not fall within my Department’s remit.  The Executive Office (TEO) has strategic responsibility for refugees and asylum seekers and coordinate our regional response with the Home Office.The Strategic Migration Partnership is managed by TEO.
