question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to secure an endometriosis specialist in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, as recommended by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence.
answer_text: The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence Recommendations state that specialist endometriosis services (endometriosis centres) should have access to:Gynaecologists with expertise in diagnosing and managing endometriosis, including advanced laparoscopic surgical skillsA colorectal surgeon with an interest in endometriosisA urologist with an interest in endometriosisAn endometriosis specialist nurseA multidisciplinary pain management service with expertise in pelvic painA healthcare professional with specialist expertise in gynaecological imaging of endometriosisAdvanced diagnostic facilities (for example, radiology and histopathology)Fertility services.Generally, endometriosis services across Northern Ireland are provided on an ad hoc basis as a sub–specialty within gynaecology services.  There are no specific endometriosis services within the Northern and Southern areas and there is a limited service within the South Eastern Trust.  Patients in these areas are usually referred to services within the Belfast and Western Trust.  Due to the changes over the past 6-7 years with regards to the treatment of endometriosis the Belfast Trust has indicated to the Strategic Policy Planning Group that it would like to submit a business case to develop and establish a Regional Endometriosis service in Northern Ireland, which is likely to request at least 1 whole time equivalent Consultant Gynaecologist with specialist interest/sub-spec training in Endometriosis plus a full multi-disciplinary support team.There are currently two Consultants employed in the Western Trust with the skill set to carry out Endometriosis surgery at Altnagelvin.  It has been confirmed that South West Acute Hospital is not a suitable site to carry out this surgery as it is essential to have a Urologist and Colorectal surgeon available.The Western Trust has applied for preliminary revalidation as an Endometriosis Centre, but in order to receive full accreditation the Trust must be able to demonstrate that they are undertaking this surgery on a regular basis, and this will require additional funding. Whilst the direction of travel is to develop specialist endometriosis care so as to comply with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence Guidance this cannot be provided in each Trust area and therefore there are currently no discussions open with the Northern Trust as the current approach is to develop specialist centres in both the Belfast and Western Trust.  This two centre approach will require new investment.  However, there is no indication at this stage that there will be any monies available in 2022/23 to allow for investment and/or service development and therefore I am unable to give a confirmation of details regarding timescales, additional funding and recruitment to enhance the local endometriosis service. 	As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available.Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action his Department is taking to introduce new flexibility measures, such as energy storage, to help prevent future energy price spikes.
answer_text: The Executive’s Energy Strategy Action Plan includes an action (Action 19) to prepare a Northern Ireland Smart Systems and Flexibility Plan which will consider key issues such as flexibility opportunities and barriers for consumers and networks as well as digitalisation needs and future markets for flexibility.Officials from my Department are currently working with the Utility Regulator (UR) to progress this action.  I have committed to report on progress across all the actions by March 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action his Department is taking to increase the connection of renewable energy sources to the transmission grid.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act 2022 set a target of 80% of electricity to be produced from renewable sources by 2030.  Planning of the network and connections to the transmission system are matters for the System Operator for Northern Ireland (SONI) plus the Utility Regulator (UR) which regulates SONI via its licence.  My officials continue to liaise with those bodies to ensure that the legislative target is met.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail plans for the implementation of Clause 22 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: As outlined in my previous answer to you on this topic, the introduction of the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 and the requirement for all Departments to set out policies and proposals within climate action plans to meet the 2050 net zero targets presents us with a real opportunity to address the climate emergency.My Department is working collaboratively with DAERA in the development of the sectoral plans required by the Act including determining how the specific measures contained in Clause 22 can be met.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the development of a cycle lane on Scullions Road, Mallusk.
answer_text: I am pleased to confirm that design work to provide cycleway/footway on Scullions Road at Mallusk is at an advanced stage and it is anticipated the first phase of the scheme will be completed later this financial year, consisting of 330m of footway/cycleway from the junction of the Mallusk Road to just beyond the entrance to Cowan Bros.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 277/22-27 and in the absence of a functioning Assembly, whether his Department will be able to implement the Ammonia Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has developed a comprehensive draft ammonia strategy which I am currently finalising for publication. The publication of this strategy for consultation is within my existing ministerial authority.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps he is taking to ensure that Northern Ireland achieves the targets of our Nationally Determined Contribution of the Paris Agreement.
answer_text: Whilst the Nationally Determined Contribution is a UK wide target set under the Paris Agreement, there is a need for all regions to play their part.  My Department is playing a key role on behalf of the Executive through progressing both the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 and the draft Green Growth Strategy which will act as key drivers to reduce emissions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will implement an immediate ban on burning on peatland habitats to prevent further damage to these carbon stores and enhance efforts to restore peatlands to full health.
answer_text: My Department is developing a Peatland Strategy for Northern Ireland, reflecting the commitments in the UK Peatland Strategy and New Decade, New Approach document and will provide a framework for conserving our intact peatlands and restoring degraded peatlands.A key action within the draft Strategy is to “Publish a Position Statement on Upland Management, with accompanying new legislation if required”.  This Position Statement will clarify my Department’s advice with regard to the use of prescribed burning as a management tool for peatlands in Northern Ireland. Prescribed burning is a management tool (in addition to grazing, re-wetting and flailing) which can be used to manage vegetation on peatlands. Prescribed burning can damage habitats particularly if it gets out of control or is not implemented correctly. The use of prescribed burning in managing vegetation in peatland habitats remains at the bottom of a hierarchy of upland management options, and is particularly unsuitable on blanket bog.  It can, however, have a role as part of a targeted, strategic wildfire risk reduction approach.  My Department is developing an agreed and co-ordinated approach among relevant stakeholders to the issue of wildfires in the uplands.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the reasoning behind his Department’s decision to permit Kilroot power station to exceed its emissions limits.
answer_text: The Industrial Emissions Directive (IED) 2010 is implemented through the Pollution Prevention Control (Industrial Emissions) (PPC(IE)) Regulations NI 2013 via a permit issued by the Chief Inspector which sets out specific Emission Limit Values for Large Combustion Plants (LCP) such as EP Kilroot (EPK).Of the LCP’s in Northern Ireland, three are powers stations and only one, EPK is coal fired. In June 2020 due to the unprecedented impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on regulated sectors such as LCP’s the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) issued specific COVID-19 Regulatory Position Statements (RPS). Where the conditions set out in the RPS were adhered to the NIEA would not take regulatory action.As a result of concerns raised by EPK due to the ending of the NIEA COVID-19 Regulatory Position Statements (RPS) for LCP’s on 31 March 2022 and potential interruption of coal supplies from Russia due to the war in Ukraine; the Department issued a Direction to the NIEA IPRI Chief Inspector on 29 March in order to ensure the security of Northern Ireland’s electricity supply as determined by Department for Economy. The Chief Inspector has written to EPK to confirm a direction to waive enforcement action for breach of Oxides of Nitrogen or Sulphur Dioxide emission limits under certain circumstances for up to one year (to end 31 March 2023), with built in three month review points.Since the RPS issued during the COVID-19 pandemic expired on 31 March 2022, EPK have chosen to curtail the output of their coal units to approximately 50% of maximum output in order to comply with the Nitrous Oxides Emission Limit Values in their permit. As a result, to date the direction has not been used. It is anticipated that during the winter period, SONI (the Transmission System Operator for Northern Ireland) will require the EPK units to run at higher output levels in order to maintain security of supply. The DAERA direction permits the Chief Inspector to waiver any enforcement action on EPK providing them with an option to generate sufficient electricity to meet this demand. Assembly members should be assured that my Department remains fully committed to limiting Northern Ireland’s greenhouse gas emissions, working within its remit and with other departments to deliver on the emissions reduction requirements of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what mitigating actions his Department is taking to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, following the decision to permit Kilroot power station to exceed its emissions limits.
answer_text: The Department remains fully committed to limiting Northern Ireland’s greenhouse gas emissions. Several examples of how the Department is working towards this goal are:Working within its remit and with other departments to deliver on the emissions reduction requirements of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 including developing Northern Ireland’s first climate action plan and a range of sectoral plans.Leading on development of the Executive’s draft Green Growth Strategy which aims to ensure that future Government policy making has climate action at its core.Bringing into operation in NI all requirements of EU legislation to limit emissions of fluorinated greenhouse gases (“F-gases”)Delivering air quality improvements, in relation to energy and domestic heating, transport emissions and active travel, andFurther work developing the UK-wide emissions trading scheme.The Direction issued by DAERA to the Chief Inspector within the provisions of the Pollution Prevention Control (Industrial Emissions) (PPC(IE)) Regulations NI 2013, was required in order to ensure the security of Northern Ireland’s electricity supply as determined by the Department for Economy. This direction can permit excess emissions of Oxides of Nitrogen and Sulphur Dioxide only for up to one year, with built in three month review points, and to date the direction has not been used.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 25269/17-22, for an update on the Department’s review of petroleum licensing.
answer_text: The review of petroleum licensing looked at a wide range of evidence including Northern Ireland specific research, existing policies and commitments as well as international studies and the views of stakeholders. Following this, in January 2022, I circulated a draft Executive paper to my Ministerial colleagues about future onshore petroleum licensing policy.  My paper recommended that the Executive agree a preferred policy option of a moratorium on all forms of exploration and extraction of oil and gas, to be followed by the introduction of a legislative ban.As this is considered to be a cross cutting and controversial policy area it will require the agreement of an Executive, following which, the preferred option will be subject to public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will appoint organic advisors in his Department to advise farmers on transitioning to organic farming.
answer_text: Currently organic farmers across Northern Ireland receive support through existing Knowledge Transfer and Innovation programmes delivered by my Department. My officials will continue to monitor the uptake and adoption of organic farming and respond accordingly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of funding for rural projects provided by his Department in South Antrim, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Funding support for rural projects provided by my Department in South Antrim, in each of the last five years is as follows:201720182019202020212022Totals£520,363£934,941£1,215,286£564,438£823,681£470,636£4,529,344The cumulative total breakdown for each Scheme for the last five years is as follows:SchemeTotalsALL-IRELAND CO-OPERATION SCHEME£268,498BUSINESS INVESTMENT SCHEME£960,975EUROPEAN INNOVATION PARTNERSHIP SCHEME£1,238M1.1 BDG PARTICIPANTS PAYMENTS£180,816M16 AGRI-FOOD CO-OPERATION SCHEME£21,394M19.2 RURAL BASIC SERVICES SCHEME£260,988M19.2 VILLAGE RENEWAL SCHEME£312,833M4.1 FARM BUSINESS IMPROVEMENT SCHEME CAP TIER 2£225,580M7.5 RURAL TOURISM PAYMENTS£500,000RURAL BUSINESS INVESTMENT SCHEME£1,324,541RURAL COMMUNITY POLLINATOR SCHEME£89,494FOREST SERVICE TRAILS (CRUMLIM GLEN) £17,000SOCIAL FARMING CAPITAL GRANT SCHEME£21,252RURAL MICRO CAPITAL GRANT SCHEME£48,453COVID 19 RECOVERY REVITALISATION SCHEME£102,000RURAL BUSINESS DEVELOPMENT GRANT SCHEME£194,284In Addition to the above funding COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT in the area were given funding of £634,799.07.  The funding is based on council areas not constituencies and relates to 4 council areas - Antrim & Newtownabbey; Mid & East Antrim; Lisburn & Castlereagh; and Ards & North Down.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration his Department has given to the development of sectoral plans for the transport sector, as required in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The introduction of the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 and the requirement for all Departments to set out policies and proposals within climate action plans to meet the 2050 net zero targets presents us with a real opportunity to address the climate emergency.My Department is working collaboratively with DAERA in the development of the plans required by the Act. The development of sectoral plans for the transport sector is being considered as a part of that engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the schools in the South Antrim constituency that have benefited from the School Pollinator Garden Scheme.
answer_text: In the South Antrim constituency, 13 groups were granted awards under the Schools Pollinator Garden Scheme.  The schools included:	Ballyclare Secondary School;	Ballymacward Primary School;	Creavery PS; 	Duneane Primary School; 	Fairview Primary School; 	Greystone Primary School; 	Mallusk Integrated Primary School;	Mount St Michael’s PS; 	Randalstown Central Primary School; 	Six Mile Integrated Primary; 	St. MacNissi’s Primary School; 	St Oliver Plunkett’s PS; 	Templepatrick Primary School.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what consideration his Department has given to the development of sectoral plans for the energy sector, as required in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: My Department is engaging with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs along with other Departments to collaboratively agree the methodology and design of Northern Ireland’s first Climate Action Plan. In May, as members of the Inter-Departmental Strategic Oversight Group for Green Growth and Climate Change, my officials attended a workshop which considered the approach to all sectoral plans required in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what consultation his Department has conducted with stakeholders ahead of developing Sectoral Plans, as required in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: My officials are already leading engagement with representatives from all Departments to scope and agree the approach to developing the sectoral plans. When developing their policies and proposals for inclusion in the sectoral plans, Departments will consult with relevant sector-specific advisory groups. The intention is that sectoral plans will form part of the overall Climate Action Plan for Northern Ireland. A wide range of stakeholder engagement activities shall be undertaken at the appropriate times during development of the Plan. In line with the requirements of the Act, my Department will lay the first Climate Action Plan before the Assembly by the end of 2023 and prior to this a public consultation of not less than 16 weeks will be undertaken.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what consideration his Department has given to (i) the development of sectoral plans for the agriculture sector; and (ii) the rollout of carbon audits of farms to assess where performance improvements and savings can be made, as required in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: Sectoral plans will be included within Northern Ireland’s first Climate Action Plan. The Climate Action Plan is required to be laid before the Assembly before the end of December 2023 and the sectoral plan for agriculture will be taken forward in line with the overall approach to and timeline for producing the Climate Action Plan. 	My Officials are currently developing a Farm Carbon Policy for Northern Ireland and it is expected that farm carbon auditing will form part of this policy and could gather data to assess where performance improvements and savings can be made.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what consideration he has given to the disparity of charges, between local councils, for complaints in relation to the High Hedges Act (Northern Ireland) 2011; and (ii) whether there is a planned review, with a view to standardising this charge.
answer_text: The High Hedges (Fee) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2012 made provision for councils to charge a fee for investigating a complaint under the High Hedges Act (Northern Ireland) 2011. The level of fee is determined by individual councils, subject to a maximum of £360.		Correspondence between my officials and local councils indicates that the majority of councils charge the maximum fee and there are no plans to review the charges.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the Park Road, Mallusk, to detail (i) when the last road safety assessment was conducted, given the high numbers of heavy goods vehicles using this rural road; and (ii) the outcome of any such road safety assessment.
answer_text: My Department is currently in the process of reviewing the existing speed limit on Park Road, Mallusk in conjunction with the PSNI Roads Policing Unit.  The review will involve analysis of recent Road Traffic Collision data, average speeds and other relevant environmental factors.  My officials will advise you directly of the outcome of the review in due course.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what consideration he has given to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change’s Special Report on Climate Change and Land which concludes that the expansion of areas agriculture have contributed to increasing net greenhouse gas emissions.
answer_text: This report is at a global level. The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 and the draft Green Growth Strategy, which my Department has progressed on behalf of the Executive, will act as key drivers to ensure that Northern Ireland accelerates its action to play its part in tackling the global climate challenge. In contrast to the global situation, the Statistical Review of Northern Ireland Agriculture indicates that the total area of farms has decreased from 1,067,200 hectares in 2001 to 1,029,500 hectares in 2020. The total area of agricultural crops has decreased from 49,500 hectares to 42,900 hectares, while the total area of grass has increased from 843,700 hectares to 861,800 hectares over the same period.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what consideration he has given to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change’s report, Climate Change 2022: Mitigation of Climate Change.
answer_text: The Path to Net Zero Energy Strategy, launched in December 2021 set out the direction of travel for our transition to net zero carbon energy.  The focus for the first year of the delivery of the strategy is captured in the 22 actions published in the Path to Net Zero Action Plan.  The IPCC report, Climate Change 2022 Mitigation of Climate Change assesses literature on the scientific, technological, environmental, economic and social aspects of mitigation of climate change. This report forms part of the evidence base in the ongoing development of the Energy Strategy (leading up to the first formal review in 2025), research which sits alongside the delivery of the energy strategy outlined above.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what consideration he has given to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change’s report, Climate Change 2022: Mitigation of Climate Change.
answer_text: Following the publication of the IPCCs Working Group III report ‘Climate Change 2022: Mitigation of Climate Change’ on 4 April 2022 I considered the content of the report along with other recently published reports from the IPCC. Whilst the reports are at a global level the need for all Countries to play our part to accelerate action to tackle emissions to limit global warming is a key take away. The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 and the draft Green Growth Strategy which my Department has progressed on behalf of the Executive, will act as key drivers to ensure that Northern Ireland accelerates its action to play its part in tackling the global climate challenge.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to halt land degradation in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I recognise the environmental challenges that need to be tackled in Northern Ireland range from deteriorating water and air quality, habitat and biodiversity loss and fragmentation, to the wide-ranging impacts of climate change.  Many of our peatlands have been damaged to some degree, are in poor condition, and require more sustainable management and restoration. Climate change is a significant, but as yet unquantified, threat.  The great majority of natural habitats in Northern Ireland remain in unfavourable condition, although underlying trends are more positive as a result of targeted conservation actions. My Department currently operates controls to discourage and prevent land degradation via Statutory Management Requirement 3 of the Northern Ireland Cross-Compliance Verifiable Standards and the Good Agricultural and Environmental Condition Standards including the Environmental Impact Assessment (Agriculture) Regulations 2007 (NI).  Furthermore, the voluntary Environmental Farming Scheme offers farmers annual payments to manage our most important habitats, including peatlands, in a favourable way with over 60,000 ha currently under agreement with 1280 farm business. My Department opened the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme (SNHS) to farmers in the first (Zone 1) of four zones on 27 May 2022. This innovative and forward looking scheme represents an investment of up to £45m and is being rolled out in stages to farmers across Northern Ireland over the next four years. The SNHS will provide farmers with individualised baseline data of nutrient levels at field scale, as well as baseline estimates of carbon stocks in their farmed soils and above ground biomass. This information will help farmers manage carbon, nutrients applications, reduce excess run-off to watercourses and improve soil health.In March, I announced 54 decisions that define future agricultural policy.  These policy decisions will be followed, as soon as possible, with new policies and systems to ensure that we will have a future sustainable agricultural industry.  There is significant potential for farmers and land managers to make vital positive contributions to tackling environmental impacts across the board.Within my policy decisions I committed that my Department will also undertake the development of a future land use policy during the next mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what consideration he has given to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change’s Special Report on Climate Change and Land which concludes that unsustainable land management is a direct cause of land degradation, with agriculture being a dominant sector driving degradation.
answer_text: The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change’s Special Report on Climate Change and Land is at a global level, though the need for all Countries to play their part is vital.In March, I announced 54 decisions which define future agricultural policy.  These policy decisions will be followed as soon as possible with new policies and systems to ensure that we will have a future sustainable agricultural industry.Within my policy decisions I committed that my Department will also undertake the development of a future land use policy during the next mandate. In 2020, I launched the Forests For Our Future programme, with the ambition to plant 18 million trees and create 9,000 hectares of new woodland to help our environment and economy.I also launched the four year £45m Soil Nutrient Health Scheme (SNHS) in April this year with the aim of improving soil health and baselining soil carbon stocks across Northern Ireland. Earlier this year, I was pleased to host the UK Agriculture Partnership (UKAP) in Northern Ireland, which brought together leading experts, including researchers, government officials, farming organisations and NGOs from across the UK to discuss the challenges and issues faced with regards to soil health in agriculture.	My Officials have also commissioned a number of research studies to determine how we can continue to improve soil health and sequester additional carbon in our soils in future.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 796/22-27, to provide a timeframe in which he intends to launch the public consultation on the first Climate Action Plan.
answer_text: Following initial scoping work that has been undertaken within DAERA and with other NICS Departments, it is currently anticipated that the public consultation on a draft Climate Action Plan could be launched in spring 2023.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration she has given to the implementation of a scheme to improve the energy performance of social rented homes, such as the Social Housing Decarbonisation Fund, as proposed by the UK Government.
answer_text: My Department has developed a Housing Supply Strategy which acknowledges the importance of ensuring we have good quality homes. I am currently considering options for the Strategy in the absence of a functioning Executive and agreed budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his plans to bring forward legislative proposals to require new build homes and buildings to have electric car charging points.
answer_text: The minimum requirements for electric vehicle charging infrastructure were originally set out in Article 8 of the European Union’s Energy Performance of Buildings Directive (EPBD) for new residential and new and existing non-residential buildings. Following the British Government’s indication of its intention to comply with the Directive, transposition of the EPBD into legislation, is a devolved responsibility.My Department is currently engaging with officials from other departments here and across these islands to examine the most effective way to bring forward the provision of EV Charging Infrastructure in dwellings into legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, following the publication of the Doubling Back and Doubling Down: G20 scorecard on fossil fuel funding report, what steps he has taken to improve Northern Ireland’s contribution to the UK’s (i) rating for progress in ending support for fossil fuels; ii) rating for transparency; and (iii) overall ranking and score of Members of the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development.
answer_text: My Department has recently published the ‘Energy in Northern Ireland 2022’ Official Statistics publication which shows that the annual proportion of electricity consumption from renewable sources in Northern Ireland has risen considerably in recent years, from 9.7% in 2009 to 41.3% in 2021.		The report also finds that in terms of the percentage of total indigenous electricity generation accounted for by indigenous renewable generation, Northern Ireland had the second highest proportion (45.6%) of all four UK regions in 2020 using this measure – higher than England (39.5%) and Wales (36.1%).		In January 2022 the Action Plan for the new Energy Strategy – ‘The Path to Net Zero Energy’ was published.  This Action Plan is an integral part of delivering the overall energy strategy.		Within this Action Plan there are a total of 22 actions including 7 actions relating specifically to replacing fossil fuels with renewable energy.  Delivery of these actions is a priority for the Executive and will make a significant contribution to reducing both Northern Ireland’s and the UK’s reliance on fossil fuels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what consideration he has given to the recommendations in the Doubling Back and Doubling Down: G20 scorecard on fossil fuel funding report.
answer_text: My Department does not provide any direct support to fossil fuels and therefore I have given no consideration to the Doubling Back and Doubling Down: G20 scorecard on fossil fuel funding report. In January 2022, my Department published the Action Plan for the new Energy Strategy – ‘The Path to Net Zero Energy’. This Action Plan is an integral part of delivering the overall energy strategy.Within this Action Plan there are a total of 22 actions including 7 actions relating specifically to replacing fossil fuels with renewable energy. Delivery of these actions is a priority for the Executive and will make a significant contribution to reducing both Northern Ireland’s and the UK’s reliance on fossil fuels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether he is aware of sectarian graffiti at the fishery at Stoneyford Reservoir; and (ii) whether his Department intend to remove this graffiti.
answer_text: NI Water has made me aware of the sectarian graffiti at Stoneyford Reservoir and of the efforts being made to have the graffiti removed.  As this is an operational matter for NI Water, you should direct any further questions regarding the removal of the graffiti to NI Water at waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether he is aware of sectarian graffiti at the fishery at Stoneyford Reservoir; and (ii) whether his Department intend to remove this graffiti.
answer_text: I was not aware of sectarian graffiti at Stoneyford Reservoir. While Stoneyford Reservoir is part of My Department’s Public Angling Estate, the site is owned by Northern Ireland Water. DAERA Inland Fisheries leases the fishing rights from the owner of the site, NI Water. Any reports of graffiti or requests to remove it should be directed to NI Water. My officials will pass on the report of graffiti to NI Water.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans he has to bring forward legislative proposals to ban the use of snares.
answer_text: I have no immediate plans to reform the legislation on the use of snares.  I understand that, although not widespread, there is still a need to retain snares to control pest species in the countryside.  For example, some landowners use them to reduce the impact of foxes on new born lambs during the lambing season. Gamekeepers also use them periodically to reduce the destruction of game birds by foxes at certain times of the year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to address rural crime such as wildlife crime and livestock theft.
answer_text: The lead enforcement agency for addressing rural crime such as wildlife crime and livestock theft is the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI).My Department’s Northern Ireland Environment Agency provides support and technical advice to the PSNI wildlife unit, when required, for cases of wildlife crime. My Department’s Welfare and Enforcement Branch work closely with the PSNI in cases of livestock theft providing Department information and records concerning animal identification and movements.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to increase the take up of HIV testing.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) holds a number of sexual health contracts across the five Health & Social Care Trusts and Community/Voluntary Sector organisations which promote, encourage and provide HIV testing. Through contracts with the Trusts, the PHA continues to promote and resource Outreach Nurse-led clinics and Sexual Health/Genitourinary Medicine clinics via posters, leaflets and wallet cards. Promotional videos have also been created to advertise the health clinics to young people aged 16-25 years, working in partnership with Regional Further Educational Colleges. The Public Health Agency has contracts for the provision of telephone enquiry services and information helplines for the general public and professionals on a range of sexual health issues, including STIs and information on the availability and importance of HIV testing. Contracts with community and voluntary organisations ensure promotion of good sexual health and relationships is an integral part of their service.  This includes various helplines, web pages, leaflets and drop in centres.  A few of our providers also support the emotional health and wellbeing of their clients, partners and families.The PHA currently has a regional tender for relationship and sexuality education for young people in community settings, which promotes the theme with youth organisations, parents and young people.  HIV and sexually transmitted infections is an important theme within this education programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what recent assessment his Department has made of the levels of pyridine in Northern Ireland waters.
answer_text: Currently there is no legislative driver either within the retained EU, UK or local legislation that requires the monitoring of pyridine in the aquatic environment in Northern Ireland.  Therefore no formal monitoring programmes are in place.During the development of a non-targeted semi-quantitative screening method by the DAERA Water Chemistry Group, twenty freshwater sites were selected across Northern Ireland and a baseline screen carried out. Derivatives of pyridine were detected at some sites but were of low significance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to promote inland fishing opportunities as part of Northern Ireland’s tourist product.
answer_text: The COVID-19 pandemic and associated restrictions have had a significant negative impact on my Department’s ability to promote angling as part of Northern Ireland’s tourist product. Prior to the pandemic, my Department worked closely with the Loughs Agency and Inland Fisheries Ireland to promote angling across the island of Ireland by attending game fairs and angling trade shows across the British Isles, Europe and North America. COVID-19 restrictions resulted in such events being cancelled or postponed over the last two years and these are only now beginning to return to normal all be it on a more limited basis. It will also take time for my Department to ramp up promotional activities and my officials will work with partner organisations to identify the most beneficial events to attend. DAERA Inland Fisheries staff attended the North West Angling Show earlier this year and are attending the Shanes Castle Game Fair later this month.My Department has recently commissioned a number of professionally produced angling videos and these have been made available on relevant websites and social media platforms to promote angling across Northern Ireland. The videos have received significant numbers of views and are an alternative method of promoting the sport here amongst previously less accessible demographics.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans he has to bring forward legislative proposals to prevent companies from fire and rehire practices.
answer_text: The Member will recall that in my statement to the Assembly on 21st March, I expressed my deep anger and concern at the recent redundancies made by P&O Ferries.To ensure there is not a systemic issue, I will be looking closely at fire-and-rehire practices; however, there is no immediate suggestion that this practice is regularly happening in Northern Ireland.My officials will also liaise with counterpart in BEIS regarding proposals in GB for a statutory Code of Conduct on Fire and Rehire.Nonetheless, I would caution any employer considering giving notice and offering a new contract with revised terms - effectively ‘firing and rehiring’ an employee.  There are statutory processes which must be followed, including statutory minimum dismissal procedures and the need for an employer to follow a collective redundancy consultation process if a group of employees is involved.I would encourage all employers considering changing terms and conditions of employment for staff to consult their staff or their representatives to explain the reasons for their plan and to listen to alternative ideas.  Any employees who believe they have been unfairly dismissed from their original contract may be able to complain to an Industrial Tribunal providing they have at least one year's continuous service with their employer. Employees may also be able to claim redundancy if they have at least two years’ service.Anyone who finds themselves in this position may wish to consider contacting the Labour Relations Agency - Workplace Information Service on 03300 555 300 for confidential and impartial information.In addition, the Law Centre NI provides free, independent, specialist legal advice (subject to capacity) on employment rights issues. The Law Centre can be contacted on their advice line on 028 9024 4401 or by email: employmentadvice@lawcentreni.org .Further information may be found at  https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/changes-employment-conditions

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans he has to bring forward legislative proposals to ban single-use plastic carrier bags.
answer_text: My Department continues to tackle plastic waste with one of our major successes being the introduction of the Carrier Bag Levy in 2013 which has already removed over 1.7 billion bags from circulation across Northern Ireland. Our Carrier Bag Levy is the most extensive of its kind across the UK. The most recent published annual validated statistics for 2020-2021 inform that bag usage continues to fall in our jurisdiction with 56.2 million carrier bags dispensed which represents a 24.3 million reduction (30.2%) from the 80.5 million dispensed the previous year. While the downward trend in bag usage has been encouraging so as to further address the unnecessary purchase of bags and to encourage reuse, on 01 April 2022 I introduced an increase in the levy from 5 pence to 25 pence on ALL carrier bags priced at £5 or less.As part of the commitment laid out by the Executive in the New Decade, New Approach document, my Department is leading on work to bring forward a plan to tackle and eliminate plastic pollution in Northern Ireland.  My Department has recently removed all unnecessary single-use plastic from the Government estate within the target date of October 2021. The Plastic Reduction Action Plan Annual Report was published on the DAERA website on 24 March 2022.  DAERA is also a signatory to the UK Plastics Pact which enables us to work closely with industry partners to focus on issues around eliminating problematic and unnecessary plastic packaging.  In addition to this, upcoming legislation aimed at tackling some of our most problematic plastics will provide quantitative data to illustrate reductions in plastic pollution.My Department will continue to monitor carrier bag usage, but at this time does not have plans to bring forward legislative proposals to ban single-use plastic carrier bags. It is anticipated that this recent increase in the levy will further reduce the overall number of bags being dispensed, particularly single-use plastic bags. It will also reduce the large volumes of heavy duty bags in circulation, many of which were priced at 20 pence or more and were excluded from the levy prior to 01 April 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans he has to bring forward legislative proposals to ban single-use plastic cutlery.
answer_text: You may recall that in November 2020 I announced in the Assembly my intention to ban a range of common single-use plastic items including plastic cutlery. However, shortly after my announcement the UK Government unveiled its withdrawal agreement with the European Union that included the requirement to transpose elements of EU Directive 2019/904 on the reduction of the impact of certain plastic products on the environment (the Single-use Plastic Directive). Therefore until ongoing discussions between the UK and the EU are resolved I am unable to make any plans regarding legislation to ban single-use plastic cutlery.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to increase the diversion of food waste from the residual waste stream.
answer_text: As part of the current policy development on the future of recycling, my Department is seeking ways to increase the diversion of food waste from the residual stream. This includes options for extending the food regulations to cover food waste collections from non-food businesses and weekly separate collection of food waste from households where this is not already offered.My Department also runs regular awareness raising and behaviour change campaigns focused on both the prevention of food waste and ensuring food that is wasted is put into the correct receptacle for recycling. Some of the many examples are that funding has been provided to the Waste and Resources Action Programme (WRAP) for Food Waste Action Week in March 2022 and to Councils to roll out bespoke NI advertising on food waste as part of the ‘Better Recycling’ campaign in late 2021.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the recent rising input costs and price volatility around certain agricultural commodities, what assessment he has made of the merits of increasing support payments and grants to farmers.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my answer provided to AQW 731/22-27. With end markets for agricultural produce not yet responding sufficiently to offset rising costs, farmers are experiencing significant adverse effects on their cash flows and profitability.I met the Rt Hon George Eustice MP, Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, on 8 June to reiterate once again my call for a funding package for the agricultural and fisheries sectors as quickly as possible. These pressures on the industry are not temporary and we do not want to jeopardise our domestic food supply.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department plans to take to increase the glass recycling rate.
answer_text: As announced in March, I have agreed that new targets for glass recycling will be placed on producers as part of the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) for packaging reforms due to be implemented from 2024. A glass recycling target of 75% in 2024 rising to 83% in 2030 is proposed. The target exceeds the EU glass recycling targets of 70% in 2025 and 75% in 2030.The targets take into account the decision to exclude glass drink containers in Northern Ireland and England in response to the 2021 Deposit Return Scheme (DRS) consultation. However, Northern Ireland will keep under review the inclusion of glass once the DRS is fully operational to ensure glass drinks containers are meeting the required recycling targets.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans she has to bring forward legislative proposals on Renters Reform to improve standards in the private and socially rented sectors.
answer_text: In the continued absence of a functioning Executive and no agreed Executive budget, all options are being considered by the Department

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what discussions his Department has had with (i) airlines; and (ii) airports, in relation to supporting customers who have experienced flight cancellations.
answer_text: As Minister for the Economy my responsibility is with maintaining and enhancing Northern Ireland’s air connectivity, both domestically and internationally. In doing so, I fully recognise that civil aviation is a reserved matter for the UK Department for Transport (DfT). The Secretary of State for Transport has pointed out that this is a private sector industry and responsibility for cancelled flights and lengthy queues lies with the airlines and airports.  They are commercially responsible for manging their businesses and providing their customers with an appropriate and reasonable service. I welcome the fact that the UK Government is working closely with the sector to ensure that there will be no repeat of those scenes this summer.The scenes we have witnessed at airports are distressing, with holidaymakers missing out on their first trips abroad after the pandemic. Passengers who have been affected by cancelled flights should be offered a full refund within seven days, or re-routing via alternative transportation at the earliest opportunity by the airline.  If the flight is delayed or cancelled the consumer may also be entitled to compensation.  To claim compensation, passengers can complete an online form on the relevant airline’s website. Should passengers remain dissatisfied with the airline’s response, they can get in touch with the Consumer Council, which is the designated complaints handling body for flights in and out of Northern Ireland, and it will take up the complaint on their behalf.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the recent outbreak of foot and mouth disease in Southeast Asia, what measures are in place (i) to reduce the risk of the virus entering Northern Ireland; and (ii) to prevent its introduction to livestock.
answer_text: My officials actively monitor animal disease patterns and incidents reported across the world and is aware of the recent outbreak of Foot and Mouth Disease (FMD) in South-East Asia, with Indonesia reporting its first outbreak since 1983. The World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH) has also confirmed outbreaks in Java and Sumatra and there are media reports of outbreaks in Borneo. However, the risk of incursion into the UK is still assessed as Low.   	FMD has the potential to cause serious animal health and economic consequences. Maintaining freedom from the disease is of paramount importance in protecting animal health and welfare, supporting the economy and underpinning international trade of live animals and products of animal origin. Therefore, my Department has robust disease surveillance programmes in place to help prevent incursion of the major exotic diseases such as FMD into Northern Ireland (NI) which includes targeted/passive surveillance and portal checks on animals arriving into NI. These checks have helped detect diseased animals on arrival enabling prompt action to prevent spread into NI. Risk based controls, including the use of sniffer dogs, are also carried out at NI ports and airports on personal imports of food being brought in from countries or regions where epizootic disease is confirmed or there is a heightened risk.Detailed contingency plans for handling outbreaks of epizootic disease are published on the DAERA website. The aim is to prepare a comprehensive response encompassing all aspects of resource and planning necessary for the prevention, detection, control, eradication and recovery from an epizootic disease incursion.  The contingency plans are exercised regularly and staff are trained for their specific emergency roles.My Department also has legislative powers to take action to help prevent the incursion of epizootic diseases such as FMD.  This includes secondary legislation, The Foot and Mouth Disease Regulations (NI) 2006, which provides further powers in respect of this specific disease. This legislation also allows for swift action to protect disease free status and prevent incursion. It requires suspect cases to be reported to my Department and allows for movement and trade controls to be applied where disease is suspected or confirmed. In most cases, the legislation also provides for the culling of animals for disease control purposes. In parallel to statutory controls, my officials provide biosecurity advice and guidance to farmers through a range of communication channels to help prevent incursion into livestock and raise awareness of the risks. Furthermore, the risk of incursion to NI is regularly assessed through veterinary risk assessments.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail on any additional legislation required to increase the maximum fine for littering to £200, as proposed in the draft Environment Strategy.
answer_text: As detailed on page 30 of the draft Environment Strategy, an amendment to the Environmental Offences (Fixed Penalties) (Miscellaneous Provisions) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2012 will be required to increase the maximum fixed penalty available to district councils in respect of litter offences from £80 to £200.The amending Regulations will be subject to the negative resolution procedure in the Assembly. An SL1 will be presented to a newly formed AERA Committee in due course.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail a timeframe in which he expects an action plan for the Peatland Strategy 2021-2040.
answer_text: I anticipate publication of the Peatland Strategy in Autumn 2022.  My Officials have established a Peatland Strategy Steering Group which is currently considering a Governance Structure for implementation of the Peatland Strategy.  Once this is finalised and resources are in place, an Action Plan will be agreed and taken forward.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail a timeframe in which he expects to commence the (i) Climate Action Plan; and (ii) Carbon Budget, as outlined in the Climate Change Bill.
answer_text: In line with the requirements of the Climate Change (Northern Ireland) Act 2022, my Department will lay the first Climate Action Plan before the Assembly by the end of 2023. In advance of this a public consultation of not less than 16 weeks must be undertaken and the results of this must also be laid before the Assembly.In line with Section 58 of the Act, the Climate Change Committee has been asked to provide advice in relation to setting the level of the first three Carbon Budget periods, which are required to be set at a level that is consistent with the 2030, 2040 and 2050 emissions targets.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what consultation his Department has conducted with stakeholders ahead of developing a Climate Action Plan, as required in the Climate Change Bill.
answer_text: In line with the requirements of the Climate Change (Northern Ireland) Act 2022, my Department will lay the first Climate Action Plan before the Assembly by the end of 2023. As part of the process a public consultation of not less than 16 weeks must be undertaken and the results of this must also be laid before the Assembly.My officials are already leading engagement with representatives from all other Departments to scope and set the strategic direction for the Plan.When developing their policies and proposals for inclusion in the Plan, Departments will consult with relevant sector-specific advisory groups. There will be engagement with all of the key sectors as part of the process of developing and agreeing the sectoral plans for inclusion in the overall Climate Action Plan. A wide range of stakeholder engagement activities shall be undertaken at the appropriate times during development of the Plan.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to review enforcement powers under the Wildlife and Natural Environment Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: The Wildlife and Natural Environment Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 amended the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985 and extended the powers of the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) to investigate cases of suspected wildlife crime and also reviewed penalties for individuals found guilty of committing such crimes. Whilst PSNI lead on criminal proceeding, they may seek specialist advice from governmental or non-governmental experts to assist the investigation.   I have no plans to review these enforcement powers at this time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what engagement his Department has had with Translink in regard to assisting blind and partially sighted passengers at rail stations and platforms.
answer_text: All main Translink bus and rail stations fully comply with the Disability Discrimination legislation. My Officials and Translink continue to engage with the Royal National Institute for the Blind (RNIB) and the Inclusive Mobility and Transport Advisory Committee (IMTAC) on how to improve travel for blind and partially sighted passengers. Tactile paving has been used to provide touch guidance in bus and rail stations. Translink also provides machine readable timetables and it is currently working on providing accessible planning options on the journey planner to assist people with disabilities to plan their journey. In the meantime, the Translink contact centre is available to help with the planning of these journeys. Translink staff receive accessibility training and in manned locations they would be available to provide assistance. My Department and Translink remain committed to supporting people with disabilities and ensuring that our public transport network is inclusive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what engagement his Department has had with Translink in regard to assisting blind and partially sighted passengers in their travel to and from bus stations.
answer_text: All main Translink bus and rail stations fully comply with the Disability Discrimination legislation. My Officials and Translink continue to engage with the Royal National Institute for the Blind (RNIB) and the Inclusive Mobility and Transport Advisory Committee (IMTAC) on how to improve travel for blind and partially sighted passengers. Tactile paving has been used to provide touch guidance in bus stations. In addition, recognising that information on travel arrangements is vitally important to blind and partially sighted passengers, Translink has recently updated its website to Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.0 AA standard which means that the website must be usable and understandable for the majority of people with or without disabilities. Translink also provides machine readable timetables and it is currently working on providing accessible planning options on the journey planner to assist people with disabilities to plan their journey. In the meantime, the Translink contact centre is available to help with the planning of these journeys and Translink staff receive accessibility training and in manned locations would be available to provide assistance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail a timeframe in which he intends to publish the Peatland Strategy.
answer_text: Officials are currently finalising the Peatland Strategy document, seeking internal agreement on the Objectives and Actions and ensuring resources are in place for delivery.  An Equality Impact Assessment has been drafted to accompany the strategy and I intend to carry out a 12 week public consultation on this assessment over the summer. Following my final and Executive approval, I anticipate publication of an agreed Peatland Strategy in Autumn 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many rail stations across Northern Ireland have tactile paving on the platform to assist blind and partially sighted passengers.
answer_text: The design and construction of rail stations, including features to assist blind and partially sighted passengers, is an operational matter for Translink. You should therefore direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what consideration his Department has given to providing financial support for farmers to address the increasing costs of animal feed and fertiliser.
answer_text: I am fully aware of the pressures that Northern Ireland producers are facing in relation to unprecedented rises in input costs of energy, feed and fertiliser which simply cannot be absorbed by farm businesses. It must be balanced quickly by price adjustments throughout the supply chain if the economic viability of primary production and domestic supplies of agricultural goods are to be sustained. Unfortunately, end markets for agricultural produce and food are currently responding too slowly to offset these rising costs, causing a major squeeze on cash flow and loss of profitability. This is undermining confidence throughout the industry and will inevitably result in a contraction of production due to mounting losses if the situation is not corrected very soon. This correction must come in the form of increased farm gate prices for all sectors. I have written to retailers to voice my concern regarding farm gate prices for primary food producers.Since the situation that our agri-food industry is facing is a UK-wide problem I have written to the Secretary of State, George Eustice MP, to urgently consider a range of measures, including financial assistance for UK farmers to support them through the crisis.  I have also written to the Finance Minister, Conor Murphy seeking funding to help address the challenges currently facing the agricultural sector in Northern Ireland. Farmers and growers are experiencing difficulties with cash flows and there is a need to provide the industry with some confidence over the coming months. With this in mind I announced my intention to bring forward full Direct Payments from 16 October 2022 to 01 September 2022, subject to the necessary legislation changes, financial approvals and system upgrades.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to introduce mandatory licensing for mobile animal exhibits.
answer_text: At present, any mobile exhibits that display non-native vertebrae animals must obtain a zoo licence from my Department under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 1972. The animals displayed by such exhibits are also protected by the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 which imposes a duty of care on any person with control of an animal to ensure that its needs are met.  My Department is, nonetheless, committed to reviewing and updating the regulation of animal establishments during this mandate. As part of that review, it intends to give consideration to whether specific licences should be required for mobile animal exhibits.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to introduce mandatory licensing for dog home boarding activities if they are carried out as a commercial business.
answer_text: My Department is committed to reviewing and updating the licensing system for animal boarding establishments, including those that provide boarding from homes, during this mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will publish the outcomes, in full, of the External Independent Review of how his Department dealt with concerns raised in the recent Industrial Tribunal case taken by a former Departmental vet, announced on 27 May 2022.
answer_text: The terms of reference for the external review already commit my Department to publication of the outcomes of the review.  Decisions on publication of the full reports will need to take account of the responsibilities of all departments involved to protect any personal information they may contain and to ensure appropriate confidentiality relating to any relevant HR or other processes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will conduct an assessment of Northern Ireland's food production becoming self-sufficient.
answer_text: Northern Ireland is not self-sufficient in food production and this is not a policy objective. For example, due to climate reasons it is not possible for all vegetables, fruit, cereals and other food products used in Northern Ireland to be produced viably here. Furthermore, it is not possible for Northern Ireland to produce all of its essential fertiliser, machinery and energy requirements.  Within these constraints, my Department will be encouraging sustainable production, seeking to reduce energy dependency through the promotion of renewable sources where possible, rather than pursuing a policy of self-sufficiency. We produce more than we need for a range of meat and dairy products and rely on trade to sell these products and to meet our needs for those food products we do not or cannot produce profitably.  I believe our farmers should continue to concentrate on what they do well and capitalise on the natural advantages that we have.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to introduce mandatory licensing for pet grooming parlours.
answer_text: The pet grooming sector is not licenced in any other part of the United Kingdom (UK) and I am not aware of any evidence to suggest that there is an immediate need to regulate the sector here.  Pet groomers in Northern Ireland, as in the rest of the UK, already have a legal duty to ensure that the needs of animals in their care are met and they can be prosecuted for causing any unnecessary suffering to those animals.That said, my Department intends to explore whether or not there is merit in regulating pet grooming during this mandate as part of its wider review of companion animal establishments.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to bring forward legislative proposals to ban the import of foie gras.
answer_text: While the production of Foie Gras has been illegal in both the UK and the Republic of Ireland for many years it is not currently illegal to import it into the UK. The import of foie Gras cannot be banned in Northern Ireland as under the terms of the EU Withdrawal Agreement and the Northern Ireland Protocol, NI must legally adhere to the Official Controls Regulation 2017/625 (OCR).  Without the EU introducing measures to ban imports of foie gras the product could still enter Northern Ireland legally.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps he has taken to facilitate entry into Northern Ireland for Ukrainian refugees travelling with pets.
answer_text: Ukraine is recognised as an unlisted third country with regards to the EU Pet Travel Regulations 576/2013. As you will be aware, this legislation is still in place in Northern Ireland (NI) as a result of the NI Protocol and Withdrawal Agreement. Retained EU law in Great Britain (GB) following the end of the transition period also means that for the most part, the requirements as laid out in that legislation are also applicable for entry. This means pets moving from Ukraine to the EU, GB or NI require:	a microchip;	a rabies vaccination; 	a successful blood test taken at least 30 days after the rabies vaccination; 	an EU pet passport or a single use EU animal health certificate; 	tapeworm treatment for dogs; and	to wait 3 calendar months from the date the blood sample was taken before travelling.	NI has long held rabies-free status which we value highly and wish to protect.  We also have strong biosecurity measures in place to protect the public and other animals from diseases which can be brought to NI by animals from overseas. 		I do, however, recognise the difficult and distressing situation that refugees from Ukraine currently face and that individuals in these circumstances would not have had time to complete the correct health preparations to travel with their pets.  DAERA has put special arrangements in place to facilitate the non-commercial movement of pets with their owners from Ukraine. Pets will be allowed to travel with their owners even if the pet is non-compliant or has missing documentation.  		People travelling to NI from Ukraine are asked to apply for an emergency import licence, where possible, via email. An emergency import licence is issued to the pet owner and provides instructions for their entry to NI. On arrival, a compliance check will be carried out to determine the status of the animal. Fully compliant animals will be allowed to enter as normal and proceed to their destination. Should an animal be non-compliant, DAERA will make arrangements for quarantine and the necessary health preparations in order to meet compliance with the legislation. DAERA will cover the costs of any necessary health preparations, quarantine stay and approved transportation to quarantine destination for the pets.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department’s investigation into the water quality or pollution incidents at Lough Neagh, following the deaths of two dogs.
answer_text: I was sorry to hear about the recent deaths of two young dogs in Rea’s Wood area of Lough Neagh. Officials in NIEA continue to liaise and work closely with colleagues in Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council (ANBC) to assist them with their investigation. NIEA officials have inspected the area for visual signs of water pollution. No blue green algae were found in water samples examined at the Water Management Unit in Lisburn.  A water sample was taken for a broad sweep of organic substances and nothing of significance was detected. The substances that were identified are commonly found at low levels in rivers and lakes across NI. The results of all our analyses have been shared with ANBC to assist with their investigation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has any plans to introduce mandatory licensing for professional dog walkers.
answer_text: I have no plans to introduce mandatory licensing for dog walking services in Northern Ireland. There are already laws in place which ensure that dogs here are under control when they are walked. Dog walkers also have a legal duty under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 to ensure that the needs of dogs in their care are met.  Licences are not required elsewhere in the United Kingdom to provide this service.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what recent assessment his Department has made of the extent to which good soil health reduces flood risk.
answer_text: My department encourages and supports farmers to adopt good farming practices. With regard to soil health DAERA has supported four pilot soil sampling projects which provided the evidence to support a Northern Ireland wide Soil Nutrient Health Scheme (SNHS). I opened the first Zone of the SNHS to farmers in March 2022 and invited 6,800 farmers to apply to the free to farmer service which will provide a baseline of soil health status on their fields. Soil health status maps will provide valuable information which farmers can use to optimise production by improving soil health and LiDAR scanning will help farmers identify areas prone to overland flow during extreme weather events. The SNHS will also provide information on soil carbon stocks and in above ground biomass.  Through our Evidence & Innovation research programme in partnership with the Agri-Food & Biosciences Institute (AFBI) we have funded soil health projects including identifying which soil characteristics are the best indicators of soil health in grassland and arable soils within NI.  For land where the run-off risk is high during storm events, good soil management can help mitigate the risk of flooding, for example, by ensuring that there is winter crop cover to bind soil and reduce sediment loss. CAFRE also offer guidance to farmers on soil management to avoid flood risk and have published articles such as Improving soil and sward performance which includes advice on managing and introducing drainage systems to the farm.  Flood risk and prevention falls under the Department for Infrastructure. Flood risk management plans highlight the flood hazards and risks in the 20 most significant flood risk areas in Northern Ireland from flooding from rivers, the sea, surface water and reservoirs. The plans identify the measures that will be undertaken over the next 6 years and they set out how the relevant authorities will work together and with communities to reduce the flood risks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what additional legislation needs to be introduced to fully implement the Bovine Tuberculosis Eradication Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The new Bovine Tuberculosis Eradication Strategy for Northern Ireland (“the Strategy”) contains 21 actions across six thematic areas. 		Whilst many of the Strategy’s measures will be implemented administratively, others will require the making of new subordinate legislation under the Diseases of Animals (Northern Ireland) Order 1981 (as amended) (“the 1981 Order”).	In this context, it is envisaged that we will require the following subordinate legislation:As per specified wildlife intervention areas, the making of orders to permit destruction of badgers under the 1981 Order, along with the making of orders to permit ancillary powers to accompany such interventions.The making of a new order (or the possible amendment of the Tuberculosis Control Order (Northern Ireland) 1999 (as amended)) to enable the testing of non-bovines for TB on holdings where no cattle are present.In the longer term, the Department may seek to proceed with the making of new orders to provide for statutory improvement notices and statutory segregation notices for the control of bovine TB.I have postponed the Strategy’s recommendations relating to finance and funding, which will be subject to review in two years’ time, however, should changes be required after that review, additional amendments to the Tuberculosis Control Order (Northern Ireland) 1999 (as amended) will also be required.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what measures are being taken within the Future Agricultural Policy Proposals to support small farmers.
answer_text: I published a Future Agricultural Policy Framework Portfolio in August 2021 that is based on four key outcomes of increased productivity, environmental sustainability, improved resilience and an effective functioning supply chain, alongside measured transition to new schemes.  In March 2022, I announced 54 decisions which define future agricultural policy and will allow schemes to be developed that will provide opportunities for all Northern Ireland’s farmers.  Schemes and support are needed to help farmers develop their businesses, no matter where they farm, to become more productive, profitable and resilient, and to maximise the sustainable returns from the assets at their disposal.During the public consultation on my future agricultural policy proposals, I proposed increasing the minimum claim size to 10 ha for the Farm Sustainability Payment. However, after listening to the concerns raised by stakeholders on the proposals, I made the decision to set that minimum claim size at 5 ha.Small farmers will also have the opportunity to apply to other measures, such as Farming with Nature to ensure that environmental outcomes are delivered at landscape scale.There is still significant work to be done, which my officials will continue to take forward along with continued engagement with stakeholders.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework.
answer_text: My officials carried out an eight week public consultation exercise on proposals for a Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework in autumn 2021. The consultation sought views on the proposed vision, principles and goals of a Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework. Overall, the responses indicated a strong endorsement for the proposed approach.
