The summary of responses has been published on the DAERA website, enabling stakeholders to see the overall views that were expressed in the consultation exercise.As the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework cuts across the responsibilities of a number of NI Departments, it requires Executive approval before publication. In the interim, my officials will continue to work with officials from other Departments through the recently formed Food Programme Board in order to develop detailed proposals for an extensive programme of work.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to introduce further proposals to the existing Bovine Tuberculosis Eradication Strategy for Northern Ireland, such as the testing of non-bovine animals.
answer_text: The new Bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) Eradication Strategy for Northern Ireland contains 21 actions across six thematic areas, including proposed new subordinate legislation to broaden my Department’s powers to test for bTB in non-bovine animals.  Currently, powers to test non-bovine animals are limited to farm holdings where cattle are also present.  The new Strategy details my intention to proceed with legislation which will enable testing, where deemed necessary, to take place on farms where no cattle are present.		With respect to further proposals above what I only recently announced in March 2022, there are currently no plans to add further actions to the new Strategy.   However, my officials, along with the TB Eradication Partnership, will continue to develop policy in our efforts to achieve bTB eradication, work with our stakeholders and monitor developments in neighbouring jurisdictions and, should additional measures be identified that would improve the control and eradication of this costly disease, I will consider updating the Strategy accordingly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on a blue carbon action plan for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As outlined in my Oral Statement to the Assembly on 28 February 2022, my high level vision for the Northern Ireland Blue Carbon Action Plan is to provide protection for our blue carbon habitats and where possible, take steps towards their restoration. 	My officials are currently coordinating the establishment of Climate and Biodiversity focus groups, comprising of representatives from the fishing industry, renewable energy sector and environmental NGOs. The focus groups plan to meet throughout the second half of 2022, with launch of a public consultation on a co-designed draft Blue Carbon Action Plan in early 2023.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Biodiversity Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The new Biodiversity Strategy for Northern Ireland is under development through a process of co-design with stakeholders. During the process the department will engage a wide range of stakeholders including other government departments, councils and other public bodies, a number of bodies representing marine and land users and environmental non-governmental organisations.  It is anticipated that stakeholder workshops will take place over the summer with a view to preparing a document for consultation by the end of the year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Forests for Our Future programme.
answer_text: In my reply on 1 July 2021 to your question on the same subject last year, I stated that almost 900 hectares of new woodland had been created under the Forests for Our Future programme. 		There has been significant interest in both the revised Forest Expansion and the new Small Woodland Grant schemes for the 2021-22 planting season from around 280 landowners. However, I recognise that not all landowners will have taken forward their proposals to create woodland in the 2021-22 planting season for a variety of business reasons. 		Grant aided woodland created in 2021-22 is subject to the 2022 SAF claim period.  The actual area planted by individual landowners will be verified after the claim period closes in June, with payments issued in line with the 2022 SAF process. Forest Service has planted 15 hectares of new woodland during the 2021-22 planting season on its land, which is not subject to SAF processes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Clean Air Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Work is progressing well in the development of Northern Ireland’s first Clean Air Strategy and the Department intends to publish the synopsis of consultation responses to the Clean Air Strategy Discussion Document in the coming weeks.  		I have considered the findings of the consultation, along with potential actions and recommendations, and have instructed officials to begin cross-Departmental discussions, with the key Departments which have the remit to deliver the necessary air quality improvements. These are, the Department for Economy, in relation to energy and domestic heating, and the Department for Infrastructure, in relation to transport emissions and active travel. Other Departments may need to be involved depending on the final scope of the strategy.		Following cross departmental engagement, officials will then draft the Clean Air Strategy for Northern Ireland in preparation for the new Executive. This draft Clean Air Strategy will be subject to an additional public consultation, and due to the cross-cutting nature of the policy, Executive approval will also be sought at that time. It is hoped that this consultation will launch later in 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on progress towards publishing a litter strategy for Northern Ireland by 2024.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department completed the public consultation on the Executive’s draft Environment Strategy on 18 January 2022. This included my proposal to publish Northern Ireland’s first overarching Litter Strategy by 2024, which will consider and propose a range of detailed actions to help tackle the scourge of litter here.Officials have begun progressing this substantive piece of work and I fully expect to have Northern Ireland’s first Litter Strategy published by the end of 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the Ammonia Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has developed a comprehensive ammonia strategy which I am currently finalising for publication. The draft strategy will propose a series of farm measures to reduce ammonia, conservation actions to improve the condition of habitats, and a revised operational protocol for the assessment of air pollution effects. The ammonia reduction element of the strategy will build on the significant support provided for low emissions slurry spreading equipment in Tier 1 of the Farm Business Improvement Scheme (FBIS)-Capital.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what investigation has his Department undertaken regarding animal welfare breaches at the Ballymena Livestock Market.
answer_text: DAERA inspectors undertake a programme of inspections of Livestock Markets in Northern Ireland. These inspections focus on animal welfare, animal identification and biosecurity. The frequency of the inspections are determined by the throughput of the individual markets and the history of compliance at past inspections.Two unannounced inspections carried out by Departmental inspectors at Ballymena Livestock Market in September 2017 uncovered non-compliances with animal welfare legislation and a warning letter was issued. Since then there have been almost 50 further unannounced inspections by Departmental inspectors, with the vast majority compliant. However at an inspection in September 2021 and at a separate inspection in October 2021, inspectors uncovered non-compliances which merited a warning letter on each occasion.  Department inspectors continue to carry out unannounced inspections of Ballymena Livestock Market and the Department will take appropriate enforcement steps if further non-compliance is discovered.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
